11 SECTION 1. AMENDATORY 2 O.S. 2021, Section 5-10, is
12 amended to read as follows:
13 Section 5-10. A. To implement the Made in Oklahoma Program and
14 the Grown in Oklahoma Program, the State Department of Agriculture
15 shall:
16 1. Design a “Made in Oklahoma” logo and a “Grown in Oklahoma”
17 logo for use by Oklahoma processors, providers, or growers;
18 2. Establish standards and requirements for logos;
19 3. Establish fees for logos; provided any Oklahoma processor,
20 provider, or grower may acquire use of the logos on their foods,
21 products, or services free of charge for the first year after
22 authorization for use;
23 4. Establish a task force to assist in ascertaining strengths
24 and weaknesses of the program;
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 10
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 5. Cooperate with other state agencies and other organizations
2 as needed in development of the program;
3 6. Foster conferences, institutes, and exhibits on Oklahoma-
4 made products and services or Oklahoma-grown foods;
5 7. Publish reports, surveys, news bulletins, or other materials
6 pertaining to its findings, recommendations, and work; and
7 8. Report Electronically report, utilizing the centralized
8 filing system provided for in Section 378 of this act, to the
9 Governor, the Speaker of the House of Representatives, and the
10 President Pro Tempore of the Senate areas needing legislative or
11 procedural changes to help promote the purchase and use of Oklahoma-
12 made products and services and Oklahoma-grown foods or to attract
13 manufacturers to supply processors with needed materials.
14 B. The Department may apply for, accept, and use any gift,
15 grant, or bequest from any source for the purpose of discharging its
16 duties.

14 annual report of combative sports activities in Oklahoma with the
15 Governor, the President Pro Tempore of the Senate, and the Speaker
16 of the House of Representatives. Said Such report shall include,
17 but not be limited to, the amount of revenue collected, the number
18 of permits and licenses issued and revoked, the number of violations
19 of the Oklahoma State Athletic Commission Act, and the number of
20 professional combative sports events, amateur mixed martial arts
21 events and amateur kickboxing events conducted.

14 SECTION 14. AMENDATORY 10 O.S. 2021, Section 22.1, is
15 amended to read as follows:
16 Section 22.1. A. The Oklahoma Legislature recognizes that:
17 1. Children who have been abused, who are dependent or
18 neglected, or whose parents, for whatever reason, may be unable or
19 unwilling to provide care for their children, are best served when
20 they can be cared for by grandparents or other suitable relatives
21 instead of placing those children in foster care with the State of
22 Oklahoma; and
23 2. While grandparents or other relatives are often willing to
24 provide for the care of children who can no longer remain with their
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 36
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 pare nts, there may exist financial obstacles to the provision of
2 such care, or there may be a need for other services to enable the
3 children to remain with their grandparents or other relatives in
4 order to prevent the entry of those children into the foster care
5 system.
6 B. It is the intent of the Oklahoma Legislature in enacting
7 this section to:
8 1. Recognize family relationships in which a grandparent or
9 other relative within the third degree of relationship to the child
10 is the head of a household that includes a child otherwise at risk
11 of foster care placement by the Department of Human Services;
12 2. Enhance family preservation and stability by recognizing
13 that most children in placements with grandparents and other
14 relatives within the third degree of relationship to the child do
15 not need intensive supervision of the placement by the courts or by
16 the Department;
17 3. Provide additional placement options and incentives that
18 will achieve permanency and stability for many children who are
19 otherwise at risk of foster care placement by the Department because
20 of abuse, abandonment, or neglect, but who may successfully be able
21 to reside in the care of relatives within the third degree of
22 relationship to the child; and
23 4. Reserve the limited casework and supervisory resources of
24 the Department and the courts expended to care for children in state
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 37
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 cust ody for those cases in which children do not have the option for
2 safe, stable care within their immediate family.
3 C. The Department of Human Services shall establish and operate
4 a relative support program pursuant to eligibility guidelines
5 established in this section and by rules of the Department
6 promulgated thereto which will divert children from the foster care
7 program operated by the Department. The relative support program
8 shall provide assistance to relatives within the third degree of
9 relationship to a child who are caring for the child on a full-time
10 basis, regardless of whether there is a court order granting custody
11 of the child to the relative.
12 D. Grandparents or other such relatives who qualify for and
13 participate in the relative support program are not required to be
14 certified as foster parents or to meet the foster care requirements
15 but shall be capable of providing a physically safe environment and
16 a stable, supportive home for the children under their care.
17 E. Upon request by grandparents or other relatives who are
18 caring for a child on a full-time basis, the Department shall
19 complete a needs assessment on such grandparents or other relatives
20 to determine the appropriate services and support needed by the
21 child and the grandparents or other such relatives.
22 F. Within available funding specified by this section, the
23 relative support program may provide grandparents or other suitable
24 relatives with:
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 38
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 1. Case management services;
2 2. Monthly stipends or other financial assistance, family
3 support and preservation services;
4 3. Flexible funds to enable the grandparents or other relatives
5 to meet unusual or crisis expenditures, including but not limited
6 to, making housing deposits, utility deposits, or to purchase beds,
7 clothing and food;
8 4. Subsidized child care and after school care;
9 5. Respite care;
10 6. Transportation;
11 7. Counseling;
12 8. Support groups;
13 9. Assistance in accessing parental child support payments;
14 10. Aid in accessing food stamps, Social Security and other
15 public benefits;
16 11. Information about legal options for relative caregivers;
17 12. Assistance for establishing a relative guardianship or
18 relative custodianship for the child;
19 13. Available volunteer attorney services;
20 14. Mediation/family group conferencing; and
21 15. Community-based services and state or federal programs
22 available to the child and relatives to support the child’s safety,
23 growth and health development.
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 39
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 G. Children living with grandparents or other relatives within
2 the third degree of relationship to the child who are receiving
3 assistance pursuant to this section shall be eligible for Medicaid
4 coverage.
5 H. Subject to availability of funding, and as may be permitted
6 by federal law or regulations governing the Department of Human
7 Services’ block grant for Temporary Assistance for Needy Families
8 (TANF), the Department is specifically authorized to provide funding
9 assistance from such block grant or other available funds for the
10 development and operation of the relative support program by
11 providing available funds which are not otherwise committed to or
12 necessary for the provision of the Statewide Temporary Assistance
13 Responsibility System. In addition, the Department may use any
14 other state, federal or private funds available to the Department
15 for such purposes to implement the provisions of this section.
16 I. 1. In order to qualify for the receipt of any monthly
17 stipend, the grandparent or other relative shall meet any
18 eligibility criteria determined by the Department of Human Services.
19 2. Within limits of available funding, monthly stipends may be
20 paid to grandparents or other relatives with the third degree of
21 relationship to the child who have physical full-time custody of a
22 child who would be unable to serve in that capacity without a
23 monthly stipend because of inadequate financial resources, thus
24 exposing the child to the trauma of potential placement in a shelter
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 40
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 or i n foster care placement by the Department of Human Services.
2 The statewide average monthly rate for children in the legal custody
3 of grandparents or other relatives who are not certified as foster
4 homes shall not exceed the cost of providing foster care.
5 J. Additional assistance may be made available to qualified
6 grandparents or other relatives within the third degree of
7 relationship and children, based upon specific needs of the
8 grandparent or other relative of the child and the specific needs of
9 the child. Such assistance shall also be subject to available
10 funding.
11 K. The relative support program established by the Department
12 pursuant to this section may receive referrals from district courts
13 of this state, from social service or child advocate agencies, from
14 any other agency of this state, or other states or federal programs.
15 In addition, the relative support program may be accessed directly
16 by the grandparents or other relatives of the affected children by
17 application made to the Department of Human Services.
18 L. The Department of Human Services may provide any services
19 necessary to effectuate the purposes of this section by contract
20 with any person or with any public or private entity.
21 M. The provisions of this section shall also be available to a
22 legal guardian of a child who is within the fifth degree of relation
23 to the child.
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 41
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 N. The Department of Human Services shall, pursuant to the

4 O. As a part of the relative support program, the Department
5 shall develop, publish, and distribute an informational brochure for

8 this section may include, but is not limited to, the following:

18 support services.

20 partnership to serve children, thereby maximizing the opportunities
21 for families to receive services that best fit their needs.

23 following:
24

6 4. Improve parental self-sufficiency, including increased

2 grants. All monies accruing to the credit of the fund are hereby
3 appropriated and may be budgeted and expended by the Administrative
4 Office of the Courts for the purpose of administering the Family
5 Representation and Advocacy Program and for the provision of legal
6 and interdisciplinary services to indigent parents and children by
7 and through the Family Representation and Advocacy Program. By
8 January 31, 2024, and by January 31 of each year thereafter, the
9 Administrative Office of the Courts shall disburse funds from the
10 Family Representation and Advocacy Program Revolving Fund to the
11 contracted eligible organization.
12 B. The Administrative Office of the Courts shall allocate and
13 expend funds from the Family Representation and Advocacy Program
14 Revolving Fund to provide for the necessary operating costs of the
15 Family Representation and Advocacy Program including court-appointed
16 legal and interdisciplinary representation to children, indigent
17 parents, legal guardians, or Indian custodians in proceedings
18 governed by the Oklahoma Children’s Code, to the extent that funds
19 are available from the Family Representation and Advocacy Program
20 Revolving Fund. The Administrative Office of the Courts shall
21 allocate and expend these funds pursuant to the contract with the
22 eligible organization. The Administrative Office of the Courts may
23 charge an administrative fee as provided in subsection D of this
24 section for administering the contract.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 55
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 C. The eligible organization that contracts to operate and
2 manage the Family Representation and Advocacy Program to provide
3 legal and interdisciplinary services shall maintain books and
4 records in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles.
5 The books and records shall account for the receipt and expenditure
6 of all funds paid pursuant to contract. Books and records shall be
7 maintained for a period of five (5) years from the close of the
8 fiscal year of the contract period. The State Auditor and Inspector
9 may audit each organization annually. The necessary expense of each
10 audit including but not limited to the cost of typing, printing, and
11 binding shall be paid from funds of the organization.
12 D. The Administrative Office of the Courts may use up to two
13 and one-half percent (2.5%) of the funds deposited in the Family
14 Representation and Advocacy Program Revolving Fund in any given
15 fiscal year to provide financial support staff, financial data entry
16 staff and facilities, and operating assistance for the Family
17 Representation Program Advisory Board.
18 E. An annual report issued by the Administrative Office of the

1 SECTION 23. AMENDATORY 10 O.S. 2021, Section 1150.2, as
2 last amended by Section 10, Chapter 347, O.S.L. 2024 (10 O.S. Supp.
3 2025, Section 1150.2), is amended to read as follows:
4 Section 1150.2. A. There is hereby re-created until July 1,
5 2026, in accordance with the Oklahoma Sunset Law, the Child Death
6 Review Board within the Oklahoma Commission on Children and Youth.
7 The Board shall have the power and duty to:
8 1. Conduct case reviews of deaths and near deaths of children
9 in this state;
10 2. Develop accurate statistical information and identification
11 of deaths of children due to abuse and neglect;
12 3. Improve the ability to provide protective services to the
13 surviving siblings of a child or children who die of abuse or
14 neglect and who may be living in a dangerous environment;
15 4. Improve policies, procedures and practices within the
16 agencies that serve children including the child protection system;
17 5. Enter into agreements with regional teams established by the
18 Board to carry out such duties and responsibilities as the Board
19 shall designate including assigned cases in the geographical area
20 for that regional team. The Commission, with the advice of the
21 Board, shall promulgate rules necessary for the implementation of
22 the provisions of this paragraph; and
23 6. Enter into agreements with other state, local, or private
24 entities as necessary to carry out the duties of the Board
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 57
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 incl uding, but not limited to, conducting joint reviews with the
2 Domestic Violence Fatality Review Board on domestic violence cases
3 involving child death or child near-death incidents.
4 B. In carrying out its duties and responsibilities the Board
5 shall:
6 1. Establish criteria for cases involving the death or near
7 death of a child subject to specific, in-depth review by the Board.
8 As used in this section, the term “near death” means a child is in
9 serious or critical condition, as certified by a physician, as a
10 result of abuse or neglect;
11 2. Conduct a specific case review of those cases where the
12 cause of death or near death is or may be related to abuse or
13 neglect of a child;
14 3. Establish and maintain statistical information related to
15 the deaths and near deaths of children including, but not limited
16 to, demographic and medical diagnostic information;
17 4. Establish procedures for obtaining initial information
18 regarding near deaths of children from the Department of Human
19 Services and law enforcement agencies;
20 5. Review the policies and procedures of the child protection
21 system and make specific recommendations to the entities comprising
22 the system as to actions necessary for improvement;
23 6. Review the extent to which the state child protection system
24 is coordinated with foster care and adoption programs and evaluate
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 58
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 whet her the state is efficiently discharging its child protection

6 living in a dangerous environment, refer specific cases to the
7 Department of Human Services or the appropriate district attorney
8 for further investigation;
9 8. Request and obtain a copy of all records and reports

9 Confidential information provided to the Board shall be

24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 60
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 but not limited to, clinical summaries from treating physicians,

8 state employee, or any problem within the child protective services
9 system discovered by the Board while performing its duties;
10 12. Recommend, when appropriate, amendment of the cause or
11 manner of death listed on the death certificate; and
12 13. Subject to the approval of the Commission, exercise all
13 incidental powers necessary and proper for the implementation and
14 administration of the Child Death Review Board Act.
15 C. The review and discussion of individual cases of death or
16 near death of a child shall be conducted in executive session and in
17 compliance with the confidentiality requirements of Section 1-6-102
18 of Title 10A of the Oklahoma Statutes. All other business shall be
19 conducted in accordance with the provisions of the Oklahoma Open
20 Meeting Act. All discussions of individual cases and any writings
21 produced by or created for the Board and recommended by the Board,
22 as the result of a review of an individual case of the death or near
23 death of a child, shall be privileged and shall not be admissible in
24 evidence in any proceeding. The Board shall periodically conduct
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 61
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 meet ings to discuss organization and business matters and any
2 actions or recommendations aimed at improvement of the child

5 designated as a business meeting of the Board subject to the
6 Oklahoma Open Meeting Act.
7 D. 1. The Board shall submit an annual statistical report on
8 the incidence and causes of death and near death of children in this
9 state for which the Board has completed its review during the past
10 calendar year, including its recommendations, to the Oklahoma
11 Commission on Children and Youth on or before May 1 of each year.
12 The Board shall also prepare and make available to the public, on an
13 annual basis, a report containing a summary of the activities of the
14 Board relating to the review of deaths and near deaths of children,
15 the extent to which the state child protection system is coordinated
16 with foster care and adoption programs, and an evaluation of whether
17 the state is efficiently discharging its child protection
18 responsibilities. The report shall be completed no later than
19 December 31 of each year.
20 2. The Commission shall review the report of the Board and, as
21 appropriate, incorporate the findings and recommendations into the
22 State Plan for Services to Children and Youth.

14 SECTION 26. AMENDATORY 10A O.S. 2021, Section 1-6-105,
15 is amended to read as follows:
16 Section 1-6-105. A. When used in this section, unless the
17 context otherwise requires:
18 1. “Abuse” means harm or threatened harm or failure to protect
19 from harm or threatened harm to the health, safety, or welfare of a
20 child by a person responsible for the child, including but not
21 limited to nonaccidental physical or mental injury, sexual abuse, or
22 sexual exploitation. Provided, however, that nothing contained in
23 this act shall prohibit any parent from using ordinary force as a
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 68
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 mean s of discipline including, but not limited to, spanking,
2 switching, or paddling;
3 2. “Identifying information” means information that identifies
4 an individual, including the individual’s:
5 a. name, address, date of birth, occupation, place of
6 employment and telephone number,
7 b. employer identification number, mother’s maiden name,
8 Social Security number, or any identification number
9 issued by a governmental entity, or
10 c. unique biometric data, including the fingerprints,
11 voice print, or retina or iris image of the
12 individual;
13 3. “Near death” means a child is in serious or critical
14 condition as verified by a physician, a registered nurse or other
15 licensed health care provider. Verification of medical condition of
16 a child may be given in person or by telephone, mail, electronic
17 mail or facsimile;
18 4. “Neglect” means:
19 a. the failure or omission to provide any of the
20 following:
21 (1) adequate nurturance and affection, food,
22 clothing, shelter, sanitation, hygiene, or
23 appropriate education,
24 (2) medical, dental, or behavioral health care,
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 69
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 (3) supervision or appropriate caretakers, or
2 (4) special care made necessary by the physical or
3 mental condition of the child,
4 b. the failure or omission to protect a child from
5 exposure to any of the following:
6 (1) the use, possession, sale, or manufacture of
7 illegal drugs,
8 (2) illegal activities, or
9 (3) sexual acts or materials that are not age-
10 appropriate, or
11 c. abandonment.
12 Nothing in this paragraph shall be construed to mean a child is
13 abused or neglected for the sole reason the parent, legal guardian
14 or person having custody or control of a child, in good faith,
15 selects and depends upon spiritual means alone through prayer, in
16 accordance with the tenets and practice of a recognized church or
17 religious denomination, for the treatment or cure of disease or
18 remedial care of such child. Nothing contained in this paragraph
19 shall prevent a court from immediately assuming custody of a child,
20 pursuant to the Oklahoma Children’s Code, and ordering whatever
21 action may be necessary, including medical treatment, to protect the
22 child’s health or welfare; and
23 5. “Person responsible for a child” means “person responsible
24 for a child’s health, safety or welfare” as provided in Section 1-1-
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 70
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 105 of this title but shall also include any person who has
2 voluntarily accepted the duty of supervising a child or who has been

5 B. Department of Human Services information shall be maintained
6 by the Department as required by federal law as a condition of the

10 C. If the Department has reasonable cause to suspect that a
11 child death or near death is the result of abuse or neglect, the
12 Department shall, utilizing the centralized filing system provided

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 71

5 child’s death or near death; and
6 5. Whether the child was under the supervision of the child’s
7 parent, guardian, or person responsible for the child at the time of
8 the death or near death of the child.

18 expressing apology, sympathy, commiseration, condolence, compassion,

20 Department of Human Services or an employee of the Department to the
21 public or to the family or foster parents of a child which relate
22 solely to discomfort, pain, suffering, injury, tragedy, near death
23 or death of a child shall be inadmissible as evidence of an
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 78
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 admi ssion of liability or wrongdoing or as evidence of an admission
2 against interest.

17 minimum requirements:
18 1. Resolution of disputes with foster parents shall be
19 accomplished quickly, informally, and at the lowest possible level,
20 but shall provide for access to impartial arbitration by management
21 level personnel within the central office;
22 2. Prompt resolution of grievances no more than sixty (60) days
23 after receipt of the grievance or complaint; and
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 83

15 circumstances then prevailing that a prudent person acting in a like
16 capacity and familiar with such matters would use in the conduct of
17 an enterprise of a like character and with like aims;
18 3. By diversifying the investments of the System so as to
19 minimize the risk of large losses, unless under the circumstances it

21 4. In accordance with the laws, documents and instruments
22 governing the System.

24 members of the State Board from personal loss or accountability from
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 94
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 liab ility resulting from a member’s action or inaction as a member

3 C. The State Board may establish an investment committee. The
4 investment committee shall be composed of not more than five (5)
5 members of the State Board appointed by the chairman of the State
6 Board. The committee shall make recommendations to the full State
7 Board on all matters related to the choice of custodians and
8 managers of the assets of the System, on the establishment of
9 investment and fund management guidelines, and in planning future
10 investment policy. The committee shall have no authority to act on
11 behalf of the State Board in any circumstances whatsoever. No
12 recommendation of the committee shall have effect as an action of
13 the State Board nor take effect without the approval of the State
14 Board as provided by law.
15 D. The Board shall retain qualified investment managers to
16 provide for the investment of the monies of the System. The
17 investment managers shall be chosen by a solicitation of proposals
18 on a competitive bid basis pursuant to standards set by the State
19 Board. Subject to the overall investment guidelines set by the
20 State Board, the investment managers shall have full discretion in
21 the management of those monies of the System allocated to the
22 investment managers. The State Board shall manage those monies not
23 specifically allocated to the investment managers. The monies of
24 the System allocated to the investment managers shall be actively
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 95
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 mana ged by the investment managers, which may include selling
2 investments and realizing losses if such action is considered
3 advantageous to longer term return maximization. Because of the
4 total return objective, no distinction shall be made for management
5 and performance evaluation purposes between realized and unrealized
6 capital gains and losses.
7 E. Funds and revenues for investment by the investment managers
8 or the State Board shall be placed with a custodian selected by the
9 State Board. The custodian shall be a bank or trust company
10 offering pension fund master trustee and master custodial services
11 and any related custodial agreement or trust agreement is
12 incorporated herein by reference. The custodian shall be chosen by
13 a solicitation of proposals on a competitive bid basis pursuant to
14 standards set by the State Board. In compliance with the investment
15 policy guidelines of the State Board, the custodian bank or trust
16 company shall be contractually responsible for ensuring that all
17 monies of the System are invested in income-producing investment
18 vehicles at all times. If a custodian bank or trust company has not
19 received direction from the investment managers of the System as to
20 the investment of the monies of the System in specific investment
21 vehicles, the custodian bank or trust company shall be contractually
22 responsible to the State Board for investing the monies in
23 appropriately collateralized short-term interest-bearing investment
24 vehicles. Any assets of the System may be invested in a collective
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 96
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 inve stment fund or group trust that satisfies the requirements of

5 investment fund or group trust is adopted, with respect to any

12 agreement(s), upon approval by the State Board.

6 Board. The committee shall make recommendations to the full State
7 Board on all matters related to the choice of custodians and
8 managers of the assets of the System, on the establishment of
9 investment and fund management guidelines, and in planning future
10 investment policy. The committee shall have no authority to act on
11 behalf of the State Board in any circumstances whatsoever. No
12 recommendation of the committee shall have effect as an action of
13 the State Board nor take effect without the approval of the State
14 Board as provided by law.
15 D. The State Board shall retain qualified investment managers
16 to provide for the investment of the monies of the System. The
17 investment managers shall be chosen by a solicitation of proposals
18 on a competitive bid basis pursuant to standards set by the State
19 Board unless the State Board deems it necessary and prudent to do
20 otherwise to fulfill its fiduciary responsibility. Subject to the
21 overall investment guidelines set by the State Board, the investment
22 managers shall have full discretion in the management of those
23 monies of the System allocated to the investment managers. The
24 State Board shall manage those monies not specifically allocated to
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 101
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 the investment managers. The monies of the System allocated to the
2 investment managers shall be actively managed by the investment
3 managers, which may include selling investments and realizing losses
4 if such action is considered advantageous to longer term return
5 maximization. Because of the total return objective, no distinction
6 shall be made for management and performance evaluation purposes
7 between realized and unrealized capital gains and losses.
8 E. Funds and revenues for investment by the investment managers
9 or the State Board shall be placed with a custodian selected by the
10 State Board. The custodian shall be a bank or trust company
11 offering pension fund master trustee and master custodial services
12 and any related custodial agreement or trust agreement is
13 incorporated herein by reference. The custodian shall be chosen by
14 a solicitation of proposals on a competitive basis pursuant to
15 standards set by the State Board. In compliance with the investment
16 policy guidelines of the State Board, the custodian bank or trust
17 company shall be contractually responsible for ensuring that all
18 monies of the System are invested in income-producing investment
19 vehicles at all times. If a custodian bank or trust company has not
20 received direction from the investment managers of the System as to
21 the investment of the monies of the System in specific investment
22 vehicles, the custodian bank or trust company shall be contractually
23 responsible to the State Board for investing the monies in
24 appropriately collateralized short-term interest-bearing investment
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 102
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 vehi cles. Any assets of the System may be invested in a collective
2 investment fund or in a group trust that satisfies the requirements
3 of Rev. Rul. 81-100, as further amended by Rev. Rul. 2004-67, Rev.
4 Rul. 2008-40, and Rev. Rul. 2011-1, and as subsequently amended by
5 future guidance. Each such collective investment fund or group
6 trust is adopted, with respect to any monies invested therein, as
7 part of the System, its trust, and custodial account and each such
8 declaration of trust or trust agreement and related adoption,
9 participation, investment management, subtrust, or other agreements,
10 as amended from time to time, with respect to any monies invested
11 therein, are incorporated by reference into the System, its trust
12 agreement(s) or custodial agreement(s), upon approval by the State
13 Board.
14 F. By November 1, 1988, and prior to August 1 of each year
15 thereafter, the State Board shall develop a written investment plan
16 for the System.
17 G. After July 1 and before November 1 of each year, the State
18 Board shall publish widely an annual report presented in simple and
19 easily understood language pursuant to uniform reporting standards
20 prescribed by the Oklahoma State Pension Commission for all state
21 retirement systems. The report shall be electronically submitted to
22 the Governor, the Speaker of the House of Representatives, the
23 President Pro Tempore of the Senate, the Oklahoma State Pension
24 Commission, and the members of the System utilizing the centralized
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 103
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 fili ng system provided for in Section 378 of this act. The annual
2 report shall cover the operation of the System during the past
3 fiscal year, including income, disbursements, and the financial
4 condition of the System at the end of the fiscal year. The annual
5 report shall also contain a summary of the results of the most
6 recent actuarial valuation to include total assets, total
7 liabilities, unfunded liability or over funded status, contributions
8 and any other information deemed relevant by the State Board. The
9 annual report shall be written in such a manner as to permit a
10 readily understandable means for analyzing the financial condition
11 and performances of the System for the fiscal year.
12 H. The State Board shall adopt a cost of living adjustment
13 actuarial assumption in its annual actuarial valuation report.

6 effectuate the provisions of this subsection.
7 C. For purposes of this section, qualifying hydrogen fuel
8 production shall include:
9 1. Hydrogen fuel produced from low carbon sources such as
10 natural gas;
11 2. Hydrogen fuel produced from zero carbon sources such as:
12 a. wind,
13 b. photovoltaic,

15 d. geothermal, and
16 e. biomass, which projects may include agricultural

18 degradable organic wastes, municipal solid waste, and

6 D. Board members shall serve for a term of four (4) years,
7 which shall begin on January 1 of the first year of the appointment
8 and end on December 31 of the fourth year. There shall be no limit
9 to the number of consecutive terms served. If a vacancy should
10 occur during a member’s term, the appointing authority for the
11 vacant position shall appoint a new member to fill the remainder of
12 the unexpired term. Board members shall serve without compensation
13 but may be eligible for necessary travel expenses pursuant to the
14 State Travel Reimbursement Act.
15 E. The Board shall be responsible for establishing procedures

23 achieve the purpose and satisfy the objectives of the Initiative as
24 provided in the Oklahoma Energy Initiative Act. The Board shall
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 123
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 have authority to distribute funding for such activities and
2 programs. The Board may employ staff as it deems necessary.
3 G. The Board, in consultation with the Advisory Council and any
4 other party deemed necessary, shall submit and publish
5 electronically an annual, written report to summarize the annual

13 not apply to any project, activity or contract of the Initiative or
14 the Board.

3 Section 215.28. A. There is hereby created the District

10 the Council. The Executive Coordinator shall serve at the pleasure
11 of the Council. The Executive Coordinator shall be licensed to
12 practice law in Oklahoma and shall have been a district attorney or
13 assistant district attorney or have held an equivalent position in
14 state or federal government for at least three (3) years prior to
15 appointment. The Executive Coordinator may appoint an Assistant
16 Coordinator, both of whom shall be in the unclassified service of
17 the state. The Executive Coordinator and the Assistant Coordinator,
18 who shall also be licensed to practice law in Oklahoma, shall devote

1 court, together with interest upon any pecuniary sum

7 C. When sentencing a person convicted of a crime, the court
8 shall first consider a program of restitution for the victim, as
9 well as imposition of a fine or incarceration of the offender. The
10 provisions of paragraph 1 of subsection A of this section shall not
11 apply to defendants being sentenced upon their third or subsequent
12 to their third conviction of a felony. Provided, the court may
13 waive these prohibitions upon written application of the district
14 attorney. Both the application and the waiver shall be made part of
15 the record of the case.
16 D. When sentencing a person convicted of a crime, the judge
17 shall consider any victim impact statements if submitted to the
18 jury, or the judge in the event a jury is waived.
19 E. Probation, for purposes of subsection A of this section, is
20 a procedure by which a defendant found guilty of a crime, whether
21 upon a verdict or plea of guilty or upon a plea of nolo contendere,
22 is released by the court subject to conditions imposed by the court
23 and subject to supervision by the Department of Corrections, a
24 private supervision provider or other person designated by the
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 168
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 cour t. Such supervision shall be initiated upon an order of
2 probation from the court, and shall not exceed two (2) years, unless
3 a petition alleging a violation of any condition of deferred
4 judgment or seeking revocation of the suspended sentence is filed
5 during the supervision, or as otherwise provided by law. In the
6 case of a person convicted of a sex offense, supervision shall begin
7 immediately upon release from incarceration or if parole is granted
8 and shall not be limited to two (2) years. Provided further, any
9 supervision provided for in this section may be extended for a
10 period not to exceed the expiration of the maximum term or terms of
11 the sentence upon a determination by the court or the Division of
12 Probation and Parole of the Department of Corrections that the best
13 interests of the public and the release will be served by an
14 extended period of supervision.
15 F. The Department of Corrections, or such other agency as the
16 court may designate, shall be responsible for the monitoring and
17 administration of the restitution and service programs provided for
18 by subparagraphs a, c and d of paragraph 1 of subsection A of this
19 section, and shall ensure that restitution payments are forwarded to
20 the victim and that service assignments are properly performed.
21 G. 1. The Department of Corrections is hereby authorized,
22 subject to funds available through appropriation by the Legislature,
23 to contract with counties for the administration of county Community
24 Service Sentencing Programs.

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. Any offender eligible to participate in the Program pursuant
2 to this section shall be eligible to participate in a county
3 Program; provided, participation in county-funded Programs shall not
4 be limited to offenders who would otherwise be sentenced to
5 confinement with the Department of Corrections.
6 3. The Department shall establish criteria and specifications
7 for contracts with counties for such Programs. A county may apply
8 to the Department for a contract for a county-funded Program for a
9 specific period of time. The Department shall be responsible for
10 ensuring that any contracting county complies in full with
11 specifications and requirements of the contract. The contract shall
12 set appropriate compensation to the county for services to the
13 Department.
14 4. The Department is hereby authorized to provide technical
15 assistance to any county in establishing a Program, regardless of
16 whether the county enters into a contract pursuant to this
17 subsection. Technical assistance shall include appropriate
18 staffing, development of community resources, sponsorship,
19 supervision, and any other requirements.
20 5. The Department shall annually, utilizing the centralized
21 filing system provided for in Section 378 of this act,
22 electronically make a report to the Governor, the President Pro
23 Tempore of the Senate, and the Speaker of the House on the number of
24 such Programs, the number of participating offenders, the success
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 170
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 rate s of each Program according to criteria established by the
2 Department and the costs of each Program.
3 H. As used in this section:
4 1. “Ignition interlock device” means a device that, without
5 tampering or intervention by another person, would prevent the
6 defendant from operating a motor vehicle if the defendant has a
7 blood or breath alcohol concentration of two-hundredths (0.02) or
8 greater;
9 2. “Electronically monitored home detention” means
10 incarceration of the defendant within a specified location or
11 locations with monitoring by means of a device approved by the
12 Department of Corrections that detects if the person leaves the
13 confines of any specified location; and
14 3. “Victims impact panel program” means a program conducted by
15 a corporation registered with the Secretary of State in Oklahoma for
16 the sole purpose of operating a victims impact panel program. The
17 program shall include live presentations from presenters who will
18 share personal stories with participants about how alcohol, drug
19 abuse, the operation of a motor vehicle while using an electronic
20 communication device or the illegal conduct of others has personally
21 impacted the lives of the presenters. A victims impact panel
22 program shall be attended by persons who have committed the offense
23 of driving, operating or being in actual physical control of a motor
24 vehicle while under the influence of alcohol or other intoxicating
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 171
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 subs tance, operating a motor vehicle while the ability of the person
2 to operate such vehicle was impaired due to the consumption of
3 alcohol or any other substance or operating a motor vehicle while
4 using an electronic device or by persons who have been convicted of
5 furnishing alcoholic beverage to persons under twenty-one (21) years
6 of age, as provided in Sections 6-101 and 6-120 of Title 37A of the
7 Oklahoma Statutes. Persons attending a victims impact panel program
8 shall be required to pay a fee of Seventy-five Dollars ($75.00) to
9 the provider of the program. A certificate of completion shall be
10 issued to the person upon satisfying the attendance and fee
11 requirements of the victims impact panel program. The certificate
12 of completion shall contain the business identification number of
13 the program provider. A certified assessment agency, certified
14 assessor or provider of an alcohol and drug substance abuse course
15 shall be prohibited from providing a victims impact panel program
16 and shall further be prohibited from having any proprietary or
17 pecuniary interest in a victims impact panel program. The provider
18 of the victims impact panel program shall carry general liability
19 insurance and maintain an accurate accounting of all business
20 transactions and funds received in relation to the victims impact
21 panel program. Beginning October 1, 2020, and each October 1
22 thereafter, the provider of the victims impact panel program shall
23 provide to the District Attorneys Council the following:
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 172
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 a. proof of registration with the Oklahoma Secretary of
2 State,
3 b. proof of general liability insurance,
4 c. end-of-year financial statements prepared by a
5 certified public accountant,
6 d. a copy of federal income tax returns filed with the
7 Internal Revenue Service,
8 e. a registration fee of One Thousand Dollars
9 ($1,000.00). The registration fee shall be deposited

16 I. A person convicted of a felony offense or receiving any form
17 of probation for an offense in which registration is required
18 pursuant to the Sex Offenders Registration Act, shall submit to
19 deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) testing for law enforcement
20 identification purposes in accordance with Section 150.27 of Title
21 74 of the Oklahoma Statutes and the rules promulgated by the
22 Oklahoma State Bureau of Investigation for the OSBI Combined DNA
23 Index System (CODIS) Database. Subject to the availability of
24 funds, any person convicted of a misdemeanor offense of assault and
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 173
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 batt ery, domestic abuse, stalking, possession of a controlled
2 substance prohibited under the Uniform Controlled Dangerous
3 Substances Act, outraging public decency, resisting arrest, escape
4 or attempting to escape, eluding a police officer, Peeping Tom,
5 pointing a firearm, threatening an act of violence, breaking and
6 entering a dwelling place, destruction of property, negligent
7 homicide or causing a personal injury accident while driving under
8 the influence of any intoxicating substance, or any alien unlawfully
9 present under federal immigration law, upon arrest, shall submit to
10 DNA testing for law enforcement identification purposes in
11 accordance with Section 150.27 of Title 74 of the Oklahoma Statutes
12 and the rules promulgated by the Oklahoma State Bureau of
13 Investigation for the OSBI Combined DNA Index System (CODIS)
14 Database. Any defendant sentenced to probation shall be required to
15 submit to testing within thirty (30) days of sentencing either to
16 the Department of Corrections or to the county sheriff or other
17 peace officer as directed by the court. Defendants who are
18 sentenced to a term of incarceration shall submit to testing in
19 accordance with Section 530.1 of Title 57 of the Oklahoma Statutes,
20 for those defendants who enter the custody of the Department of
21 Corrections or to the county sheriff, for those defendants sentenced
22 to incarceration in a county jail. Convicted individuals who have
23 previously submitted to DNA testing under this section and for whom
24 a valid sample is on file in the OSBI Combined DNA Index System
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 174
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 (COD IS) Database at the time of sentencing shall not be required to
2 submit to additional testing. Except as required by the Sex
3 Offenders Registration Act, a deferred judgment does not require
4 submission to DNA testing.
5 Any person who is incarcerated in the custody of the Department
6 of Corrections after July 1, 1996, and who has not been released
7 before January 1, 2006, shall provide a blood or saliva sample prior
8 to release. Every person subject to DNA testing after January 1,
9 2006, whose sentence does not include a term of confinement with the
10 Department of Corrections shall submit a blood or saliva sample.
11 Every person subject to DNA testing who is sentenced to unsupervised
12 probation or otherwise not supervised by the Department of
13 Corrections shall submit for blood or saliva testing to the sheriff
14 of the sentencing county.
15 J. Samples of blood or saliva for DNA testing required by
16 subsection I of this section shall be taken by employees or
17 contractors of the Department of Corrections, peace officers, or the
18 county sheriff or employees or contractors of the sheriff’s office.
19 The individuals shall be properly trained to collect blood or saliva
20 samples. Persons collecting blood or saliva for DNA testing
21 pursuant to this section shall be immune from civil liabilities
22 arising from this activity. All collectors of DNA samples shall
23 ensure the collection of samples are mailed to the Oklahoma State
24 Bureau of Investigation within ten (10) days of the time the subject
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 175
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 appe ars for testing or within ten (10) days of the date the subject
2 comes into physical custody to serve a term of incarceration. All
3 collectors of DNA samples shall use sample kits provided by the OSBI
4 and procedures promulgated by the OSBI. Persons subject to DNA
5 testing who are not received at the Lexington Assessment and
6 Reception Center shall be required to pay a fee of Fifteen Dollars
7 ($15.00) to the agency collecting the sample for submission to the
8 OSBI Combined DNA Index System (CODIS) Database. Any fees collected
9 pursuant to this subsection shall be deposited in the revolving
10 account or the service fee account of the collection agency or
11 department.

17 SECTION 58. AMENDATORY 22 O.S. 2021, Section 991f-1.1,
18 is amended to read as follows:
19 Section 991f-1.1. A. Each district attorney shall create
20 within the district attorney’s office a Restitution and Diversion
21 Program and assign sufficient staff and resources for the efficient
22 operation of such program. The purpose of the Restitution and
23 Diversion Program is to allow the district attorney the discretion
24 to divert criminal complaints involving property crimes from
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 176
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 crim inal court and to monitor restitution payments. At the
2 discretion of the district attorney, the program may be administered
3 by the Bogus Check Restitution Program operated by the county.
4 B. 1. Referral of a criminal complaint to the Restitution and
5 Diversion Program shall be at the discretion of the district
6 attorney. This act shall not limit the power of the district
7 attorney to prosecute criminal complaints.
8 2. Upon receipt of a criminal complaint involving property, the
9 district attorney shall determine if the complaint is one which is
10 appropriate for deferred prosecution.
11 3. In determining whether to defer prosecution and refer a case
12 to the Restitution and Diversion Program, the district attorney
13 shall consider the following factors:
14 a. whether the criminal complaint alleges an offense
15 involving property,
16 b. whether the alleged offense was committed in an
17 aggressive, violent, premeditated or willful manner,
18 c. the prospects for adequate protection of the public if
19 the accused person is processed through deferred
20 prosecution in the Restitution and Diversion Program,
21 d. the number of criminal complaints against the
22 defendant previously received by the district
23 attorney,
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 177
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 e. whether or not there are other criminal complaints
2 currently pending against the defendant,
3 f. the strength of the evidence of the particular
4 criminal complaint, and
5 g. the wishes of the victim.
6 C. Upon referral of a complaint to the Restitution and
7 Diversion Program, a notice of the complaint shall be forwarded by
8 mail to the accused person. The notice shall contain:
9 1. The date the act which is the subject of the complaint
10 occurred;
11 2. The name of the victim;
12 3. The date before which the accused person must shall contact
13 the office of the district attorney concerning the complaint; and
14 4. A statement of the penalty for the crime which is the
15 subject of the complaint.
16 D. The district attorney may enter into a written agreement
17 with the accused person to defer prosecution on the criminal
18 complaint for a period to be determined by the district attorney,
19 not to exceed three (3) years pending restitution being made to the
20 victim of the complaint and payment of necessary fees.
21 E. Each restitution agreement shall include a provision
22 requiring the accused person to pay to the district attorneys office
23 a fee equal to the amount which would have been assessed as court
24 costs upon the filing of the case in district court plus Twenty-five
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 178
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Doll ars ($25.00) for each criminal complaint covered by the
2 agreement. This fee may be deposited in a special fund with the
3 county treasurer to be known as the “Restitution and Diversion
4 Program Fund” or in the Bogus Check Restitution Fund. The monies
5 deposited in the Restitution and Diversion Program Fund shall be
6 used by the district attorney to make any lawful expenditure
7 associated with the district attorney’s office. The district
8 attorney shall keep records of all monies deposited to and disbursed
9 from these funds. The records of these funds shall be audited at
10 the same time the records of county funds are audited.
11 F. 1. Restitution to be paid by the accused person to the
12 victim shall include out-of-pocket expenses the victim incurred as a
13 direct result of the crime having been committed. A restitution
14 agreement may include provisions for restitution in an amount up to
15 treble the amount of property involved except such restitution shall
16 not apply to false or bogus checks. If, instead of paying
17 restitution directly to the victim, the accused person delivers
18 restitution funds to the office of the district attorney, the
19 district attorney shall deposit such funds in a depository account
20 in the office of the county treasurer to be disbursed to the victim
21 by a warrant signed by the district attorney or a member of the
22 district attorney’s staff assigned to the Restitution and Diversion
23 Program. The district attorney shall keep full records of all
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 179
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 rest itution monies received and disbursed. These records shall be
2 audited at the same time the county funds are audited;
3 2. If the accused person fails to comply with the provisions of
4 the Restitution and Diversion Program agreement, the district
5 attorney may file an information and proceed with the prosecution of
6 the accused person as provided by law.
7 G. Members of the district attorney’s staff shall perform
8 duties in connection with the Restitution and Diversion Program in

11 H. 1. District attorneys shall prepare and submit an annual

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 180
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 the total amount of the restitution collected, the total amount of
2 fees collected, the total cost of the program, and such other
3 information as required by the District Attorneys Council.
4 I. For the purposes of the Restitution and Diversion Program,
5 the following definitions shall apply:
6 1. “Property Crime” shall include, but not be limited to the
7 following:
8 a. embezzlement offenses,
9 b. larceny offenses,
10 c. theft offenses,
11 d. malicious injury to property, and
12 e. any offense which results in economic loss, but does
13 not result in physical injury to another human being,
14 and which is not enumerated in Section 571 of Title 57
15 of the Oklahoma Statutes;
16 2. “Victim” is defined by Section 991f of this title;
17 3. “Restitution” is defined by Section 991f of this title; and
18 4. “Economic loss” is defined by Section 991f of this title.
19 J. The victim shall promptly provide to the Restitution and
20 Diversion Program all documentation and evidence of compensation or
21 reimbursement from insurance companies or agencies of this state,
22 any other state, or the federal government received as a direct
23 result of the crime for injury, loss of earnings or out-of-pocket
24 loss.

1 SECTION 59. AMENDATORY 22 O.S. 2021, Section 1355.4, is

3 Section 1355.4. A. The chief executive officer of the Oklahoma
4 Indigent Defense System shall be the Executive Director, who shall

8 licensed to practice law in this state or is eligible to become so
9 licensed within one (1) year of the appointment. The Executive
10 Director shall have experience in the representation of persons
11 accused or convicted of crimes.

13 functions which serve the Board.

1 SECTION 60. AMENDATORY 22 O.S. 2021, Section 1601, as

4 Section 1601. A. There is hereby created the Domestic Violence
5 Fatality Review Board within the Office of the Attorney General.
6 The Board shall have the power and duty to:
7 1. Coordinate and integrate state and local efforts to address
8 fatal domestic violence and create a body of information to prevent
9 domestic violence deaths;
10 2. Collect, analyze, and interpret state and local data on
11 domestic violence deaths;
12 3. Develop a state and local database on domestic violence
13 deaths;
14 4. Improve the ability to provide protective services to
15 victims of domestic violence who may be living in a dangerous
16 environment;
17 5. Improve policies, procedures and practices within the
18 agencies that serve victims of domestic violence; and
19 6. Enter into agreements with other state, local or private
20 entities as necessary to carry out the duties of the Domestic
21 Violence Fatality Review Board including, but not limited to,
22 conducting joint reviews with the Child Death Review Board on
23 domestic violence cases involving child death or child near-death
24 incidents.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 187
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 B. In carrying out its duties and responsibilities, the Board
2 shall:
3 1. Promulgate rules establishing criteria for identifying cases
4 involving a domestic violence death subject to specific, in-depth
5 review by the Board;
6 2. Conduct a specific case review of those cases where the
7 cause of death is or may be related to domestic violence;
8 3. Establish and maintain statistical information related to
9 domestic violence deaths, including, but not limited to, demographic
10 and medical diagnostic information;
11 4. Establish procedures for obtaining initial information
12 regarding domestic violence deaths from law enforcement agencies;
13 5. Review the policies, practices, and procedures of the
14 domestic violence protection and prevention system and make specific
15 recommendations to the entities comprising the domestic violence
16 prevention and protection system for actions necessary for the
17 improvement of the system;
18 6. Review the extent to which the state domestic violence
19 prevention and protection system is coordinated with law enforcement
20 and the court system and evaluate whether the state is efficiently
21 discharging its domestic violence prevention and protection
22 responsibilities;
23 7. Request and obtain a copy of all records and reports
24 pertaining to a domestic violence death case of the victim,
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 188
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 perp etrator, or any other person cohabitating in the domicile at the

5 b. hospital records,
6 c. school records,

1 prev ention and protection system. The Board shall also prepare and

5 domestic violence prevention and protection system is coordinated
6 and an evaluation of whether the state is efficiently discharging
7 its domestic violence prevention and protection responsibilities.
8 The report shall be completed no later than February 1 of the

23 of any oral statements made by the accused or made by
24 a codefendant,
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 192
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 d. any reports or statements made by experts in
2 connection with the particular case, including results
3 of physical or mental examinations and of scientific
4 tests, experiments, or comparisons,
5 e. any books, papers, documents, photographs, tangible
6 objects, buildings, or places which the prosecuting
7 attorney intends to use in the hearing or trial or
8 which were obtained from or belong to the accused,
9 f. any record of prior criminal convictions of the
10 defendant, or of any codefendant, and
11 g. Oklahoma State Bureau of Investigation (OSBI) rap
12 sheet/records check on any witness listed by the state
13 or the defense as a witness who will testify at trial,
14 as well as any convictions of any witness revealed
15 through additional record checks if the defense has
16 furnished Social Security numbers or date of birth for
17 their witnesses, except OSBI rap sheet/record checks
18 shall not provide date of birth, Social Security
19 number, home phone number, or address.
20 2. The state shall provide the defendant any evidence favorable
21 to the defendant if such evidence is material to either guilt or
22 punishment.
23 3. The prosecuting attorney’s obligations under this standard
24 extend to:
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 193
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 a. material and information in the possession or control
2 of members of the prosecutor’s staff,
3 b. any information in the possession of law enforcement
4 agencies that regularly report to the prosecutor of
5 which the prosecutor should reasonably know, and
6 c. any information in the possession of law enforcement
7 agencies who have reported to the prosecutor with
8 reference to the particular case of which the
9 prosecutor should reasonably know.
10 4. a. If the state intends to introduce testimony of a
11 jailhouse informant, the state shall disclose at least
12 ten (10) days prior to trial:
13 (1) the complete criminal history of such informant,
14 including any dismissed charges,
15 (2) any deal, promise, inducement, or benefit that
16 the state or law enforcement agency has made or
17 may make in the future to the jailhouse informant
18 in connection with the testimony of such
19 informant,
20 (3) the specific statements or recordings made by the
21 suspect or defendant and the time, place, and
22 manner of the disclosure to the jailhouse
23 informant,
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 194
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 (4) all other filed cases in which the state intended
2 to introduce the testimony of the jailhouse
3 informant in connection with a deal, promise,
4 inducement or benefit, the nature of the deal,
5 promise, inducement or benefit, and whether the
6 testimony was admitted in the case,
7 (5) whether at any time the jailhouse informant
8 recanted the testimony or statement, and if so, a
9 transcript or copy of such recantation, if any,
10 and
11 (6) any other information relevant to the credibility
12 of the informant.
13 b. Each district attorney’s office shall maintain a
14 central record that tracks each case in which the
15 state intended to introduce the testimony of the
16 jailhouse informant against a suspect or defendant in
17 connection with a deal, promise, inducement or
18 benefit, the nature of the deal, promise, inducement,
19 or benefit and whether such testimony or statements
20 were admitted in the case. Such record shall be sent
21 to the District Attorneys Council which shall maintain
22 a statewide record of such information. Records
23 maintained pursuant to this paragraph shall only be
24 accessible to prosecutors and shall not be subject to
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 195
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 the Oklahoma Open Records Act. By September 15 of
2 each year, the District Attorneys Council shall
3 publish an annual report of aggregate, de-identified
4 data regarding the total number of cases tracked
5 pursuant to this section, and the number of cases
6 added during the previous fiscal year pursuant to this
7 section by each district attorney’s office. A copy of
8 the report shall be electronically distributed to the
9 Governor, the President Pro Tempore of the Senate, the
10 Speaker of the House of Representatives, and the
11 chairs of the Senate and House Judiciary Committees
12 utilizing the centralized filing system provided for
13 in Section 378 of this act.
14 c. For purposes of this paragraph, “jailhouse informant”
15 means a person who provides, or who the prosecutor
16 intends to provide, testimony about admissions or
17 other relevant information made to him or her by the
18 suspect or defendant while both persons were detained
19 or incarcerated in a penal institution.
20 B. Disclosure of Evidence by the Defendant.
21 1. Upon request of the state, the defense shall be required to
22 disclose the following:
23 a. the names and addresses of witnesses which the defense
24 intends to call at trial, together with their
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 196
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 relevant, written, or recorded statement, if any, or
2 if none, significant summaries of any oral statement,
3 b. the name and address of any witness, other than the
4 defendant, who will be called to show that the
5 defendant was not present at the time and place
6 specified in the information or indictment, together
7 with the witness’ statement to that fact, and
8 c. the names and addresses of any witness the defendant
9 will call, other than himself, for testimony relating
10 to any mental disease, mental defect, or other
11 condition bearing upon his mental state at the time
12 the offense was allegedly committed, together with the
13 witness’ statement of that fact, if the statement is
14 redacted by the court to preclude disclosure of
15 privileged communication.
16 2. A statement filed under subparagraph a, b, or c of paragraph
17 1 of subsection A or B of this section is not admissible in evidence
18 at trial. Information obtained as a result of a statement filed
19 under subsection A or B of this section is not admissible in
20 evidence at trial except to refute the testimony of a witness whose
21 identity subsection A of this section requires to be disclosed.
22 3. Upon the prosecuting attorney’s request after the time set
23 by the court, the defendant shall allow him access at any reasonable
24 times and in any reasonable manner to inspect, photograph, copy, or
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 197
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 have reasonable tests made upon any book, paper, document,
2 photograph, or tangible object which is within the defendant’s
3 possession or control and which:
4 a. the defendant intends to offer in evidence, except to
5 the extent that it contains any communication of the
6 defendant, or
7 b. is a report or statement as to a physical or mental
8 examination or scientific test or experiment made in
9 connection with the particular case prepared by and
10 relating to the anticipated testimony of a person whom
11 the defendant intends to call as a witness, provided
12 the report or statement is redacted by the court to
13 preclude disclosure of privileged communication.
14 C. Continuing Duty to Disclose.
15 If, prior to or during trial, a party discovers additional
16 evidence or material previously requested or ordered, which is
17 subject to discovery or inspection under the Oklahoma Criminal
18 Discovery Code, such party shall promptly notify the other party,
19 the attorney of the other party, or the court of the existence of
20 the additional evidence or material.
21 D. Time of Discovery.
22 1. Motions for discovery may be made at the time of the
23 district court arraignment or thereafter; provided that requests for
24 police reports may be made subject to the provisions of Section 258
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 198
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 of t his title. However, a request pursuant to Section 258 of this
2 title shall be subject to the discretion of the district attorney.
3 All issues relating to discovery, except as otherwise provided, will
4 be completed at least ten (10) days prior to trial. The court may
5 specify the time, place, and manner of making the discovery and may
6 prescribe such terms and conditions as are just.
7 2. Within thirty (30) days of the filing of an endorsed
8 complaint, indictment, or information in a court of record, law
9 enforcement shall provide to the prosecuting agency the following
10 records, if such records exist:
11 a. body camera videos at the time of arrest,
12 b. vehicle-mounted camera videos at the time of arrest,
13 and
14 c. a recording of the administration of a sobriety test.
15 3. Within ninety (90) days of the filing of an endorsed
16 complaint, indictment, or information, the prosecuting agency shall
17 make available the records described in paragraph 2 of this
18 subsection to the defendant or his or her representative.
19 4. Failure of a law enforcement agency to comply with the
20 provisions of paragraph 2 of this subsection may be punished by
21 contempt.
22 5. The following information may be redacted by the prosecuting
23 agency from the records provided to a defendant or his or her
24 representative as required by the provisions of paragraph 3 of this
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 199
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 subs ection; provided that, notice of such redactions are given to
2 the defendant or to his or her representative:
3 a. information that would violate any requirement to keep
4 certain juvenile records confidential as provided for
5 in Title 10A of the Oklahoma Statutes,
6 b. information that would materially compromise an
7 ongoing criminal investigation or ongoing criminal
8 prosecution other than the case involving the
9 defendant,
10 c. information that would undermine the assertion of a
11 privilege to keep the identity of an informant
12 confidential as provided for in Section 2510 of Title
13 12 of the Oklahoma Statutes,
14 d. information that would identify any person who
15 provides information to law enforcement or the
16 information provided by that person when that person
17 requests anonymity or where disclosure of the identity
18 of the person or the information provided could
19 reasonably be expected to threaten or endanger the
20 physical safety or property of the person or the
21 physical safety or property of others, or
22 e. require production of records or videos that fall
23 outside the scope permitted under the provisions of
24 paragraph 2 of this subsection.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 200
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 E. Regulation of Discovery.
2 1. Protective and Modifying Orders. Upon motion of the state
3 or defendant, the court may at any time order that specified
4 disclosures be restricted, or make any other protective order. If
5 the court enters an order restricting specified disclosures, the
6 entire text of the material restricted shall be sealed and preserved
7 in the records of the court to be made available to the appellate
8 court in the event of an appeal.
9 2. Failure to Comply with a Request. If at any time during the
10 course of the proceedings it is brought to the attention of the
11 court that a party has failed to comply with this rule, the court
12 may order such party to permit the discovery or inspection, grant
13 continuance, or prohibit the party from introducing evidence not
14 disclosed, or it may enter such other order as it deems just under
15 the circumstances.
16 3. The discovery order shall not include discovery of legal
17 work product of either attorney which is deemed to include legal
18 research or those portions of records, correspondence, reports, or
19 memoranda which are only the opinions, theories, or conclusions of
20 the attorney or the attorney’s legal staff.
21 F. Reasonable cost of copying, duplicating, videotaping,
22 developing or any other cost associated with this Code for items
23 requested shall be paid by the party so requesting; however, any
24 item which was obtained from the defendant by the state of which
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 201
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 copi es are requested by the defendant shall be paid by the state.
2 Provided, if the court determines the defendant is indigent and
3 without funds to pay the cost of reproduction of the required items,
4 the cost shall be paid by the Indigent Defender System, unless
5 otherwise provided by law.

11 Section 2-3-109. A. There is hereby created the “Oklahoma
12 Energy Efficiency and Emission Reduction Program”, to be
13 administered by the Department of Environmental Quality. The
14 purpose of the Oklahoma Energy Efficiency and Emission Reduction
15 Program is to fund activities and projects designed to reduce
16 regional air pollution.
17 B. The Oklahoma Legislature finds that any activity or project
18 that reduces regional air pollution is desirable and advantageous
19 and serves a compelling public interest. Further, improved air
20 quality enhances the health and quality of life for the citizens of
21 Oklahoma, helps maintain the abundant natural beauty and resources
22 of the state, and fosters the economic well-being of the state by
23 reducing the potential that the federal government will designate
24 some or all of the state as in air-quality “nonattainment” status,
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 217
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 resu lting in extremely burdensome additional regulatory
2 requirements.
3 C. 1. Any funds made available for the Oklahoma Energy
4 Efficiency and Emission Reduction Program shall be used by the
5 Department for matching grants to governmental and nongovernmental
6 entities in Oklahoma to encourage the implementation of recognized
7 air pollution reduction measures, including, but not limited to, the
8 retrofitting of truck and bus fleets or locomotives to use cleaner
9 fuels and the installation and implementation of energy efficiency
10 measures.
11 2. Grants awarded under the Program shall be limited to ninety-
12 five percent (95%) of the direct project costs in the case of
13 governmental entities and seventy-five percent (75%) of the direct
14 project costs in the case of nongovernmental entities.
15 3. In making grant awards, the Department shall enter into a
16 contract or memorandum of agreement with the grantee that includes
17 conditions and safeguards to ensure that the matching funds are
18 expended for the purposes specified and that the state receives a
19 clear benefit from the expenditure. In addition to any other
20 conditions and safeguards deemed necessary and appropriate:
21 a. the Department shall require grant recipients to
22 submit a report within a reasonable time after
23 construction, installation, or implementation of the
24 project that summarizes the results, including
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 218
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 emissions reductions achieved and “lessons learned”.
2 Information from the reports may be used by the
3 Department in evaluation of future grant applications
4 or proposals for the Oklahoma Energy Efficiency and
5 Emission Reduction Program or any similar grant
6 program and to determine the viability of other
7 projects or programs that may be proposed to control
8 or reduce air pollution in the state, and
9 b. to secure the maximum possible benefit by increasing
10 awareness of the Oklahoma Energy Efficiency and
11 Emission Reduction Program, the Department may require
12 any grant recipient to post notice in a conspicuous
13 place of participation in the Program and the nature
14 of the funded project.
15 4. Before making any grants, the Department shall determine to
16 its satisfaction that the proposed project will significantly reduce
17 air pollution within the state. The Department is authorized to set
18 a deadline for grant applications, and if the total grant funding
19 sought exceeds the amount available under the Program, the
20 Department shall give priority to those projects that appear to
21 achieve the maximum public health benefit for citizens of the state.
22 5. Not more than twenty-five percent (25%) of the total sum
23 available for grants under the Program shall be awarded to any
24 single entity. Application of this limit shall not preclude
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 219
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 part icipation by the recipient in any similar grant program in the
2 future.
3 D. If funds are appropriated by the Legislature for the
4 Oklahoma Energy Efficiency and Emission Reduction Program, not more
5 than One Hundred Thousand Dollars ($100,000.00) annually of the
6 funds shall be used by the Department for personnel and other costs
7 associated with administration and management of the Program, and
8 for providing technical assistance to entities applying for and
9 participating in the Program.
10 E. On or before September 1, 2009, and by September 1 each year
11 thereafter, the Department shall, utilizing the centralized filing
12 system provided for in Section 378 of this act, electronically
13 submit to the Governor, the Speaker of the House of Representatives,
14 and the President Pro Tempore of the Senate a report on the Oklahoma
15 Energy Efficiency and Emission Reduction Program. The report shall
16 outline program expenditures, estimate emission reductions achieved,
17 and health or environmental benefits associated with those
18 reductions for the previous fiscal year, and any other information
19 the Department determines is necessary to aid the Governor and
20 Legislature in evaluating the Program.

1 of s ludge which contains heavy metal concentrations significantly

5 2. The owner or operator shall upon receipt weigh all waste
6 received and record the weight in writing. If scales at a disposal
7 site or incinerator are not operative, tonnage shall be estimated on
8 a volume basis whereby the volume reported shall be no less than the
9 volume capacity of the containers or, if none, of the vehicles
