SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 239
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 abou t collection events, collection sites, and community recycling
2 programs. Inclusion on such web site is not a determination by the
3 state that the manufacturer’s recovery plan or practices are in
4 compliance with this act or other laws.
5 B. The Department shall maintain and make available:
6 1. A list of registered manufacturers who have adopted and
7 implemented a recovery plan, as required by this act; and
8 2. A separate list of manufacturers whose registered recovery
9 plan permits consumers to return for collection and recovery other
10 manufacturers’ brands of covered devices, including orphan devices.
11 Manufacturers shall be included on this list of beyond-brand
12 collection plans if such plan:
13 a. provides recycling grants or collection events for
14 covered devices other than that manufacturer’s covered
15 devices,
16 b. requires a consumer who purchases a new covered
17 electronic device from the manufacturer to return
18 another manufacturer’s branded covered device, in
19 which case the manufacturer may require the consumer
20 to pay for transportation or shipping, or
21 c. provides for use of other collection or recovery
22 methods that are approved by the Department.
23 C. The Department shall file each recovery plan and annual
24 report submitted by a manufacturer. The Department shall make
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 240
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 reco very plans and annual reports available to the public pursuant
2 to the Oklahoma Open Records Act.
3 D. The Department shall produce a schedule of collection
4 events, based on the manufacturers’ submitted recovery plans.
5 E. The Department shall by July 1 of each year produce and,
6 utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in Section 378
7 of this act, electronically submit to the Governor, the President
8 Pro Tempore of the Senate, and Speaker of the House of
9 Representatives a summary of the recovery program annual reports
10 filed by the manufacturers.
11 F. The Department may conduct audits and inspections to
12 determine compliance with the provisions of this act and take
13 enforcement action against any manufacturer, retailer, or recoverer
14 for failure to comply with any provisions of this act.
15 G. In addition to any other remedies provided by law, the
16 Department may assess a penalty of up to One Thousand Dollars
17 ($1,000.00) for the first violation, and up to Five Thousand Dollars
18 ($5,000.00) for the second and each subsequent violation, against
19 any manufacturer who fails to label its covered devices or to adopt
20 and implement a recovery plan as required by this act.
21 H. The Environmental Quality Board may promulgate rules
22 necessary to implement the provisions of this act, including the
23 adoption of fees pursuant to the provisions of Section 2-3-402 of
24 Title 27A of the Oklahoma Statutes as necessary to cover the costs

1 of a dministering the program. The Board may adopt by reference

17 amended to read as follows:
18 Section 924.2. A. Any rate, schedule of rates or rating plan
19 for workers’ compensation insurance submitted to or filed with the
20 Insurance Commissioner, or fixed by the Board of Directors of
21 CompSource Mutual Insurance Company, and premiums, by whatever name,
22 for workers’ compensation for self-insureds except for group self-
23 insured associations shall provide for an appropriate reduction in
24 premium charges, by whatever name, for those eligible insured
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 270
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 empl oyers who have successfully participated in the occupational
2 safety and health consultation, education and training program
3 administered by the Commissioner of the Department of Labor pursuant
4 to Section 414 of Title 40 of the Oklahoma Statutes.
5 B. All insurance companies writing workers’ compensation
6 insurance in this state and all self-insureds providing workers’
7 compensation insurance except for group self-insured associations,
8 shall allow an appropriate reduction in premium charges to all
9 eligible employers who qualify for the reduction pursuant to the
10 provisions of this section.
11 C. Eligible employers shall be those employers:
12 1. Who are insured by an insurance company writing workers’
13 compensation insurance in this state; or
14 2. Who are self-insured.
15 D. In order to qualify for the reduction in workers’
16 compensation insurance premium, an employer shall successfully
17 participate annually in the occupational safety and health
18 consultation, education and training program administered by the
19 Department of Labor. Successful participation shall be defined as:
20 1. Undergoing a safety and health hazard survey of the
21 workplace, including an evaluation of the employer’s safety and
22 health program and onsite interviews with employees by the
23 Department’s consultant;
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 271
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. Correcting all hazards identified during the onsite visit
2 within a reasonable period of time as established by the Department;
3 3. Establishing an effective workplace safety and health
4 program and implementing program provisions within a reasonable
5 period of time as established by the Department. The program shall
6 include:
7 a. demonstration of management commitment to worker
8 safety and health,
9 b. procedures for identifying and controlling workplace
10 hazards,
11 c. development and communication of safety plans, rules
12 and work procedures, and
13 d. training for supervisors and employees in safe and
14 healthful work practices;
15 4. Reducing by one-third (1/3) or more the extent to which the
16 lost workday case rate, as measured by the Department of Labor, was
17 above the national average for the industry at the time the employer
18 elected to participate in the occupational safety and health
19 consultation, education and training program, or maintaining a rate
20 at or below the national average for the industry; and
21 5. Documenting a reduction in workers’ compensation claims for
22 the preceding year by showing one of the following:
23 a. a ten percent (10%) reduction in the dollar amount of
24 claims,
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 272
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 b. a ten percent (10%) reduction in the severity of
2 claims, or
3 c. no reported claims,
4 as a result of attending the occupational safety and health
5 consultation, education and training program administered by the
6 Department of Labor.
7 E. 1. Upon successful participation in the occupational safety
8 and health consultation, education and training program as defined
9 in subsection D of this section, an employer shall be issued a
10 certificate by the Commissioner of the Department of Labor which
11 shall be the basis of qualification for the reduction in workers’
12 compensation insurance premium, by whatever name. The certificate
13 shall qualify the employer for a premium reduction for a one-year
14 period.
15 2. Upon issuance of a certificate to an employer, the
16 Commissioner of the Department of Labor shall mail a copy of the
17 certificate to the employer’s insurer. Any insurer required by this
18 section to allow an appropriate reduction in premium charges to a
19 qualified employer which willfully fails to allow such reduction
20 after receiving a copy of the certificate shall be subject, after
21 notice and hearing, to an administrative fine, imposed by the
22 Insurance Commissioner, which shall be not less than Ten Thousand
23 Dollars ($10,000.00) or three times the amount of the premium
24 reduction, whichever is greater. The Insurance Commissioner shall
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 273
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 prom ulgate rules necessary to carry out the provisions of this
2 paragraph.
3 F. The Insurance Commissioner and the Administrator of the
4 Workers’ Compensation Court shall maintain records documenting
5 reductions in workers’ compensation insurance premiums granted
6 pursuant to this section and shall make, utilizing the centralized
7 filing system provided for in Section 378 of this act,
8 electronically submit an annual report of such reductions to the
9 President Pro Tempore of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of
10 Representatives by May 1 of each year. Insurers shall report such
11 premium reductions in their annual statement.

6 or after November 1, 2014, where the interest of a child under
7 eighteen (18) years of age is involved, the adult parties shall
8 attend, either separately or together, an educational program
9 concerning the impact of divorce on children. The program shall
10 include the following components:
11 1. Short-term and longitudinal effects of divorce on child
12 well-being;
13 2. Reconciliation as an optional outcome;
14 3. Effects of family violence;
15 4. Potential child behaviors and emotional states during and
16 after divorce including information on how to respond to the child’s
17 needs;
18 5. Communication strategies to reduce conflict and facilitate
19 cooperative coparenting; and
20 6. Area resources, including but not limited to nonprofit

15 application for licensure or certification;

17 to, anticipated changes in licensure status or retirement; and
18 3. Workforce practice trends, including, but not limited to:
19 a. practice specialization, if applicable,
20 b. practice location and physical address, if applicable,
21 c. service delivery area, by county,
22 d. if the licensee is currently using their license and
23 if its average use is between one (1) through ten
24 (10), eleven (11) through twenty (20), twenty-one (21)
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 286
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 through thirty (30), or over thirty-one (31) hours of
2 client-facing hours per week, and
3 e. the use of telehealth.
4 D. The boards designated in subsection A of this section shall
5 promulgate rules to effectuate the provisions of this section.
6 E. The boards designated in subsection A of this section shall
7 furnish de-identified annual licensure and certification data to the
8 Oklahoma Department of Mental Health and Substance Abuse Services.
9 F. The Oklahoma Department of Mental Health and Substance Abuse
10 Services shall compile the de-identified information collected in
11 this section into an annual report. The report shall not contain
12 any personal identifying information. The annual report shall be
13 posted on the agency’s website and electronically submitted to the
14 President Pro Tempore of the Oklahoma State Senate, the Speaker of
15 the Oklahoma House of Representatives, and the chairs of the
16 committees with responsibility over public health in both chambers
17 of the Legislature utilizing the centralized filing system provided
18 for in Section 378 of this act.
19 G. Nothing in this section shall be interpreted to expand the
20 amount of public-facing data about Oklahoma’s individual behavioral
21 health licensees.

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 D. The Board shall retain qualified investment managers to
2 provide for the investment of the monies of the System. The
3 investment managers shall be chosen by a solicitation of proposals
4 on a competitive bid basis pursuant to standards set by the Board.
5 Subject to the overall investment guidelines set by the Board, the
6 investment managers shall have full discretion in the management of
7 those monies of the System allocated to the investment managers.
8 The Board shall manage those monies not specifically allocated to
9 the investment managers. The monies of the System allocated to the
10 investment managers shall be actively managed by the investment
11 managers, which may include selling investments and realizing losses
12 if such action is considered advantageous to longer term return
13 maximization. Because of the total return objective, no distinction
14 shall be made for management and performance evaluation purposes
15 between realized and unrealized capital gains and losses.

23 of Oklahoma within their respective districts or at the District
24 Attorneys Council. To implement this program, the use of technology
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 308
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 and software to aid in detection of offenses involving uninsured
2 motorists is necessary and district attorneys and participating law
3 enforcement agencies shall have the authority to enter into
4 contractual agreements with automated license plate reader providers
5 to provide necessary technology, equipment, and maintenance thereof.
6 C. 1. Participating law enforcement agencies may use automatic
7 license plate reader systems utilizing individual automatic license
8 plate reader system units to access and collect data for the
9 investigation, detection, analysis, or enforcement of Oklahoma’s
10 Compulsory Insurance Law.
11 2. To accomplish the purposes of the program, law enforcement
12 agencies shall be allowed to access the online verification system
13 for motor vehicle liability policies to establish compliance with
14 the Compulsory Insurance Law as provided in Section 7-600.2 of Title
15 47 of the Oklahoma Statutes.
16 3. Access to the system shall be restricted to authorized law
17 enforcement agency users in the program; provided, any entity with
18 which a contract is executed to provide necessary technology,
19 equipment and maintenance for purposes of the program shall be
20 authorized, as necessary, to collaborate for required updates and
21 maintenance of their software.
22 4. Any data collected and stored by law enforcement pursuant to
23 the program shall be considered evidence if noncompliance with the
24 Compulsory Insurance Law is confirmed.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 309
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 D. A law enforcement officer may verify by sworn affidavit that
2 a photograph generated by an automatic license plate reader system
3 unit identifies a particular vehicle operating on or having been
4 operated on a public road, highway, street, turnpike, other public
5 place or upon any private road, street, alley or lane which provides
6 access to one or more single-family or multifamily dwellings and
7 that the online verification system shows that the vehicle was
8 uninsured at the time such vehicle was being operated. The
9 affidavit shall constitute probable cause for prosecution under
10 applicable state law.
11 E. Data collected or retained through the use of an automated
12 license plate reader system pursuant to the program shall be
13 retained by a law enforcement agency when the data is being used as
14 evidence of a violation of the Compulsory Insurance Law; provided,
15 when the data is no longer needed as evidence of a violation, the
16 data shall be deleted or destroyed.
17 F. Data collected or retained through the use of an automated
18 license plate reader system shall not be used by any individual or
19 agency for purposes other than enforcement of the Compulsory
20 Insurance Law or as otherwise permitted by law.
21 1. No law enforcement agency or other entity authorized to
22 operate under this program shall sell captured license plate data
23 for any purpose or share it for any purpose not expressly authorized
24 by this section.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 310
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. Any and all data collected, retained, or shared through the
2 use of an automated license plate reader system, except data
3 retained as evidence of a violation of the Compulsory Insurance Law,
4 shall be exempt from the Oklahoma Open Records Act.
5 G. The provisions of the program shall not apply to, or be
6 construed or interpreted in a manner to prohibit the use of, any
7 other automated license plate reader system by an individual or
8 private legal entity for purposes not otherwise prohibited by law.
9 H. The provisions of the program shall not be implemented until
10 such time that the Insurance Department verifies that the following
11 conditions have been met:
12 1. At least Ninety-Five Percent (95%) of the personal lines
13 auto insurance market in the state participates in the Oklahoma
14 Compulsory Insurance Verification System using a real-time web
15 portal system; and
16 2. The Oklahoma Compulsory Insurance Verification System is
17 updated in such a way to allow for the provisions of the program to
18 be implemented without interrupting or impeding any other lawful
19 uses of the system.
20 I. Following the implementation of the program and every year
21 thereafter, the District Attorneys Council shall publish an annual
22 report for the previous fiscal year of the Uninsured Vehicle
23 Enforcement Program by September 1. An electronic copy of the
24 report shall be distributed to the President Pro Tempore of the
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 311
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Sena te and the Speaker of the House of Representatives and the
2 chairs of the House and Senate Appropriations Committees utilizing
3 the centralized filing system provided for in Section 378 of this
4 act. The report shall comprise an evaluation of program operations,
5 and may include any information and recommendations for improvement
6 of the program deemed appropriate by the entity submitting the
7 report.
8 J. For purposes of this section:
9 1. “Automatic license plate reader system” means a system of
10 one or more mobile or law-enforcement-controlled cameras combined
11 with computer algorithms to convert images of registration plates
12 into computer-readable data;
13 2. “Law enforcement agency” includes the district attorney’s
14 office of any county, the Department of Public Safety, the sheriff’s
15 office of any county, and the chiefs of police of any city or town
16 having a population of more than one hundred thousand (100,000)
17 residents; and
18 3. “Program” means the Uninsured Vehicle Enforcement Program.
19 SECTION 92. AMENDATORY 47 O.S. 2021, Section 7-606.2, is
20 amended to read as follows:
21 Section 7-606.2. A. Each district attorney may create within
22 the district attorney’s office an Uninsured Vehicle Enforcement
23 Diversion Program and assign sufficient staff and resources for the
24 efficient operation of the program. The purpose of the Uninsured
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 312
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Vehi cle Enforcement Diversion Program is to authorize the district
2 attorney to divert complaints involving the failure to comply with
3 mandatory vehicle liability insurance coverage from criminal court
4 to the Uninsured Vehicle Enforcement Diversion Program and to
5 enhance public safety and security through increased compliance with
6 mandatory vehicle liability insurance coverage.
7 B. 1. Referral of a criminal complaint to the Uninsured
8 Vehicle Enforcement Diversion Program shall be at the discretion of
9 the district attorney. This act shall not limit the power of the
10 district attorney to prosecute Compulsory Insurance Law complaints.
11 2. Upon receipt of a complaint for failure to comply with the
12 Compulsory Insurance Law, the district attorney shall determine if
13 the complaint is one which is appropriate for deferred prosecution.
14 3. In determining whether to defer prosecution and refer a case
15 to the Uninsured Vehicle Enforcement Diversion Program, the district
16 attorney shall consider the following factors:
17 a. whether the criminal complaint alleges an offense
18 involving the failure to maintain required vehicle
19 liability insurance coverage,
20 b. whether it is in the best interest of the accused for
21 the accused person to be processed through deferred
22 prosecution in the Uninsured Vehicle Enforcement
23 Diversion Program,
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 313
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 c. the prospects for adequate protection of the public if
2 the accused person is processed through deferred
3 prosecution in the Uninsured Vehicle Enforcement
4 Diversion Program,
5 d. the number of criminal complaints against the
6 defendant previously received by the district
7 attorney,
8 e. whether or not there are other criminal complaints
9 currently pending against the defendant, and
10 f. the strength of the evidence of the particular
11 criminal complaint.
12 C. Upon referral of a complaint to the Uninsured Vehicle
13 Enforcement Diversion Program, a notice of the complaint shall be
14 forwarded by mail to the last known address of the record owner of
15 the vehicle. The notice shall contain:
16 1. The date the act which is the subject of the complaint
17 occurred;
18 2. A statement of the penalty for the violation of the
19 Compulsory Insurance Law which is the subject of the complaint;
20 3. A statement that the records of the State of Oklahoma
21 indicate that the owner of the vehicle is not in compliance with the
22 provisions of the Compulsory Vehicle Insurance Law and that the
23 complaint against the owner has been referred to the Uninsured
24 Vehicle Enforcement Diversion Program; and
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 314
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 4. The date before which the owner must shall contact the
2 office of the district attorney concerning the complaint.
3 D. If the owner fails to comply with the letter, the district
4 attorney may file the information and proceed with the prosecution
5 of the owner as provided by law.
6 E. The district attorney may enter into a written agreement
7 with the owner pursuant to the provisions of Sections 305.1 through
8 305.6 of Title 22 of the Oklahoma Statutes to defer prosecution on
9 the complaint for a period to be determined by the district
10 attorney, not to exceed two (2) years. The conditions of an
11 agreement to defer prosecution shall include:
12 1. The owner shall provide verification of current insurance
13 upon request of the district attorney;
14 2. The owner shall comply with the provisions of the Compulsory
15 Insurance Law for the full term of the agreement; and
16 3. The owner shall not own or operate any vehicle in violation
17 of the Compulsory Insurance Law during the full term of the
18 agreement.
19 F. Each diversion agreement shall include a provision requiring
20 the owner to pay to the district attorney’s office or District
21 Attorneys Council a fee equal to the amount which would have been
22 assessed as court costs upon the filing of the case in district
23 court pursuant to the provisions of Section 153 of Title 28 of the
24 Oklahoma Statutes. This fee shall be deposited in a special
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 315
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 dist rict attorney fund with the county treasurer to be known as the
2 “Uninsured Vehicle Enforcement Diversion Program Fund”. Diversion

4 special fund to be known as the “Uninsured Vehicle Enforcement
5 Diversion Program Fund”.
6 1. Each diversion agreement shall also include a provision
7 requiring the owner to pay an additional fee of Twenty Dollars
8 ($20.00) to the District Attorneys Council, of which Five Dollars
9 ($5.00) will be used in processing the payment, Ten Dollars ($10.00)
10 will be used in operating and maintaining the Compulsory Insurance

19 C. Verification of lawful presence under the provisions of this
20 section shall not be required:
21 1. For any purpose for which lawful presence in the United
22 States is not restricted by law, ordinance, or regulation;
23 2. For assistance for health care items and services that are
24 necessary for the treatment of an emergency medical condition, as
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 330
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 defi ned in 42 U.S.C., Section 1396b(v)(3), of the alien involved and
2 are not related to an organ transplant procedure;
3 3. For short-term, noncash, in-kind emergency disaster relief;
4 4. For public health assistance for immunizations with respect
5 to diseases and for testing and treatment of symptoms of
6 communicable diseases whether or not such symptoms are caused by a
7 communicable disease;
8 5. For application of special volunteer health care licenses
9 that specify the eligible volunteer shall be either retired from
10 practice in this state or actively licensed with a clean record in
11 another state and that such eligible volunteer shall not receive or
12 have the expectation to receive any payment or compensation, either
13 direct or indirect, for any services rendered in this state under
14 the special volunteer license; or
15 6. For programs, services, or assistance such as soup kitchens,
16 crisis counseling and intervention, and short-term shelter specified
17 by the United States Attorney General, in the sole and unreviewable
18 discretion of the United States Attorney General after consultation
19 with appropriate federal agencies and departments which:
20 a. deliver in-kind services at the community level,
21 including through public or private nonprofit
22 agencies,
23 b. do not condition the provision of assistance, the
24 amount of assistance provided, or the cost of
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 331
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 assistance provided on the income or resources of the
2 individual recipient, and
3 c. are necessary for the protection of life or safety.
4 D. Verification of lawful presence in the United States by the
5 agency or political subdivision required to make such verification
6 shall require that the applicant execute an affidavit under penalty
7 of perjury that:
8 1. He or she is a United States citizen; or
9 2. He or she is a qualified alien under the federal Immigration
10 and Nationality Act and is lawfully present in the United States.
11 The agency or political subdivision providing the state or local
12 public benefits shall provide notary public services at no cost to
13 the applicant.
14 E. For any applicant who has executed the affidavit described
15 in paragraph 2 of subsection D of this section, eligibility for
16 benefits shall be verified through the Systematic Alien Verification
17 for Entitlements (SAVE) Program operated by the United States
18 Department of Homeland Security or an equivalent program designated
19 by the United States Department of Homeland Security. Until such
20 eligibility verification is made, the affidavit may be presumed to
21 be proof of lawful presence for the purposes of this section.
22 F. Any person who knowingly and willfully makes a false,
23 fictitious, or fraudulent statement of representation in an
24 affidavit executed pursuant to subsection D of this section shall be
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 332
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 subj ect to criminal penalties applicable in this state for
2 fraudulently obtaining public assistance program benefits. If the
3 affidavit constitutes a false claim of U.S. citizenship under 18
4 U.S.C., Section 911, a complaint shall be filed by the agency
5 requiring the affidavit with the United States Attorney General for
6 the applicable district based upon the venue in which the affidavit
7 was executed.
8 G. Agencies or political subdivisions of this state may adopt
9 variations to the requirements of the provisions of this section
10 which demonstrably improve the efficiency or reduce delay in the
11 verification process, or to provide for adjudication of unique
12 individual circumstances where the verification procedures in this
13 section would impose unusual hardship on a legal resident of
14 Oklahoma.
15 H. It shall be unlawful for any agency or a political
16 subdivision of this state to provide any state, local, or federal
17 benefit, as defined in 8 U.S.C., Section 1621, or 8 U.S.C., Section
18 1611, in violation of the provisions of this section.
19 I. Each state agency or department which administers any
20 program of state or local public benefits shall provide
21 electronically submit, utilizing the centralized filing system
22 provided for in Section 378 of this act, an annual report to the
23 Governor, the President Pro Tempore of the Senate and the Speaker of
24 the House of Representatives with respect to its compliance with the
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 333
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 prov isions of this section. Each agency or department shall monitor
2 the Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements Program for
3 application verification errors and significant delays and shall
4 provide an annual public report on such errors and significant
5 delays and recommendations to ensure that the application of the
6 Systematic Alien Verification of Entitlements Program is not
7 erroneously denying benefits to legal residents of Oklahoma. Errors
8 shall also be reported to the United States Department of Homeland
9 Security by each agency or department.

3 SECTION 102. AMENDATORY 56 O.S. 2021, Section 198.16, is
4 amended to read as follows:
5 Section 198.16. A. In order to implement the Oklahoma Self-
6 Directed Care Act:
7 1. The Oklahoma Health Care Authority Board and the Commission
8 for Human Services are hereby authorized to promulgate rules
9 necessary to enact the provisions of this act;
10 2. The Oklahoma Health Care Authority shall take all actions
11 necessary to ensure state compliance with federal regulations;
12 3. The Authority shall apply for any necessary federal waivers
13 or waiver amendments required to implement the program;
14 4. The Legislature intends that, as consumers relocate from
15 institutional settings to community-based options, funds used to
16 serve consumers in institutional settings shall follow consumers to
17 cover the cost of community-based services; and
18 5. The Department of Human Services or other applicable state
19 entity for the population served may develop an electronic benefit
20 transfer feature for the provision of self-directed care services to
21 consumers.
22 B. The Oklahoma Self-Directed Care Act, at a minimum, shall
23 meet the following requirements:
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 346
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 1. The cost in the aggregate of the services offered through
2 the self-directed care plan shall be equal to or less than the cost
3 of a home- and community-based waiver or comparable waiver program;
4 2. The baseline level of consumer satisfaction shall be
5 measured by a third party prior to initiation of the Oklahoma Self-
6 Directed Care Act;
7 3. The scope of services offered within the Self-Directed Care
8 Program shall comply with current state statutes and rules, and
9 federal regulations; and
10 4. Program evaluation which shall include an indication of
11 whether consumer satisfaction for Self-Directed Care Program
12 consumers is higher than or equal to consumer satisfaction for home-
13 and community-based waiver clients or other comparable waiver
14 programs, as measured by a third party.
15 C. Upon the approval of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid
16 Services and the availability of funds, the Authority and the
17 Department shall implement the Self-Directed Care Program statewide
18 if the evaluation provided for in subsection B of this section
19 demonstrates consumer satisfaction with and cost-effectiveness in
20 the delivery of the program.
21 D. The Authority and the Department shall conduct a feasibility
22 study on the future design and implementation of expanding the home-
23 and community-based waiver program to include additional people with
24 developmental disabilities, spinal cord injury or traumatic brain
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 347
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 inju ry; provided, however, before allocating any new monies to such
2 program, the Department and the Authority shall prepare and,
3 utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in Section 378
4 of this act, electronically submit to the Legislature the results of
5 the feasibility study and a fiscal impact statement.
6 E. The Authority and the Department of Human Services shall
7 each, on an ongoing basis, review and assess the implementation of
8 the Self-Directed Care Program. By January 15 of each year, the
9 Authority shall, utilizing the centralized filing system provided
10 for in Section 378 of this act, electronically submit a written
11 report to the Governor and Legislature that includes each agency’s
12 review of the program.

9 SECTION 104. AMENDATORY 56 O.S. 2021, Section 230.50, is
10 amended to read as follows:
11 Section 230.50. A. Sections 230.50 through 230.73 and 241.1
12 through 241.3 of this title shall be known and may be cited as the
13 “Statewide Temporary Assistance Responsibility System (STARS)”.
14 B. 1. There is hereby established the Statewide Temporary
15 Assistance Responsibility System (STARS) which shall consist of
16 programs and services to be offered by the state with funds provided
17 by the temporary assistance for needy families block grant
18 authorized by Section 103 of the federal Personal Responsibility and
19 Work Opportunity Reconciliation Act of 1996, P.L. 104-193, in
20 addition to other monies appropriated by law.
21 2. The STARS shall consist of integrated and coordinated
22 programs and services that will provide recipients with the
23 necessary tools to enable them to make the transition from reliance
24 on public assistance programs to becoming independent, self-
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 350
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 suff icient citizens who are capable of supporting themselves and
2 their families. Such programs and services include but are not
3 limited to: career guidance and employment preparation, job
4 referral, vocational and technical training, child care initiatives,
5 literacy development, adult basic education, and medical assistance.
6 3. The Oklahoma Legislature further recognizes the importance
7 of encouraging establishment of statewide, One-stop
8 Career/Employment Centers that would link existing resources and
9 federal and state programs for utilization by persons receiving
10 temporary assistance, and underemployed and unemployed persons.
11 C. The Department of Human Services shall be responsible for:
12 1. Implementing the federal Temporary Assistance for Needy
13 Families Act (TANF);
14 2. Carrying out the projects and programs specified in the
15 STARS. The Department shall implement the provisions of the STARS
16 to the fullest extent permitted by law and in accordance with the
17 terms and conditions specified by the federal government;
18 3. Expediting the acquisition of any federal waivers necessary
19 to implement the provisions of STARS through amendments, combined
20 waivers, requests, and in any other such manner authorized by
21 federal law and regulations;
22 4. Working in close and continuous coordination with
23 appropriate federal officials and preparing and submitting
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 351
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 comp letely and in a timely manner all forms and data required by
2 federal officials to implement STARS; and
3 5. Continuing to explore and expand opportunities for the state
4 to pursue agreements with additional service providers including but
5 not limited to community colleges, technology centers, and community
6 resources in order to provide direct referral and link recipients
7 with career and technology education and training programs.
8 D. In administering the STARS, the Department shall collaborate
9 with the Oklahoma Employment Security Commission, the Department of
10 Commerce, the State Department of Health and the State Department of
11 Education and any other state or federal agency necessary to
12 implement the provisions of the STARS.
13 E. In submitting any state plan or amendments to the state plan
14 to the federal government in order to obtain a temporary assistance
15 for needy families block grant or child care and development block
16 grant, the Department shall seek the maximum block grant funding and
17 federal contingency fund allocations that are available pursuant to
18 the federal Personal Responsibility and Work Opportunity
19 Reconciliation Act of 1996, P.L. 104-193.
20 F. The Department for Human Services shall promulgate rules for
21 developing any projects and programs to implement the STARS.
22 G. 1. The Department of Human Services is directed to file
23 with the Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 352
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 requ est for amendment or exemption filed with the federal

8 fiscal year, the Department shall, utilizing the centralized filing

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 353

11 work experience and supportive service needs of the applicant or
12 recipient and shall be:
13 1. Written in English, or translated into Spanish or other

18 4. Signed by the case manager; and
19 5. Reviewed by both the applicant or recipient and the case
20 manager at least once a year. The agreement may be revised from
21 time to time according to the needs of the recipient, the
22 recipient’s family, and the program.

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 356
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 1. Develop a detailed plan for achieving self-sufficiency;

6 limited to, financial management classes, conflict resolution

8 4. Be available for and actively seek and maintain employment,
9 and accept any reasonable employment as soon as it becomes available
10 as required by the TANF program;
11 5. Participate in a community service, public works or private
12 sector job pursuant to the requirements of the Statewide Temporary
13 Assistance Responsibility System and the results of the
14 employability and literacy assessments;
15 6. If the recipient is a minor parent, live in a supervised
16 adult setting;
17 7. Accept responsibility for ensuring that the recipient’s
18 child complies with the attendance requirements of the local school
19 district and attends school until the child of the recipient either:
20 a. graduates from high school or attains a high school

24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 357
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 8. Accept responsibility for attending any classes required by

6 testing instrument which measures whether such
7 recipient is reading at a minimum of an eighth grade

12 improvement program.

20 or her reading skills until such recipient

1 1. “Liquor store” means any retail establishment that sells

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 361
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

5 deemed permanently ineligible for TANF benefits. Individuals with
6 children receiving TANF benefits shall only be eligible to receive
7 benefit payments for dependent children as provided by state and
8 federal law.

3 SECTION 109. AMENDATORY 56 O.S. 2021, Section 1011.5, is
4 amended to read as follows:
5 Section 1011.5. A. 1. The Oklahoma Health Care Authority
6 shall develop an incentive reimbursement rate plan for nursing
7 facilities focused on improving resident outcomes and resident
8 quality of life.
9 2. Under the current rate methodology, the Authority shall
10 reserve Five Dollars ($5.00) per patient day designated for the
11 quality assurance component that nursing facilities can earn for
12 improvement or performance achievement of resident-centered outcomes
13 metrics. To fund the quality assurance component, Two Dollars
14 ($2.00) shall be deducted from each nursing facility’s per diem
15 rate, and matched with Three Dollars ($3.00) per day funded by the
16 Authority. Payments to nursing facilities that achieve specific
17 metrics shall be treated as an “add back” to their net reimbursement
18 per diem. Dollar values assigned to each metric shall be determined
19 so that an average of the five-dollar-quality incentive is made to
20 qualifying nursing facilities.
21 3. Pay-for-performance payments may be earned quarterly and
22 based on facility-specific performance achievement of four equally-
23 weighted, Long-Stay Quality Measures as defined by the Centers for
24 Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS).

15 SECTION 114. AMENDATORY 57 O.S. 2021, Section 115, is
16 amended to read as follows:
17 Section 115. A. A reentry program is hereby authorized to be
18 created within the Department of Corrections by January 1, 2005, for
19 offenders who need structured release prior to completion of the
20 sentence. The reentry program shall be designed to provide
21 structure and control as offenders near release to the community
22 without supervision. No offender otherwise eligible for any
23 community placement, halfway house, work release program, or minimum
24 security placement shall be eligible for this program. The program
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 375
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 shal l not receive state appropriations and shall be fully funded by
2 federal or private funds.
3 B. The reentry program shall begin in a county or counties
4 selected by the Department and may expand or change to other
5 locations within the state at the discretion of the Department. The
6 program shall provide a continuum of services to meet the needs of
7 offenders assigned or required to complete the program. The program
8 duration shall be eighteen (18) months composed of twelve (12)
9 months at minimum security level and six (6) months at community
10 placement prior to release. The inmate is required to complete
11 twelve (12) months at minimum security level prior to completing the
12 six (6) months at community placement. The Department is authorized
13 to use monitoring and supervision in all levels of security as
14 necessary to ensure structure, control, and compliance.
15 C. The Department shall designate at least one medium, minimum
16 and community security level facility capable of serving male and
17 female offenders assigned or required to complete the program. Male
18 and female offenders shall not be housed in the same facility. Each
19 of the designated facilities shall be solely devoted to the program.
20 Nothing in this act shall require all offenders to be assigned to a
21 reentry program prior to release without supervision.
22 D. Any offender assigned or required to complete a reentry
23 program who subsequently fails, refuses or is noncompliant in the
24 reentry program shall be removed from the program, after notice of a
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 376
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 prog ram failure, and placed in a higher security level facility
2 according to the rules established for the program. Earned credits
3 may be withheld for those inmates who fail to satisfactorily
4 complete the program as provided by rule.
5 E. All services available in the reentry program shall be
6 selected after open bid and the Department shall actively solicit
7 faith-based and secular providers in all service provider
8 categories. Offenders assigned to the reentry program shall choose
9 a provider of services for each service category.
10 F. All service providers contracting for the reentry program
11 shall be required to meet outcome-based standards and evaluations
12 and are required to measure recidivism for all offenders placed for
13 services with that provider according to the rules of the
14 Department.
15 G. The Department shall consider offenders with long-term
16 incarceration, violent offenses, repeat offenders with multiple
17 incarcerations, offenders ineligible for parole who will be released
18 without supervision, offenders with parole stipulations, and
19 offenders having consecutive sentences longer than the calculation
20 of a life sentence for purposes of determing determining parole
21 eligibility.
22 H. The Governor and the Pardon and Parole Board shall work
23 together with the Department of Corrections within the capabilities
24 of the reentry program and shall have the authority to stipulate
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 377
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 that an offender shall be paroled, conditioned upon completion of
2 the program, without further hearing recommendation or approval.
3 I. No offender shall be placed in the reentry program until the
4 offender is within eighteen (18) months of release from the custody
5 of the Department or is granted, by stipulation or otherwise, a
6 parole release upon completion of the reentry program. Nothing in
7 this act shall operate to reduce the length of a sentence to
8 incarceration, except completion of the reentry program.
9 J. The Department shall establish rules and procedures to
10 implement the provisions of this act. An annual report shall be
11 generated at the completion of each calendar year. This report
12 shall reflect the evaluation of the program based on the outcomes
13 designated by the Department. Copies of said such report shall be
14 forwarded by electronic submission, utilizing the centralized filing
15 system provided for in Section 378 of this act, to the Governor, the
16 Chair of the Sentencing Commission, the President Pro Tempore of the
17 Senate, the Speaker of the House of Representatives, and the
18 majority and minority leaders of the Legislature.

6 SECTION 117. AMENDATORY 57 O.S. 2021, Section 541, is
7 amended to read as follows:
8 Section 541. A. There is hereby created in the State Treasury
9 an Industries Revolving Fund for the Department of Corrections.
10 B. The revolving fund shall consist of earnings derived from
11 prison industries operated by the Department of Corrections and from
12 that portion of the prisoner’s income apportioned and paid into the
13 prison system to recover the cost of incarceration of the prisoners
14 as provided by law. Funds derived from prisoner wages shall be
15 maintained and accounted for separately in this fund. The
16 Industries Revolving Fund shall be a continuing fund, not subject to
17 fiscal year limitations, and shall be under the control and
18 management of the State Board of Corrections.
19 C. Expenditures from the Industries Revolving Fund shall be
20 budgeted and expended pursuant to the laws of the state and the
21 statutes relating to public finance and to the institution. The
22 fund may be used to purchase, maintain, and repair machinery, to
23 purchase materials and supplies for the prison industries, and to
24 defray any other expenses necessary to operation of the industries,
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 383
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 with first priority being given to repairs, replacement, and
2 modernization of industrial or agricultural machinery or equipment.
3 These funds may also be used to support the overall operation of the
4 Department of Corrections subject to approval of the Director of the
5 Department of Corrections. Expenditures from the fund derived from
6 prisoner’s income may be used for the maintenance of prisoners in
7 prison institutions and all expenses related thereto under such
8 rules as may be established by the State Board of Corrections.
9 Warrants for expenditures from the Industries Revolving Fund shall
10 be based on claims signed by an authorized employee or employees of
11 the Department, and approved for payment by the Director of the
12 Office of Management and Enterprise Services. The Department shall
13 maintain a separate accounting of receipts and expenditures for each
14 industry for periodic review by the Legislature. The fund may not
15 be used to employ personnel in excess of those authorized by
16 legislative action.
17 D. All funds in the Department of Corrections’ Industries
18 Revolving Funds not encumbered or obligated upon the operative date
19 of this section shall be transferred to a single Industries

17 shall present to the Board a written recommendation and summary for

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

5 pursuant to the Administrative Procedures Act until final action by
6 the Board. Collection of unpaid, finalized administrative fines by
7 the Board, directly or through contracted services unless otherwise
8 provided in law, may be sought beginning ninety (90) days after
9 final disposition and order of the matter through the processes
10 established by this act and the Administrative Procedures Act.
11 E. The Construction Industries Board shall hear all appeals
12 timely made from an administrative ruling relating to an industry
13 and trade regulated by the Board; however, this appeal authority
14 shall not be in addition to the appeal process authorized by the
15 Administrative Procedures Act. Any ruling by the Board from an
16 administrative hearing may be further appealed to the district court
17 of Oklahoma County. The district court, upon conclusion of an
18 appeal from a Board ruling, shall be authorized to award reasonable
19 legal fees to the prevailing party.

6 b. elevator, two-four floors $ 75.00
7 c. elevator, five-ten floors $100.00
8 d. elevator, eleven floors and over $125.00
9 e. wheelchair lift $ 25.00
10 C. All revenues received shall be deposited to the Department
11 of Labor Revolving Fund. It is the intent of the Legislature that
12 fees charged pursuant to the Elevator Safety Act be adjusted to
13 provide sufficient income, but not substantially more than
14 sufficient income, to ensure elevator safety as provided by the
15 Elevator Safety Act. Accordingly, the Commissioner of Labor shall
16 make an annual study of the revenues to and expenditures from the
17 Department of Labor Revolving Fund related to elevator safety and
18 shall prepare a report indicating what fee adjustments, if any,
19 shall be recommended. The report shall be electronically submitted
20 by September 1 each year, utilizing the centralized filing system
21 provided for in Section 378 of this act, to the Director of the
22 Office of Management and Enterprise Services, the Chair of the
23 Appropriations Committee of the Senate, and the Chair of the
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 400
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Appr opriations and Budget Committee of the House of Representatives,
2 and shall be filed with the Department of Labor.
3 D. Licenses and certifications issued in accordance with the
4 provisions of the Elevator Safety Act shall be renewed according to
5 the following schedule:
6 1. Elevator contractor, elevator inspector, elevator mechanic
7 licenses, and elevator apprentice registration shall be renewed
8 annually prior to the last day of the calendar month in which the
9 license or registration was initially issued;
10 2. Any such license, registration or certificate required by
11 the Elevator Safety Act not renewed by the last day of the calendar
12 month in which renewal is required shall be subject to a late fee as
13 provided by this act;
14 3. Any elevator contractor, elevator inspector, elevator
15 mechanic license, or apprentice registration having been expired for
16 a period of not less than thirty (30) days nor more than three
17 hundred sixty-five (365) days shall be subject to a reinstatement
18 fee as provided for in the Elevator Safety Act; and
19 4. Any elevator contractor, elevator inspector, elevator
20 mechanic license, or apprentice registration being expired for a
21 period of one (1) year or longer from the last day of the month in
22 which renewal was required shall be considered void and the licensee
23 shall be subject to all requirements for new issuance.

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 401
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 SECTION 122. AMENDATORY 59 O.S. 2021, Section 4000.1, as
2 last amended by Section 9, Chapter 227, O.S.L. 2024 (59 O.S. Supp.
3 2025, Section 4000.1), is amended to read as follows:
4 Section 4000.1. A. As used in this section:
5 1. “Substantially relate” means the nature of the criminal
6 conduct for which the person was convicted has a direct bearing on
7 the fitness or ability to perform one or more of the duties or
8 responsibilities necessarily related to the occupation; and
9 2. “Pose a reasonable threat” means the nature of the criminal
10 conduct for which the person was convicted involved an act or threat
11 of harm against another and has a bearing on the fitness or ability
12 to serve the public or work with others in the occupation.
13 B. Notwithstanding any other provision of law, a conviction,
14 plea of guilty or nolo contendere, or pending criminal charge of a
15 crime may be grounds for the denial of an applicant for a state
16 license or state certification to practice an occupation only if the
17 underlying offense substantially relates to the duties and
18 responsibilities of the occupation and poses a reasonable threat to
19 public safety, health, or welfare. When making a determination
20 pursuant to this subsection, a licensing or certification authority
21 shall consider:
22 1. The nature and seriousness of the offense;
23 2. The amount of time that has passed since the offense;
24 3. The age of the person at the time the offense was committed;
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 402
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 4. Evidence relevant to the circumstances of the offense
2 including any aggravating or mitigating circumstances of social
3 conditions surrounding the commission of the offense;
4 5. The nature of the specific duties and responsibilities for
5 which the license or certification is required; and
6 6. Any evidence of rehabilitation submitted by the applicant
7 including, but not limited to, evidence related to the person’s
8 compliance with any conditions of community supervision, parole, or
9 mandatory supervision, the conduct and work activity of the person,
10 programming, or treatment undertaken by the person, and testimonials
11 or personal reference statements.
12 C. Notwithstanding any other provision of law, a licensing or
13 certification authority shall not deny a state license or state
14 certification to practice an occupation due to:
15 1. An arrest that was not followed by a valid plea of guilty or
16 nolo contendere unless charges are currently pending;
17 2. A conviction that has been sealed, or expunged;
18 3. A conviction or plea of guilty or nolo contendere for which
19 more than five (5) years have elapsed since the date of conviction,
20 plea, or release from incarceration, whichever is later, so long as
21 the person has not been convicted of a new crime. This paragraph
22 shall not apply to any conviction or plea of guilty or nolo
23 contendere for:
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 403
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 a. an offense enumerated in Section 571 of Title 57 of
2 the Oklahoma Statutes,
3 b. a felony involving domestic assault, domestic assault
4 and battery, or domestic abuse as defined in Section
5 644 of Title 21 of the Oklahoma Statutes,
6 c. an offense that would require registration as a sex
7 offender pursuant to the Sex Offenders Registration
8 Act, or
9 d. any equivalent law enumerated in this paragraph from
10 another jurisdiction; or
11 4. A finding that an applicant lacks good character or fails to
12 meet any other similarly vague standard where a criminal conviction
13 is the basis for the finding.
14 D. Before a state licensing or state certification authority
15 makes a final determination that a criminal conviction, plea of
16 guilty or nolo contendere, or pending criminal charge may disqualify
17 an applicant for licensure, that authority shall provide written
18 notice of:
19 1. The specific offense that is the basis for the intended
20 denial;
21 2. The reasons the offense was determined to substantially
22 relate to the duties and responsibilities of the occupation and
23 posed a reasonable threat to public safety, health, or welfare,
24 including findings for each of the factors in subsection B of this
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 404
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 sect ion that the licensing or certification authority deemed
2 relevant to the determination; and
3 3. The right to submit additional evidence relevant to each of
4 the factors listed in subsection B of this section within thirty
5 (30) days, which the licensing or certification authority shall
6 consider before issuing a final determination.
7 E. A final determination that a criminal conviction, plea of
8 guilty or nolo contendere, or pending criminal charge may prevent a
9 person from receiving a license shall be in writing and include
10 notice of the right to appeal the determination pursuant to the
11 Administrative Procedures Act, or a more specific statutory
12 authority, and notice of the earliest date the applicant may reapply
13 for a license.
14 F. A person with a criminal history record may request a
15 determination of whether his or her criminal history record may
16 disqualify him or her from obtaining the desired license or
17 certification in the occupation from a state licensing or state
18 certification authority at any time, including before obtaining any
19 required education or training for such occupation. The request
20 shall be in writing and shall include either a copy of the person’s
21 criminal history record with explanation of each conviction
22 mentioned in the criminal history record or a statement describing
23 each criminal conviction including the date of each conviction, the
24 court of jurisdiction and the sentence imposed. The person may
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 405
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 incl ude a statement with his or her request describing additional
2 information for consideration by the licensing or certification
3 authority including, but not limited to, information relevant to any
4 of the factors for consideration described in subsection B of this
5 section.
6 G. Upon receipt of a written request for consideration of a
7 criminal history record for an occupation as provided in subsection
8 F of this section, the licensing or certification authority shall
9 evaluate the request and make a determination based upon the
10 information provided in such request whether the stated conviction
11 is a disqualifying offense for the occupation. A notice of the
12 determination shall be issued to the petitioner within sixty (60)
13 days from the date such request was received by the licensing or
14 certification authority; except, however, a licensing or
15 certification authority regulating fifty thousand or more members in
16 its occupation shall be allowed ninety (90) days to make its initial
17 determination and issue notice to the requestor.
18 H. A determination made pursuant to subsection F of this
19 subsection that a person may not be disqualified for licensure or
20 certification due to criminal history shall be binding upon a
21 licensing or certification authority unless, at the time a full
22 application for a license is submitted, the applicant has
23 subsequently pled guilty or nolo contendere to a crime, has pending
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 406
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 crim inal charges, or has previously undisclosed criminal
2 convictions.
3 I. The notice of a determination made pursuant to subsection F
4 of this section shall be in writing and mailed to the requestor at
5 the address provided in his or her request, and shall contain the
6 following statements:
7 1. Whether the person is eligible for licensure or
8 certification in the occupation at the current time based upon the
9 information submitted by the requestor;
10 2. Whether there is a disqualifying offense that would
11 disqualify the person from engaging in the occupation at the current
12 time and a statement identifying such offense in the criminal
13 history record or information submitted for consideration;
14 3. Any actions the person may take to remedy a
15 disqualification, if any;
16 4. The earliest date the person may submit another request for
17 consideration, if any; and
18 5. A statement that the determination may be rescinded if, at
19 the time a full application for a license is submitted, the
20 applicant has subsequently pled guilty or nolo contendere to a
21 crime, has pending criminal charges, or has previously undisclosed
22 criminal convictions.
23 J. A state entity charged with oversight of an occupational
24 license or certification may promulgate forms for requests for
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 407
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 dete rminations for the occupation as authorized in subsection F of
2 this section. Each state licensing or certification authority may
3 charge a fee not to exceed Ninety-five Dollars ($95.00) for each
4 initial determination of eligibility it makes for the occupation
5 based upon the information provided by the requestor.
6 K. Each state licensing or state certification authority shall
7 include in its application for a license or certification and

6 As used in this section, “short-term cash management” means any
7 borrowing or any method employed by a school district or county to
8 obtain funds in advance of the receipt of tax revenue, and shall
9 include, but not be limited to, the issuance of certificates of

19 Section 178. A. The instrument or will creating such trust may

22 subject to the provisions of subsections C and E of this section,

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 417
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 with out compensation, but may be reimbursed for actual expenses
2 incurred in the performance of their duties as trustees. If the
3 said instrument or will makes no provisions in regard to any of the
4 foregoing, then the general laws of the state shall control as to
5 such omission or omissions. Every person hereafter becoming a
6 trustee of a public trust first shall take the oath of office
7 required of an elected public officer and every officer and employee
8 who handles funds of a public trust shall furnish a good and
9 sufficient fidelity bond in an amount and with surety as may be
10 specified and approved by the persons constituting a majority of
11 each of the governing bodies of the beneficiaries of the trust, such
12 bond to be in a surety company authorized to transact surety
13 business in the State of Oklahoma but in no event shall any bond be
14 required of a trustee. The cost of said bond shall be paid from
15 funds of the trust authority. The oaths of office shall be
16 administered by any person authorized to administer oaths in the
17 State of Oklahoma, and shall be filed with the Secretary of State in
18 trusts wherein the State of Oklahoma is the beneficiary; in the

20 beneficiary; and in the office of the clerk of the municipality in a

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 418
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 have five (5) trustees appointed by the Governor of the State of
2 Oklahoma with the advice and consent of the Senate. The terms of

4 one member shall be appointed for a term of one (1) year; one member
5 shall be appointed for a term of two (2) years; one member shall be
6 appointed for a term of three (3) years; one member shall be
7 appointed for a term of four (4) years; and one member shall be
8 appointed for a term of five (5) years. At the expiration of the

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 D. Meetings of trustees of all public trusts shall be open to

5 or report the activities of the meetings. In such trusts wherein
6 the State of Oklahoma is the beneficiary, a written notice of
7 trustees’ meetings shall be filed with the office of the Secretary
8 of State at least three (3) days prior to the meeting date. Records
9 of the trust and minutes of the trust meetings of any public trust
10 shall be written and kept in a place, the location of which shall be

19 2024 (60 O.S. Supp. 2025, Section 1403), is amended to read as
20 follows:
21 Section 1403. A. There is hereby created the Task Force on
22 Trust Administration Review and Reform. The Task Force shall be
23 composed of the following seven (7) members:
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 421
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 1. The Governor shall appoint three members, one of whom shall
2 be a member of the Oklahoma State Chamber, and one of whom shall be

5 appoint two members; and
6 3. The Speaker of the Oklahoma House of Representatives shall
7 appoint two members.

11 C. The Task Force shall, utilizing the centralized filing
12 system provided for in Section 378 of this act, electronically
13 provide a report to the Governor, the President Pro Tempore of the
14 Oklahoma State Senate, and the Speaker of the Oklahoma House of
15 Representatives with its recommendations for legislation by November
16 1 of each year in which changes are recommended.
17 D. A majority of the members shall constitute a quorum for the
18 purpose of conducting the business of the Task Force. The Task
19 Force shall meet quarterly and at such other times as may be called
20 by the chair.
21 E. Members of the Task Force shall serve without compensation
22 but shall be reimbursed for expenses incurred in the performance of
23 their duties in accordance with the provisions of the State Travel
24 Reimbursement Act.

7 condition threatens the security of the state correctional system,

1 Section 130. A. The provisions of the Public Competitive

7 2. The Transportation Commission and the Oklahoma Tourism and
8 Recreation Commission, by majority vote of all the members of each
9 Commission, declare that an emergency exists; or
10 3. The chief administrative officer of a public agency without
11 a governing body declares that an emergency exists.

15 agency the authority to declare an emergency whereby the provisions

18 Fifty Thousand Dollars ($150,000.00) in amount; provided, such

7 Section 34.11.1. A. There is hereby created the position of
8 Chief Information Officer who shall be appointed by the Governor.
9 The Chief Information Officer, in addition to having authority over
10 the Information Services Division of the Office of Management and
11 Enterprise Services, shall also serve as Secretary of Information
12 Technology and Telecommunications or successor cabinet position and
13 shall have jurisdictional areas of responsibility related to
14 information technology and telecommunications systems of all state
15 agencies as provided for in state law. The salary of the Chief
16 Information Officer shall not be less than One Hundred Thirty
17 Thousand Dollars ($130,000.00) or more than One Hundred Sixty
18 Thousand Dollars ($160,000.00).
19 B. Any person appointed to the position of Chief Information
20 Officer shall meet the following eligibility requirements:
21 1. A baccalaureate degree in Computer Information Systems,
22 Information Systems or Technology Management, Business
23 Administration, Finance, or other similar degree;
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 433
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. A minimum of ten (10) years of professional experience with
2 responsibilities for management and support of information systems
3 and information technology, including seven (7) years of direct
4 management of a major information technology operation;
5 3. Familiarity with local and wide-area network design,
6 implementation, and operation;
7 4. Experience with data and voice convergence service
8 offerings;
9 5. Experience in developing technology budgets;
10 6. Experience in developing requests for proposal and
11 administering the bid process;
12 7. Experience managing professional staff, teams, and
13 consultants;
14 8. Knowledge of telecommunications operations;
15 9. Ability to develop and set strategic direction for

3 O. As used in this section:
4 1. “High technology system” means advanced technological
5 equipment, software, communication lines, and services for the
6 processing, storing, and retrieval of information by a state agency;
7 2. “Consolidation contract” means a contract for several state
8 or public agencies for the purpose of purchasing information
9 technology and telecommunication goods and services; and
10 3. “Enterprise agreement” means an agreement for information
11 technology or telecommunication goods and services with a supplier
12 who manufactures, develops, and designs products and provides
13 services that are used by one or more state agencies.

15 SECTION 140. AMENDATORY 62 O.S. 2021, Section 34.200-1,
16 is amended to read as follows:
17 Section 34.200-1. A. The State Treasurer in cooperation with
18 the Office of Management and Enterprise Services shall produce a
19 written debt affordability study (study) to be presented to the
20 Legislature and the Governor.
21 B. The study shall be used to determine Oklahoma’s debt
22 position relative to its benchmark debt ratio of debt service as a
23 percentage of revenues. The study shall incorporate information
24 available in other sources, such as the Annual Bonded Indebtedness
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 461
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Repo rt produced by the State Treasurer, into an analysis of
2 Oklahoma’s debt position.
3 C. The study shall include the net tax-supported and net
4 revenue-supported debt of this state for the most recently concluded
5 fiscal year. It shall also include the debt for the most recently
6 concluded fiscal year of state agencies and state-beneficiary public
7 trusts which are authorized to issue debt.
8 D. The study shall include the following:
9 1. Projections of debt service, future debt issuance, and debt
10 to capacity, such as debt service as a percentage of revenues. Each
11 projection shall extend at least five (5) years from the study’s
12 fiscal year of publication;
13 2. A discussion of Oklahoma’s unfunded pension liabilities and
14 the impact of these liabilities on the state’s ability to borrow and
15 cost of debt;
16 3. An identification and calculation of relevant metrics
17 including, but not limited to, debt service as a percentage of
18 revenues, total debt as a percentage of state personal income, and
19 total debt per capita;
20 4. A comparison of debt metrics to a select group of at least
21 ten other states so that Oklahoma may be able to measure and
22 contextualize its debt relative to other states;
23 5. A sensitivity analysis to understand the effects of
24 uncertain conditions. This sensitivity analysis may include
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 462
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 anal ysis on the impact of debt ratios of revenues being above or
2 below expectations or interest rates increasing or decreasing from
3 positions at time of publication; and
4 6. An estimate of available debt capacity the state may issue
5 over the next five (5) years without causing the benchmark debt
6 ratio of debt service as a percentage of revenues to exceed five
7 percent (5%). This estimate is based on the state’s net tax-
8 supported debt and the debt of the relevant state units and
9 agencies.
10 E. In preparing any authorization of new debt, the debt-issuing
11 entity, the Legislature, and the Governor shall take the study’s
12 recommendations and estimates into consideration. In addition, the
13 study’s recommendations and estimates shall be taken into
14 consideration by the Legislature and the Governor during capital
15 planning and budgeting processes.
16 F. The State Treasurer and the Office of Management and
17 Enterprise Services shall report the results of the study to the
18 Legislature by electronically transmitting, utilizing the
19 centralized filing system provided for in Section 378 of this act, a
20 copy of such study to the Speaker of the House of Representatives,
21 the President Pro Tempore of the State Senate, and to the Governor
22 on or before January 15 of each year.
23 G. The study’s recommendations and estimates shall be advisory
24 and not binding.

5 C. Complaints shall be filed with the Civil Service Division
6 within ten (10) business days of the date of when such action

8 days from the filing of the complaint, with the exception of actions

12 action by the state agency. The review of the merits of the
13 complaint may be limited to the employee disciplinary file directly
14 at issue. In the event documents needed are not maintained in the
15 disciplinary file, or additional witnesses are requested by the
16 parties, the administrative law judge shall have the discretion to

18 disciplinary action taken. Complaints relating to punitive
19 transfers shall be administrated through mediation first and shall
20 only proceed to a hearing if mediation is unsuccessful. Employees

23 punitive transfer. Complaints relating to written reprimands shall

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 466
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

2 E of Section 3311 of Title 70 and Section 2-105 of Title 47 of the
3 Oklahoma Statutes shall be permitted to proceed to a hearing if
4 mediation is unsuccessful. Mediation may also be available for
5 other disciplinary actions.
6 E. Claimants shall be permitted to secure and utilize
7 representation during the adverse action process.
8 F. The presiding officer of any proceeding before the Civil
9 Service Division may require payment of reasonable attorney fees and
10 costs to the prevailing party if the position of the nonprevailing
11 party was without reasonable basis or was frivolous.
12 G. For purposes of this section, “disciplinary actions” means
13 termination, suspension without pay, involuntary demotion, punitive
14 transfers, or written reprimand.
15 H. Nothing in this section shall apply to:
16 1. Persons employed by the Governor, Lieutenant Governor,
17 Oklahoma House of Representatives, Oklahoma State Senate,
18 Legislative Service Bureau, or the Legislative Office of Fiscal
19 Transparency;
20 2. Elected officials;
21 3. Political appointees;
22 4. District attorneys, assistant district attorneys or other
23 employees of the district attorney’s office, and the District
24 Attorneys Council;
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 467
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 5. The state judiciary or persons employed by the state
2 judiciary;
3 6. Not more than five percent (5%) of an agency’s employees
4 designated as executive management as determined by the agency
5 director and the agency shall designate the status of the employee
6 as state employee or executive management in the State of Oklahoma’s
7 Human Resources Information System, maintained by the Human Capital
8 Management Division;
9 7. Temporary employees employed to work less than one thousand
10 (1,000) hours in any twelve-month period;
11 8. Seasonal employees employed to work less than one thousand
12 six hundred (1,600) hours in any twelve-month period;
13 9. Employees in a trial period; or
14 10. State employees whose employment status is otherwise
15 provided by law.
16 I. Except as provided by subsection H of this section,
17 effective January 1, 2022, all state employee positions shall be
18 administered by the Human Capital Management Division of the Office
19 of Management and Enterprise Services, without reference to prior
20 classified or unclassified status.
21 J. In collaboration with executive branch agencies, and their
22 human resources personnel, the Human Capital Management
23 Administrator shall establish and define statewide minimum standards
24 for human resource business processes, based on industry standards
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 468
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 and statewide best practices, to be followed by all executive branch
2 agencies. The Human Capital Management Administrator has the
3 authority to grant exceptions to the statewide minimum standards.
4 Additionally, the Human Capital Management Administrator shall
5 establish and maintain a statewide job catalog and pay structure for
6 executive branch jobs and establish policies and procedures for a
7 market-based pay system, pay-for-performance system, and dispute
8 resolution process for issues that do not rise to a disciplinary
9 action as provided by the Civil Service and Human Capital
10 Modernization Act. The Human Capital Management Administrator shall
11 promulgate rules necessary to carry out the authority set forth in
12 this section.
13 K. The Civil Service Division is authorized to employ attorneys
14 or contract with private attorneys to serve as legal counsel to the

16 for and represent the Civil Service Division in all litigation that
17 may arise from the discharge of its duties, including the
18 representation of the Civil Service Division when its decisions are

20 Management and Enterprise Services to represent the Civil Service
21 Division shall represent the Civil Service Division notwithstanding
22 its representation of the Office of Management and Enterprise

1 Ente rprise Services shall establish internal administrative

4 Section 89.2. A. The State Treasurer is directed to invest the
5 maximum amount of funds under control of the State Treasurer

11 1. Obligations of the United States Government, its agencies

18 associations, and credit unions located in this state;
19 3. Negotiable certificates of deposit issued by a nationally or
20 state-chartered bank, a savings bank, a savings and loan
21 association, or a state-licensed branch of a foreign bank.

23 percent (10%) of the cash available for investment which may be

6 acceptances shall not exceed ten percent (10%) of the cash available
7 for investment which may be invested pursuant to this section. Not

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 489

1 D of this section that have underlying collateral consisting of
