23 United States Department of State, or
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 490
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 b. any authoritarian or totalitarian government the
2 sovereign powers of which are exercised through a
3 single person or group of persons who are not elected
4 by any form of legitimate popular voting.
5 B. Investments shall be made with judgment and care, under
6 circumstances then prevailing, which persons of prudence,
7 discretion, and intelligence exercise in the management of their own
8 affairs, not for speculation, but for investment, considering the
9 probable safety of their capital as well as the probable income to
10 be derived.
11 C. The State Treasurer shall appoint an investment officer who
12 shall perform duties related to the investment of state funds in the
13 Office of the State Treasurer. The investment officer shall not
14 perform or supervise any accounting functions, data processing
15 functions or duties related to the documentation or settlement of
16 investment transactions.
17 D. Investments of public funds by the State Treasurer shall be
18 made in accordance with written policies developed by the State
19 Treasurer. The written investment policies shall address:
20 1. Liquidity;
21 2. Diversification;
22 3. Safety of principal;
23 4. Yield;
24 5. Maturity and quality; and
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 491
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 6. Capability of investment management.
2 The State Treasurer shall place primary emphasis on safety and
3 liquidity in the investment of public funds. To the extent
4 practicable taking into account the need to use sound investment
5 judgment, the written investment policies shall include provision
6 for utilization of a system of competitive bidding in the investment
7 of state funds. The written investment policies shall be designed
8 to maximize yield within each class of investment instrument,
9 consistent with the safety of the funds invested.
10 E. Except as provided in subsection H of this section, the
11 State Treasurer shall select one custodial bank to settle
12 transactions involving the investment of state funds under the
13 control of the State Treasurer. The State Treasurer shall review
14 the performance of the custodial bank at least once every year. The
15 State Treasurer shall require a written competitive bid every five
16 (5) years. The custodial bank shall have a minimum of Five Hundred
17 Million Dollars ($500,000,000.00) in assets to be eligible for
18 selection. Any out-of-state custodial bank shall have a service
19 agent in the State of Oklahoma so that service of summons or legal
20 notice may be had on such designated agent as is now or may
21 hereafter be provided by law. In order to be eligible for
22 selection, the custodial bank shall allow electronic access to all
23 transaction and portfolio reports maintained by the custodial bank
24 involving the investment of state funds under control of the State
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 492
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Trea surer. The access shall be given to the State Treasurer. The
2 requirement for electronic access shall be incorporated into any
3 contract between the State Treasurer and the custodial bank.
4 Neither the State Treasurer nor the custodial bank shall permit any
5 of the funds under the control of the State Treasurer or any of the
6 documents, instruments, securities, or other evidence of a right to
7 be paid money to be located in any place other than within a
8 jurisdiction or territory under the control or regulatory power of
9 the United States Government.
10 F. The investment policy shall specify the general philosophy,
11 policies, and procedures to be followed in the investment of state
12 monies by the State Treasurer. The investment policy shall include,
13 but not be limited to, the following:
14 1. Policy objectives;
15 2. Performance measure objectives;
16 3. Authority for investment program;
17 4. Possible use of an investment advisory committee;
18 5. Reporting and documentation of investments;
19 6. Authorized investment instruments;
20 7. Diversification of investment risk;
21 8. Maturity limitations;
22 9. Selections of financial institutions;
23 10. Interest controls;
24 11. Safekeeping of investments;
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 493
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 12. Investment ethics; and
2 13. Formal adoption of policy.
3 G. The State Treasurer may invest cash balance amounts as
4 defined and limited by Section 2402 of this title in the Invest in
5 Oklahoma program.
6 H. The State Treasurer is hereby authorized to establish
7 internal custodial accounts within the State Treasury, and enter
8 contractual relationships for custodial account services with
9 private institutions as needed, for the implementation and
10 administration of the Invest in Oklahoma program.
11 I. Not later than July 1 of each year, the State Treasurer
12 shall, utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in
13 Section 378 of this act, electronically forward a copy of the
14 written investment policy to the Governor, the Speaker of the House
15 of Representatives, the President Pro Tempore of the Senate, the
16 Attorney General, the Bank Commissioner, and the Director of the
17 Office of Management and Enterprise Services. In addition, the
18 State Treasurer shall maintain one copy of the investment policy in
19 the office of the State Treasurer for public inspection during
20 regular business hours. Copies of any modifications to the
21 investment policy shall be forwarded electronically to the Governor,
22 Speaker of the House of Representatives, President Pro Tempore of
23 the Senate, and each member of the Cash Management and Investment

10 Section 7004. A. For calendar years 2024 through 2027 and
11 every four (4) years thereafter, the Incentive Evaluation Commission
12 shall ensure that each incentive is evaluated within the four-year
13 evaluation period unless the Commission determines that the
14 incentive is exempt from evaluation. The Commission may exempt from
15 evaluation any incentive that it concludes has a minimal fiscal
16 impact. The Commission shall determine a specific threshold amount
17 which shall be considered as a minimal fiscal impact for the current
18 evaluation cycle. The Commission may also conduct an expedited
19 evaluation for any incentive that has been evaluated at least two
20 times from calendar years 2016 through 2023 and has not had a
21 material change to the program since its prior evaluation. The
22 expedited evaluation will update the prior evaluation’s financial
23 and economic impact and findings and recommendations.
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 521
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 B. For calendar years 2016 through 2023, the Incentive
2 Evaluation Commission shall develop a four-year schedule for
3 evaluating incentives. The development of the schedule for
4 evaluating the incentives shall take into consideration fiscal
5 impacts to revenues of this state, including but not limited to the
6 General Revenue Fund, the opportunity to group incentives with
7 similar goals and objectives for evaluation, and the ability to
8 obtain sufficient data related to the incentives for evaluation.
9 Each schedule shall include a list of all incentives in the state,
10 including any it exempts from evaluation. In determining whether a
11 program is an incentive, the Incentive Evaluation Commission may
12 consider legislative intent and may also consider whether the
13 program is promoted as an incentive by any state agency. For each
14 incentive listed in the schedule, the Commission shall attempt to
15 identify the goal or goals of the incentive.
16 C. Upon approval of the schedule, the Commission shall,
17 utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in Section 378
18 of this act, electronically provide the schedule to the Governor,

20 Representatives.

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 524
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 1. An estimate of the economic and fiscal impact of the
2 incentive. This estimate shall take into account the following
3 considerations in addition to other relevant factors:
4 a. the extent to which the incentive changes business
5 behavior,
6 b. the results of the incentive for the economy of
7 Oklahoma as a whole. This consideration includes both
8 positive direct and indirect impacts and any negative
9 effects on other Oklahoma businesses, and
10 c. a comparison to the results of other incentives or
11 other economic development strategies with similar
12 goals;
13 2. An assessment of whether adequate protections are in place
14 to ensure the fiscal impact of the incentive does not increase
15 substantially beyond the state’s expectations in future years;
16 3. An assessment of whether the incentive is being administered
17 effectively;
18 4. An assessment of whether the incentive is achieving its
19 goals;
20 5. Recommendations for how the state can most effectively
21 achieve the incentive’s goals, including recommendations on whether
22 the incentive should be retained, reconfigured, or repealed; and
23 6. Recommendations for any changes to state policy, rules, or
24 statutes that would allow the incentive to be more easily or

1 conc lusively evaluated in the future. These recommendations may

5 evaluated by the Commission. Each incentive shall be evaluated
6 using criteria specific to the individual incentive. The criteria
7 shall be developed by the Commission through the administrative

9 Section 250 et seq. of Title 75 of the Oklahoma Statutes, and
10 codified in the administrative code of the Oklahoma Department of
11 Commerce.

19 SECTION 163. AMENDATORY 62 O.S. 2021, Section 9010.4, is
20 amended to read as follows:
21 Section 9010.4. A. An agency or agencies may enter into a pay-
22 for-success contract with a private entity or entities to receive
23 up-front capital to fund a service or program. The agency or
24 agencies may not enter into a pay-for-success contract until each
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 526
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 stat e agency head entering into the contract determines with
2 reasonable certainty that the contract will result in a public
3 benefit to the state.
4 B. Each pay-for-success contract shall:
5 1. Require a private entity to underwrite or secure up-front
6 capital from private funding sources, including foundations,
7 financial institutions, businesses, or individuals;
8 2. Identify the specific service or program to be funded under
9 the contract;
10 3. Identify performance targets and outcome measures against
11 which the service or program’s success can be measured to determine
12 whether the service or program has achieved quantifiable public
13 benefits or monetary savings;
14 4. Require and specify an independent third-party evaluator to
15 review and issue reports annually at specific times during the
16 contract term specifying the degree to which the service or program
17 has met the identified performance targets and outcome measures
18 specified in the contract;
19 5. Identify the calculation or algorithm to be used by the
20 agency or agencies in determining the amount and timing of
21 reimbursable success payments to the private entity;
22 6. Contain a statement that the independent third-party
23 evaluator will annually provide a report to the agency or agencies
24 that includes data deemed relevant by the agency or agencies; and
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 527
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 7. State that the amount of funds to be reimbursed to the
2 private entity is contingent upon the degree to which the service or
3 program has met the performance targets and outcome measures as
4 evaluated by the independent third-party evaluator.
5 C. No later than April 1 annually, the agency or agencies
6 shall, utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in
7 Section 378 of this act, electronically provide a report to the
8 chairs of the legislative appropriations committees that contains
9 the evaluation from the independent third-party evaluator.
10 D. Payments to private entities for the delivery of performance
11 targets and outcome measures as authorized in this section shall be
12 made only in accordance with the terms of the pay-for-success
13 contract. Payments may be made utilizing the Pay for Success
14 Innovation Fund created in Section 5 of this act or utilizing other
15 appropriated agency funds in accordance with Oklahoma law.

23 is amended to read as follows:
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 558
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Section 1-2530.9. A. There is hereby created in the State
2 Treasury a revolving fund for the State Department of Health to be
3 designated the “Trauma Care Assistance Revolving Fund”. The fund
4 shall be a continuing fund, not subject to fiscal year limitations,
5 and shall consist of all monies received by the State Department of
6 Health from monies apportioned thereto for purposes of this section.
7 All monies accruing to the credit of the fund are hereby
8 appropriated and may be budgeted and expended by the Department as
9 follows:
10 1. Ninety percent (90%) of such monies shall be used to
11 reimburse recognized trauma facilities, licensed ambulance service
12 providers, and physicians for uncompensated trauma care expenditures
13 as documented in the statewide emergency medical services and trauma
14 analysis system developed pursuant to the provisions of Section 1-
15 2511 of this title. In lieu of or in combination with reimbursement
16 for uncompensated care, monies from the fund may also be used to
17 support readiness costs incurred by recognized trauma facilities
18 associated with ensuring a stable trauma care system with
19 availability of twenty-four-hour physician services for the
20 provision of trauma care. Any monies used for the treatment of
21 Medicaid-eligible patients that are subsequently used to establish
22 federal matching fund requirements shall also be reimbursed to
23 eligible trauma facilities, licensed ambulance service providers,
24 and physicians; and
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 559
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. Ten percent (10%) of such monies shall be used by the
2 Department in the furtherance of its powers and duties set forth in
3 the Oklahoma Emergency Response Systems Development Act.
4 B. Expenditures from the fund shall be made upon warrants
5 issued by the State Treasurer against claims filed as prescribed by
6 law with the Director of the Office of Management and Enterprise
7 Services for approval and payment.
8 C. The State Board of Health shall establish by rule a formula
9 and procedure for the distribution of funds for uncompensated trauma
10 care and/or or readiness costs that shall provide for the allocation
11 of funds to hospitals, ambulance service providers, and physicians.
12 D. Annually, monies accumulated in the fund may be transferred
13 to the Oklahoma Health Care Authority, by order of the State
14 Commissioner of Health, to maximize Medicaid reimbursement of trauma
15 care. The Oklahoma Health Care Authority shall use these funds with
16 federal matching funds to reimburse hospitals, ambulance service
17 providers and physicians for trauma care provided to severely
18 injured patients who are participants in Medicaid.
19 E. An annual report detailing the disbursements from the fund
20 shall be electronically provided on January 1 of each year to the
21 Speaker of the House of Representatives, the President Pro Tempore
22 of the Senate, and the Chair of each health-related committee of
23 both the House of Representatives and the Senate utilizing the

18 Section 2565. A. Any agency making decisions on utilization
19 management measures, coverage, reimbursement, or incentive programs
20 shall be required to consult with:
21 1. Organizations representing patients and people with
22 disabilities, including both self-advocacy organizations and
23 organizations representing patients, prior to proceeding on any
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 582
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 meas ure likely to impact the relevant patient or disability
2 community; and
3 2. Organizations representing patients and people that advocate
4 for the rights of patients to obtain treatment without regard to the
5 patients’ quality of life and representatives of organizations that
6 advocate for the rights of older persons to receive health care.
7 B. Any agency making decisions on utilization management
8 measures, coverage, reimbursement, or incentive programs shall
9 ensure that a process is in place to ensure robust stakeholder
10 engagement and full transparency surrounding the provision of any
11 research and analysis relied upon for decision-making that would
12 impact access to health care treatments and services by patient
13 groups provided for in subsection A of this section, including:
14 1. Providing stakeholders with meaningful notice and
15 opportunity to comment on the retention of any vendor providing
16 research and analysis to the agency;
17 2. Subjecting research and analysis relied upon by an agency to
18 meaningful notice and comment process;
19 3. Ensuring deliberation around the coverage or reimbursement
20 for health care treatments and services occurs in open meetings;
21 4. Presenting and releasing any research and analysis relied
22 upon for decision-making in public meetings or publicly released
23 prior to deliberation;
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 583
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 5. Requiring full disclosure into funding sources and conflicts
2 of interest of any third party providing research and analysis to
3 the state;
4 6. Prohibiting sole-source contracts for research and analysis
5 to ensure reliance on a range of evidence; and
6 7. Preparing an annual report assessing beneficiary access to
7 health care treatments and services. The report shall assess the
8 impact of any form of utilization management on access to care with
9 a specific analysis of the impact on persons with disabilities,
10 chronic illness, and advanced age. The report shall be
11 electronically submitted to the State Legislature utilizing the
12 centralized filing system provided for in Section 378 of this act,
13 be posted on the state Medicaid website, and the agency shall
14 provide an opportunity for public comment.
15 C. Any research and analysis relied upon for decision-making
16 that would impact coverage and access to health care treatments and
17 services shall measure outcomes prioritized by patients and persons
18 with disabilities as required by this section, as well as consider
19 meaningful differences in the characteristics, needs and preferences
20 of patients and persons with disabilities.

10 Section 1013. A. The Commissioners of the Land Office shall be
11 responsible for the investment of the permanent school funds, other
12 educational funds, and public building funds solely in the best
13 interests of the current and future beneficiaries. The
14 Commissioners of the Land Office shall make investments:
15 1. For the exclusive purpose of:
16 a. providing maximum benefits to current and future
17 beneficiaries, and
18 b. defraying reasonable expenses of administering the
19 trust funds;
20 2. With the care, skill, prudence, and diligence under the
21 circumstances then prevailing that a prudent person acting in a like
22 enterprise of a like character and with like aims would use; and
23 3. By diversifying the investments of the trust funds so as to
24 minimize the risk of large losses.

20 amended to read as follows:
21 Section 190. A. As it is immediately necessary for the safety
22 and welfare of the people, no railcar shall be brought to rest in a
23 position which blocks vehicular traffic at a railroad intersection
24 with a public highway or street for longer than ten (10) minutes.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 640
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 B. Municipalities, county sheriffs, and the Oklahoma Highway
2 Patrol shall have the authority to issue a citation to any person or
3 corporation that violates a provision of this section. Such person
4 or corporation shall be subject to a fine of up to One Thousand
5 Dollars ($1,000.00) for each violation. Seventy-five percent (75%)
6 of the collected fine shall be deposited to the credit of the
7 general fund of the entity that issued the citation and the
8 remaining twenty-five percent (25%) shall be credited to the
9 Corporation Commission Revolving Fund established in Section 180.7
10 of Title 17 of the Oklahoma Statutes. A copy of the citation, along
11 with any information regarding train identification, shall be sent
12 to the Corporation Commission for enforcement of the penalty at a
13 hearing before an administrative law judge of the Commission. The
14 violating entity or individual may appeal the administrative law
15 judge’s decision to the Commission en banc. The Commission shall
16 annually deliver an electronic report detailing the number of
17 violations, number of rulings, number of appeals, and amount of
18 fines assessed under this section. Commission reports shall be
19 delivered to the Speaker of the Oklahoma House of Representatives,
20 the President Pro Tempore of the Oklahoma State Senate, and the
21 Governor utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in
22 Section 378 of this act. The Commission shall promulgate rules and
23 procedures to effectuate the provisions of this section.
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 641
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 C. 1. Railroads or other persons, firms or corporations
2 operating over tracks within the State of Oklahoma shall not block
3 vehicle traffic at any railroad grade crossing for a period of time
4 in excess of ten (10) minutes except if the train is moving in a
5 continuous forward or backward direction, or if the train is stopped
6 for an emergency condition, including an accident, derailment,
7 critical mechanical failure, track or bridge washout, storm, flood
8 or other emergency situation.
9 2. A one-time exception of up to, but not exceeding, ten (10)
10 additional minutes shall be authorized under the following
11 conditions:
12 a. when a train and its crew, operating under the rules
13 of the Federal Railroad Administration (FRA), are
14 unable to complete a switching maneuver while setting
15 out or picking up railcars within the ten (10) minutes
16 as set forth in paragraph 1 of this subsection,
17 b. when a train is stopped to allow the passage of a
18 second train and the stopped train has exhausted the
19 ten (10) minutes as set forth in paragraph 1 of this
20 subsection, or if the arrival of the second train is
21 imminent and separation and coupling of the stopped
22 train would result in further unnecessary blocking of
23 motor vehicle or pedestrian traffic, and
24 c. when a train is stopped for a red train signal.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 642
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 3. When a train is cut or separated to prevent blocking of
2 motor vehicle traffic at a public crossing, and a working charging
3 station exists, the time required for recoupling a train and
4 performing air tests as required by the FRA shall not be considered
5 a violation of this section.
6 4. Every railroad shall be operated in such a manner as to
7 minimize obstruction of emergency vehicles at public highway grade
8 crossings.

1 SECTION 213. AMENDATORY 68 O.S. 2021, Section 102.3, is
2 amended to read as follows:
3 Section 102.3. In addition to their other duties, the members
4 of the Oklahoma Tax Commission shall make a continuous study of the
5 critical national energy crisis to determine its impact on the tax
6 revenues of Oklahoma, particularly the revenues derived from the
7 gross production taxes and gasoline and other motor fuel taxes and
8 also to determine and project the degree of the consequent erosion
9 of the present tax structure of the state which will be caused by
10 the gradual change from the use of oil and natural gas as a basic
11 fuel for energy. The Commission shall make periodic appraisals
12 concerning the several taxes directly related to the fuels presently
13 used by motor vehicles and other modes of travel and for heating and
14 cooling, as well as the tax paid by those engaged in the business of
15 producing oil and gas, as the use of substitute types of fuels
16 evolve which undoubtedly will result in substantial changes in the
17 types and size of vehicles used in the business activities of many
18 taxpayers.
19 The Commission shall also develop such econometric models as are
20 deemed necessary, compile data and other information as to the
21 possible rate of decline in tax revenue and, utilizing the

24 more than Twenty Million Dollars ($20,000,000.00). To the extent
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 667
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 data is available on the changes in the distribution of the tax

5 measures, such as the Suits or other similar indexes, by income
6 classes, taxpayer characteristics, or other relevant categories.
7 The report may include analyses of the effect of the bill or
8 proposal on representative taxpayers. The analysis must shall
9 include a statement of the incidence assumptions that were used in
10 computing the burdens. The report shall be electronically submitted
11 to the requestor utilizing the centralized filing system provided
12 for in Section 378 of this act.
13 B. The incidence analyses shall use the broadest measure of
14 economic income for which reliable data is available.

7 Section 1357.21. A. The exemption authorized by the provisions

3 purchased in calendar year 2022 shall be filed with the Oklahoma Tax
4 Commission not later than September 1, 2023, and in subsequent
5 calendar years shall be filed with the Oklahoma Tax Commission not
6 later than September 1 of the following year. Beginning in calendar
7 year 2025, claims for rebates for sales or use tax paid for such
8 equipment purchased in calendar year 2025 and subsequent calendar

16 Oklahoma Broadband Office, the Tax Commission shall issue a rebate
17 payment for all approved claims from funds in the Oklahoma Broadband
18 Rebate Revolving Fund, created pursuant to Section 2 of this act.
19 F. Qualifying purchases do not include supporting or ancillary
20 functions, such as office operations, field operations, marketing,
21 transportation, warehousing, data storage, or similar operations
22 that do not directly result in the distribution of broadband
23 Internet service. Property directly used or consumed in or during
24 the provision, creation, or production of a data processing service
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 672
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 or i nformation service, or property the provider grants, sells, or
2 leases to the customer for use within the home or establishment
3 receiving broadband is not eligible for a rebate under this section.
4 G. The total amount of rebates that may be paid shall not
5 exceed Fourteen Million Seventy-one Thousand Six Hundred Ninety-one
6 Dollars ($14,071,691.00).
7 H. The amount of rebates paid to each claimant shall be
8 computed by dividing the applicable total rebate pool amount by the
9 dollar amount of claims timely received by the Tax Commission with
10 respect to each fiscal year, and paying in full the amount of the
11 claims submitted if the amount of claims are equal to, or less than,
12 the total rebate pool, or a pro rata share if the total amount of
13 claims submitted exceed the rebate pool.
14 I. The total amount of rebates that may be paid shall not
15 exceed Fourteen Million Seventy-one Thousand Six Hundred Ninety-one
16 Dollars ($14,071,691.00).
17 J. Claims for rebate shall be on such forms as the Oklahoma Tax
18 Commission may prescribe for such purpose and shall contain any
19 required information or supporting documentation the Commission
20 requires to verify eligibility for the rebate payment. Claims for
21 rebate for sales or use tax paid for equipment purchased in calendar
22 year 2025 and subsequent calendar years shall be on such forms as
23 the Oklahoma Broadband Office may prescribe for such purpose.

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 K. The Oklahoma Department of Commerce shall use information

16 paid as a refund shall be adjusted annually to limit the annual
17 amount of credits to Five Hundred Thousand Dollars ($500,000.00).
18 The Tax Commission shall annually calculate and publish a percentage

20 paragraph 2 of subsection A of this section shall be reduced so the

22 not exceed Five Hundred Thousand Dollars ($500,000.00) per year.

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Hund red Thousand Dollars ($500,000.00) divided by the credits

9 documentation of the new direct jobs created under the Oklahoma
10 Remote Quality Jobs Incentive Act and a fiscal analysis of the costs
11 and benefits of this act to the state. The report shall be
12 electronically submitted to the President Pro Tempore of the Senate,
13 the Speaker of the House of Representatives, and the Governor of
14 this state no later than March 1, 2023, and every three (3) years
15 thereafter utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in
16 Section 378 of this act. The report may be used for the purpose of
17 determining whether to continue or sunset the Oklahoma Remote
18 Quality Jobs Incentive Act.

11 B. 1. The redbud school grants shall be determined by the
12 State Department of Education as follows:
13 a. divide the county four-mill levy revenue by four to
14 determine the nonchargeable county four-mill revenue
15 for each school district,
16 b. determine the amount of new revenue generated by the
17 five-mill building fund levy as authorized by Section
18 10 of Article X of the Oklahoma Constitution for each
19 school district as reported in the Oklahoma Cost
20 Accounting System for the preceding fiscal year,
21 c. add the amounts calculated in subparagraphs a and b of
22 this paragraph to determine the nonchargeable millage
23 for each school district,
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 735
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 d. add the nonchargeable millage in each district
2 statewide as calculated in subparagraph c of this
3 paragraph and divide the total by the average daily
4 membership in public schools statewide based on the
5 preceding school year’s average daily membership,
6 according to the provisions of Section 18-107 of this
7 title. This amount is the statewide nonchargeable
8 millage per student, known as the baseline local
9 funding per student,
10 e. all eligible charter schools shall be included in
11 these calculations as unique school districts,
12 separate from the school district that may sponsor the
13 eligible charter school, and the total number of
14 districts shall be used to determine the statewide
15 average baseline local funding per student,
16 f. for each school district or eligible charter school
17 which is below the baseline local funding per student,
18 the Department shall subtract the baseline local
19 funding per student from the average nonchargeable
20 millage per student of the school district or eligible
21 charter school to determine the nonchargeable millage
22 per student shortfall for each district, and
23 g. the nonchargeable millage per student shortfall for a
24 school district or eligible charter school shall be
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 736
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 multiplied by the average daily membership of the
2 preceding school year of the eligible school district
3 or eligible charter school. This amount shall be the
4 redbud school grant amount for the school district or
5 eligible charter school.
6 2. For fiscal year 2022, monies for the redbud school grants
7 shall be expended from the funds apportioned pursuant to Section 426
8 of Title 63 of the Oklahoma Statutes. For fiscal year 2023 and each
9 subsequent fiscal year, monies for the redbud school grants shall be
10 appropriated pursuant to Section 426 of Title 63 of the Oklahoma
11 Statutes, not to exceed three-fourths (3/4) of the tax collected in
12 the preceding fiscal year pursuant to Section 426 of Title 63 of the
13 Oklahoma Statutes as determined by the Oklahoma Tax Commission. For
14 fiscal year 2023 and each subsequent fiscal year, if such
15 appropriated funds are insufficient to fund the redbud school
16 grants, then an additional apportionment of funds shall be made from
17 sales tax collections as provided by subsection D of Section 1353 of
18 Title 68 of the Oklahoma Statutes. If both funds are insufficient,
19 the Department shall promulgate rules to permit a decrease to the
20 baseline local funding per student to the highest amount allowed
21 with the funding available.
22 3. As used in this section, “eligible charter school” shall
23 mean a charter school which is sponsored pursuant to the provisions
24 of the Oklahoma Charter Schools Act. Provided, however, eligible
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 737
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 char ter school shall not include a statewide virtual charter school

8 SECTION 253. AMENDATORY 70 O.S. 2021, Section 11-103.6,
9 as last amended by Section 1, Chapter 384, O.S.L. 2024 (70 O.S.
10 Supp. 2025, Section 11-103.6), is amended to read as follows:
11 Section 11-103.6. A. 1. The State Board of Education shall
12 adopt subject matter standards for instruction of students in the
13 public schools of this state that are necessary to ensure there is
14 attainment of desired levels of competencies in a variety of areas
15 to include language, mathematics, science, social studies,
16 communication, and health and physical education.
17 2. School districts shall develop and implement curriculum,
18 courses and instruction in order to ensure that students meet the
19 skills and competencies as set forth in this section and in the
20 subject matter standards adopted by the State Board of Education.
21 3. All students shall gain literacy at the elementary and
22 secondary levels. Students shall develop skills in reading,
23 writing, speaking, computing, and critical thinking. For purposes
24 of this section, critical thinking means a manner of analytical
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 753
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 thin king which is logical and uses linear factual analysis to reach
2 a conclusion. They shall learn about cultures and environments -
3 their own and those of others with whom they share the earth. All
4 students shall receive the instruction needed to lead healthy and
5 physically active lifestyles. Students, therefore, shall study
6 social studies, literature, languages, the arts, health,
7 mathematics, and science. Such curricula shall provide for the
8 teaching of a hands-on career exploration program in cooperation
9 with technology center schools.
10 4. The subject matter standards shall be designed to teach the
11 competencies for which students shall be tested as provided in
12 Section 1210.508 of this title and shall be designed to prepare all
13 students for active citizenship, employment, and/or or successful
14 completion of postsecondary education without the need for remedial
15 coursework at the postsecondary level.
16 5. The subject matter standards shall be designed with rigor as
17 defined in paragraph 6 of subsection F of this section.
18 6. The subject matter standards for English Language Arts shall
19 give Classic Literature and nonfiction literature equal
20 consideration to other literature. In addition, emphasis shall be
21 given to the study of complete works of literature.
22 7. At a minimum, the subject matter standards for mathematics
23 shall require mastery of the standard algorithms in mathematics,
24 which is the most logical, efficient way of solving a problem that
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 754
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 cons istently works, and for students to attain fluency in Euclidian

10 9. The subject matter standards for United States Government
11 shall include an emphasis on civics. For the purposes of this
12 section, “civics” means the study of the rights and duties of

14 work.

16 education shall include but not be limited to the domains of

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 765

1 scho ol accredited by the State Board of Education students shall

14 offer sufficient courses to allow a student to meet the graduation
15 requirements during the secondary grade years of the student.
16 G. For purposes of this section:
17 1. “Contextual methodology” means academic content and skills
18 taught by utilizing real-world problems and projects in a way that
19 helps students understand the application of that knowledge;
20 2. “Locally approved” means approved by the local school
21 district board of education;
22 3. “Math based application course” means a course that teaches
23 math content within a real-world application;
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 766
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 4. “Pathway unit” means a course taken by a high school student
2 that is aligned with the student’s current Individualized Career and
3 Academic Plan (ICAP);
4 5. “Qualified agricultural education courses” means courses
5 that have been determined by the State Board of Education to offer
6 the sets of competencies for one or more science content areas and
7 which correspond to academic science courses. Qualified
8 agricultural education courses shall include, but are not limited
9 to, horticulture, plant and soil science, natural resources and
10 environmental science, and animal science. The courses shall be
11 taught by teachers certified in agricultural education and comply
12 with all rules of the Oklahoma Department of Career and Technology
13 Education;
14 6. “Rigor” means a level of difficulty that is thorough,
15 exhaustive and accurate and is appropriate for the grade level;
16 7. “Science based application course” means a course that
17 teaches science content within a real-world application;
18 8. “Sets of competencies” means instruction in those skills and
19 competencies that are specified in the subject matter standards
20 adopted by the State Board of Education and other skills and
21 competencies adopted by the Board, without regard to specified
22 instructional time; and
23 9. “Unit” means a Carnegie Unit as defined by the North Central
24 Association’s Commission on Schools.

1 H. 1. The State Board of Education, State Board of Career and

6 rigor is maintained in the content, teaching methodology, level of

1 SECTION 254. AMENDATORY 70 O.S. 2021, Section 11-103.6a,
2 is amended to read as follows:
3 Section 11-103.6a. A. Beginning with the 2014-15 school year,
4 each area of subject matter standards, except for standards for
5 career and technology education adopted pursuant to Section 14-103
6 of this title, shall be adopted by the State Board of Education and
7 shall be subject to legislative review and approval as provided for
8 in Section 4 of this act. The subject matter standards shall be
9 implemented statewide by every public school district in this state.
10 The subject matter standards shall be thoroughly reviewed by the
11 State Board every six (6) years according to and in coordination
12 with the existing subject area textbook adoption cycle. After
13 review, the State Board shall adopt any revisions in such subject
14 matter standards deemed necessary to achieve further improvements in
15 the quality of education for the students of this state. Any
16 revisions adopted by the State Board of Education shall be subject
17 to review and approval as provided for in Section 4 of this act.
18 The adoption of subject matter standards or revisions to the
19 standards by the State Board of Education pursuant to this section
20 shall not be promulgated as rules and shall not be subject to
21 Article I of the Administrative Procedures Act.
22 B. 1. In addition to the requirements set forth in subsection
23 A of this section, on or before August 1, 2016, the State Board of
24 Education, in consultation with the State Regents for Higher
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 774

1 Educ ation, the State Board of Career and Technology Education and
2 the Oklahoma Department of Commerce, shall adopt subject matter
3 standards for English Language Arts and Mathematics which are
4 college- and career-ready and will replace current standards. To be
5 considered college- and career-ready, the standards shall be
6 evaluated by the State Department of Education, the State Regents
7 for Higher Education, the State Board of Career and Technology
8 Education and the Oklahoma Department of Commerce and be determined
9 to be such that the standards will address the goals of reducing the
10 need for remedial coursework at the postsecondary level and
11 increasing successful completion of postsecondary education. The
12 subject matter standards and corresponding student assessments for
13 English Language Arts and Mathematics shall be solely approved and
14 controlled by the state through the State Board of Education.

1 SECTION 255. AMENDATORY Section 1, Chapter 280, O.S.L.
2 2022, as last amended by Section 45, Chapter 59, O.S.L. 2024 (70
3 O.S. Supp. 2025, Section 11-202), is amended to read as follows:
4 Section 11-202. A. A school district, charter school, virtual
5 charter school, state agency, public library, or institution of
6 higher education within The Oklahoma State System of Higher
7 Education may offer digital or online library database resources to
8 students in kindergarten through twelfth grade only if the vendor,
9 person, or entity providing the resources verifies that all the
10 resources comply with the provisions of subsection B of this
11 section.
12 B. Digital or online library database resources offered by
13 school districts, charter schools, virtual charter schools, state
14 agencies, public libraries, or universities to students in
15 kindergarten through twelfth grade shall have safety policies and
16 technology protection measures that:
17 1. Prohibit and prevent a user of the resource from sending,
18 receiving, viewing, or downloading materials that are child sexual
19 abuse material or obscene materials, as defined in Section 1024.1 of
20 Title 21 of the Oklahoma Statutes, or materials that depict child
21 sexual exploitation, as defined in Section 843.5 of Title 21 of the
22 Oklahoma Statutes; and
23 2. Filter or block access to child sexual abuse material or
24 obscene materials, as defined in Section 1024.1 of Title 21 of the
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 782
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Okla homa Statutes, or materials that depict child sexual
2 exploitation, as defined in Section 843.5 of Title 21 of the
3 Oklahoma Statutes.
4 C. Notwithstanding any contract provision to the contrary, if a
5 provider of digital or online library resources fails to comply with
6 the requirements of subsection B of this section, the school
7 district, public charter school, state agency, public library, or
8 institution of higher education shall withhold further payments, if
9 any, to the provider pending verification of compliance.
10 D. If a provider of digital or online library database
11 resources fails to timely verify that the provider is in compliance
12 with the safety policies and requirements of subsection B of this
13 section, the school district, public charter school, state agency,
14 public library, or institution of higher education shall consider
15 the provider’s act of noncompliance a breach of contract.
16 E. No later than December 1 of each year, libraries shall,
17 utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in Section 378
18 of this act, electronically submit to the Speaker of the Oklahoma
19 House of Representatives and President Pro Tempore of the Oklahoma
20 State Senate an aggregate written report on any issues related to
21 provider compliance with technology protection measures required by
22 subsection B of this section.
23 F. Employees of school districts, charter schools, virtual
24 charter schools, state agencies, public libraries, and universities
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 783
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 shal l not be exempt from prosecution for willful violations of state
2 law prohibiting indecent exposure to obscene material or child
3 sexual abuse material as provided in Section 1021 of Title 21 of the
4 Oklahoma Statutes.
5 G. Nothing in this act shall be construed in a manner that
6 applies to digital or online library database resources offered by
7 institutions of higher education when the primary purpose of the
8 resources is for education or research.

15 Section 17-106.1. A. The Board of Trustees of the Teachers’

24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 787
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. With the care, skill, prudence, and diligence under the
2 circumstances then prevailing that a prudent person acting in a like
3 capacity and familiar with such matters would use in the conduct of
4 an enterprise of a like character and with like aims;
5 3. By diversifying the investments of the System so as to
6 minimize the risk of large losses, unless under the circumstances it
7 is clearly prudent not to do so; and
8 4. In accordance with the laws, documents and instruments
9 governing the System.
10 B. The Board of Trustees of the Teachers’ Retirement System of
11 Oklahoma may invest the assets of the System in real property owned
12 or to be acquired by the State of Oklahoma. It is further
13 authorized to acquire, exchange, and grant any real property under
14 its jurisdiction as is necessary to carry out the investment in the
15 real property. The Board of Trustees of the Teachers’ Retirement
16 System of Oklahoma is authorized to invest not more than ten percent
17 (10%) of the total value of assets of the System in connection with
18 such investments. Limitations on investment of the assets of the
19 System provided herein shall be determined as of the date of its
20 making or acquisition.
21 C. The Board of Trustees may procure insurance indemnifying the
22 members of the Board of Trustees from personal loss or
23 accountability from liability resulting from a member’s action or
24 inaction as a member of the Board.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 788
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 D. The Board of Trustees may establish an investment committee.
2 The investment committee shall be composed of not more than five (5)
3 members of the Board of Trustees appointed by the chairman of the
4 Board of Trustees. The committee shall make recommendations to the
5 full Board of Trustees on all matters related to the choice of
6 custodians and managers of the assets of the System, on the
7 establishment of investment and fund management guidelines, and in
8 planning future investment policy. The committee shall have no
9 authority to act on behalf of the Board of Trustees in any
10 circumstances whatsoever. No recommendation of the committee shall
11 have effect as an action of the Board of Trustees nor take effect
12 without the approval of the Board of Trustees as provided by law.
13 E. The Board of Trustees may retain qualified investment
14 managers to provide for the investment of the monies of the System.
15 The investment managers shall be chosen by a solicitation of
16 proposals on a competitive bid basis pursuant to standards set by
17 the Board of Trustees. Subject to the overall investment guidelines
18 set by the Board of Trustees, the investment managers shall have
19 full discretion in the management of those monies of the System
20 allocated to the investment managers. The Board of Trustees shall
21 manage those monies not specifically allocated to the investment
22 managers. The monies of the System allocated to the investment
23 managers shall be actively managed by the investment managers, which
24 may include selling investments and realizing losses if such action
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 789
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 is c onsidered advantageous to longer term return maximization.
2 Because of the total return objective, no distinction shall be made
3 for management and performance evaluation purposes between realized
4 and unrealized capital gains and losses.
5 F. Funds and revenues for investment by the investment managers
6 or the Board of Trustees shall be placed with a custodian selected
7 by the Board of Trustees. The custodian shall be a bank or trust
8 company offering pension fund master trustee and master custodial
9 services. The custodian shall be chosen by a solicitation of
10 proposals on a competitive bid basis pursuant to standards set by
11 the Board of Trustees. In compliance with the investment policy
12 guidelines of the Board of Trustees, the custodian bank or trust
13 company shall be contractually responsible for ensuring that all
14 monies of the System are invested in income-producing investment
15 vehicles at all times. If a custodian bank or trust company has not
16 received direction from the investment managers of the System as to
17 the investment of the monies of the System in specific investment
18 vehicles, the custodian bank or trust company shall be contractually
19 responsible to the Board of Trustees for investing the monies in
20 appropriately collateralized short-term interest-bearing investment
21 vehicles.
22 G. By November 1, 1988, and prior to August 1 of each year
23 thereafter, the Board of Trustees shall develop a written investment
24 plan for the System.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 790
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 H. The Board of Trustees shall compile a quarterly financial
2 report of all the funds of the System on a fiscal year basis. The

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 791

1 all state retirement systems. The report shall be submitted to the

17 academic year for full-time students who have earned ninety (90) or

20 subsection to any qualified student shall not exceed Five Thousand

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 804
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

2 who is enrolled in or has completed an internship or student

5 (12) credit hours; or
6 3. An undergraduate student who is a teacher education major
7 with an approved reasonable accommodation from Student Accessibility
8 Services at the institution due to a documented disability and who
9 has been approved to take less than twelve (12) credit hours.

16 Program. The committee shall prepare and, utilizing the centralized
17 filing system provided for in Section 378 of this act,
18 electronically deliver a report on the program to the Legislature on
19 or before July 1 of each year.
20 E. Each scholarship participant, prior to entry into the
21 Inspired to Teach Program, shall have agreed to teach in an Oklahoma
22 public prekindergarten through twelfth grade school for a minimum of
23 five (5) consecutive years upon graduation and licensure as a
24 teacher. Any scholarship participant failing to maintain a 2.5
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 805
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 grad e point average throughout matriculation, changing to an
2 ineligible area of study, failing to meet satisfactory academic
3 progress standards, withdrawing, or otherwise leaving school, or
4 failing to meet requirements for licensure and certification to
5 teach as established by law shall be subject to disqualification
6 from the program.
7 F. The Oklahoma State Regents for Higher Education are
8 authorized to make employment incentive payments pursuant to the
9 provisions of this section to Inspired to Teach Program participants
10 who are employed as traditionally certified teachers in Oklahoma
11 public prekindergarten through twelfth grade schools following
12 graduation. Incentive payments may be awarded following each
13 consecutive year of satisfactory service as documented by the
14 employing school district, up to five (5) years of service for
15 scholarship recipients as provided in subsection A of this section,
16 upon graduation from an accredited Oklahoma teacher preparation
17 degree program. The maximum amount of employment incentive payments
18 for any qualified participant shall be Four Thousand Dollars
19 ($4,000.00) per year for up to five (5) years, not to exceed a total
20 of Twenty Thousand Dollars ($20,000.00) per participant.
21 G. The Oklahoma State Regents for Higher Education shall
22 require the execution of appropriate contracts with eligible
23 Inspired to Teach Program participants. Participants failing to
24 comply with the requirements of this section or failing to provide
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 806
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 curr ent contact information shall not be eligible for the employment
2 incentive payments provided for in this section. The Chancellor of
3 the Oklahoma State Regents for Higher Education, with the approval
4 of the State Regents, may contract with any other appropriate
5 organization or unit of government for the administration of the
6 provisions of this section.
7 H. If sufficient funds are not available for employment
8 incentive payments to qualified participants during any fiscal year,
9 the Chancellor may make reductions in the payments made to qualified
10 participants.
11 I. The Oklahoma State Regents for Higher Education may, at the
12 time an award is made on behalf of the Inspired to Teach Program,
13 set aside funds for the full commitment made to an eligible student.
14 J. The Oklahoma State Regents for Higher Education may utilize
15 Inspired to Teach Program designated funds for administration of the
16 program.

6 SECTION 271. AMENDATORY 70 O.S. 2021, Section 1210.407,

8 Section 1210.407. A. 1. There is hereby created a mechanism
9 for establishing criteria and a process for designation as a STEM
10 Community or STEM Region. The STEM Community or STEM Region is one
11 of several recommendations on improving Oklahoma Science,
12 Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) education in the
13 Governor’s Science and Technology Council’s “OneOklahoma” report.
14 2. The criteria for designation as a STEM Community or STEM
15 Region shall include a requirement that educators, administrators,
16 business leaders, students, parents, government officials, and
17 business and industry groups within a community or region create
18 awareness, promote partnerships with education and industry, develop
19 and execute action plans for improving STEM education and training,
20 identify and acquire the needed resources to improve STEM education
21 and training, and identify and accumulate STEM data within the
22 community or region, including but not limited to kindergarten
23 through twelfth grade test results, the number of STEM-related
24 degree holders in the local workforce, the number of STEM-related
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 814
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 degr ees conferred, and the number of STEM-related certifications or
2 credentials obtained. The region or community shall also develop a
3 plan to recognize and promote successes in both student and teacher
4 accomplishments in the STEM area within the community or region.
5 The concept of creating a STEM Community or STEM Region is
6 incorporated in the Department of Education’s STEM strategy and will
7 include and support STEM programs in many of the Department of
8 Career and Technology Education’s technology centers.
9 3. The process for designation as a STEM Community or STEM
10 Region shall require that state legislators initiate designation of
11 a STEM Community or STEM Region through an application process. The
12 final approval and designation of an Oklahoma STEM Community or STEM
13 Region shall reside with the Governor.
14 4. The application process shall be outlined by a subcommittee
15 of an existing STEM education or workforce committee or board, to be
16 identified by the Governor. The creation, sustainment, and
17 execution of the subcommittee shall be the responsibility of the
18 Department of Career and Technology Education. The application
19 process shall be based on criteria established by the subcommittee.
20 B. The subcommittee shall also study issues related to the
21 promotion and growth of STEM education and training in the state and
22 create specific recommendations to address the current and pending
23 shortage of highly skilled employees in the energy, agriculture and
24 biosciences, aerospace and defense, transportation and distribution,
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 815
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 and information technology and financial services sectors in
2 Oklahoma.

4 system provided for in Section 378 of this act, electronically
5 submit a report of its findings and recommendations to the Governor,
6 the President Pro Tempore of the Senate and the Speaker of the House
7 of Representatives on an annual basis.
8 SECTION 272. AMENDATORY 70 O.S. 2021, Section 1210.508C,
9 as last amended by Section 2, Chapter 297, O.S.L. 2025 (70 O.S.
10 Supp. 2025, Section 1210.508C), is amended to read as follows:
11 Section 1210.508C. A. To identify students who have
12 characteristics of dyslexia that lead to or cause reading
13 difficulty, each student enrolled in kindergarten and first, second,
14 and third grade in a public school in this state shall be screened
15 at the beginning, middle, and end of each school year for reading
16 skills including, but not limited to, phonological awareness,
17 decoding, fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension. A screening
18 instrument approved by the State Board of Education, in consultation
19 with the Commission for Educational Quality and Accountability and
20 the Secretary of Education, shall be utilized for the purposes of
21 this section. In determining which screening instrument to approve,
22 the State Board of Education, the Commission for Educational Quality
23 and Accountability, and the Secretary of Education shall take into
24 consideration at a minimum the following factors:
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 816
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 1. The time required to conduct the screening instrument with
2 the intention of minimizing the impact on instructional time;
3 2. The timeliness in reporting screening instrument results to
4 teachers, administrators, and parents and legal guardians of
5 students; and
6 3. The integration of the screening instrument into reading
7 curriculum.
8 B. Beginning in the 2025-2026 school year, the State Board of
9 Education shall approve no fewer than three screening instruments
10 for use at the beginning, middle, and end of the school year for
11 monitoring of progress and for measurement of reading skills as
12 required in subsection A of this section. The screening instruments
13 shall meet the following criteria:
14 1. Assess for phonological awareness, decoding, fluency,
15 vocabulary, and comprehension;
16 2. Document the validity and reliability of each assessment;
17 3. Can be used for identifying students who are at risk for
18 reading deficiency and progress monitoring throughout the school
19 year;
20 4. Can be used to assess students with disabilities and English
21 language learners; and
22 5. Accompanied by a data management system that provides
23 profiles of each student, class, grade level, and school building.
24 The profiles shall identify each student’s instructional point of
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 817
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 need and reading achievement level. The State Board shall also
2 determine other comparable reading assessments for diagnostic
3 purposes to be used for students at risk of reading failure.
4 C. 1. Exemptions to the screening requirements of this section
5 may be provided to students who have documented evidence that they
6 meet at least one of the following criteria as related to the
7 provision of classroom instruction:
8 a. the student participates in the Oklahoma Alternate
9 Assessment Program (OAAP) and is taught using
10 alternate methods,
11 b. the student’s primary expressive or receptive
12 communication is sign language,
13 c. the student’s primary form of written or read text is
14 Braille, or
15 d. the student’s primary expressive or receptive language
16 is not English, the student is identified as an
17 English learner using a state-approved identification
18 assessment, and the student has had less than one (1)
19 school year of instruction in an English-learner
20 program.
21 2. A public school that grants an exemption pursuant to
22 paragraph 1 of this subsection shall provide ongoing evidence of
23 student progression toward English language acquisition with the
24 same frequency as administration of screening assessments. Evidence
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 818
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 may include, but not be limited to, student progression toward OAAP
2 reading essential elements, proficiency in sign language and reading
3 comprehension, and proficiency in Braille and reading comprehension.
4 D. 1. Students who are administered a screening instrument
5 pursuant to subsection A of this section and are found not to be
6 meeting grade-level targets shall be provided a program of reading
7 instruction designed to enable students to acquire the appropriate
8 grade-level reading skills. The program of reading instruction
9 shall be based on scientific reading research and align with the
10 subject matter standards adopted by the State Board of Education. A
11 program of reading instruction shall include:
12 a. sufficient additional in-school instructional time for
13 the acquisition of phonological awareness, decoding,
14 fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension,
15 b. if necessary and if funding is available, tutorial
16 instruction after regular school hours, on Saturdays,
17 and during summer; however, such instruction may not
18 be counted toward the one-hundred-eighty-day or one-
19 thousand-eighty-hour school year required in Section
20 1-109 of this title,
21 c. assessments identified for diagnostic purposes and
22 periodic monitoring to measure the acquisition of
23 reading skills including, but not limited to,
24 phonological awareness, decoding, fluency, vocabulary,
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 819
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 and comprehension, as identified in the student’s
2 program of reading instruction,
3 d. high-quality instructional materials grounded in
4 scientifically based reading research, and
5 e. a means of providing every family of a student in
6 prekindergarten, kindergarten, and first, second, and
7 third grade access to free online evidence-based
8 literacy instruction resources to support the
9 student’s literacy development at home.
10 2. A student enrolled in kindergarten or first, second, or
11 third grade who exhibits a deficiency in reading at any time based
12 on the screening instrument administered pursuant to subsection A of
13 this section shall receive an individual reading intervention plan
14 no later than thirty (30) days after the identification of the
15 deficiency in reading. The reading intervention plan shall be
16 provided in addition to core reading instruction that is provided to
17 all students. The reading intervention plan shall:
18 a. describe the research-based reading intervention
19 services the student will receive to remedy the
20 deficiency in reading,
21 b. provide explicit and systematic instruction in
22 phonological awareness, decoding, fluency, vocabulary,
23 and comprehension, as applicable,
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 820
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 c. monitor the reading progress of each student’s reading
2 skills throughout the school year and adjust
3 instruction according to the student’s needs, and
4 d. continue until the student is determined to be meeting
5 grade-level targets in reading based on screening
6 instruments administered pursuant to subsection A of
7 this section or assessments identified for diagnostic
8 purposes and periodic monitoring pursuant to
9 subparagraph c of paragraph 1 of this subsection.
10 3. The reading intervention plan for each student identified
11 with a deficiency in reading shall be developed by a Student Reading
12 Proficiency Team and shall include supplemental instructional
13 services and supports. Each team shall be composed of:
14 a. the parent or legal guardian of the student,
15 b. the teacher assigned to the student who had
16 responsibility for reading instruction in that
17 academic year,
18 c. a teacher who is responsible for reading instruction
19 and is assigned to teach in the next grade level of
20 the student, and
21 d. a certified reading specialist or an individual with
22 advanced training or specialization in literacy
23 instruction, if one is available.

11 disabilities, and English language learners, as appropriate for the
12 following:
13 1. The statewide aggregate number and percentage of students in

21 3. The statewide aggregate number and percentage of students in
22 kindergarten through third grade who have successfully completed
23 their program of reading instruction and are reading on grade level
24

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 as d etermined by the results of screening instruments administered

20 SECTION 278. AMENDATORY 70 O.S. 2021, Section 2120, as
21 amended by Section 3, Chapter 18, O.S.L. 2022 (70 O.S. Supp. 2025,
22 Section 2120), is amended to read as follows:
23 Section 2120. A. As used in this act:
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 840
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 1. “Campus community” means students, administrators, faculty,
2 and staff at the public institution of higher education and their
3 invited guests;
4 2. “Harassment” means only that expression that is unwelcome,
5 so severe, pervasive, and subjectively and objectively offensive
6 that a student is effectively denied equal access to educational
7 opportunities or benefits provided by the public institution of
8 higher education;
9 3. “Materially and substantially disrupts” means when a person,
10 with the intent to or with knowledge of doing so, significantly
11 hinders another person’s or group’s expressive activity, prevents
12 the communication of the message, or prevents the transaction of the
13 business of a lawful meeting, gathering or procession by:
14 a. engaging in fighting, violent or other unlawful
15 behavior, or
16 b. physically blocking or using threats of violence to
17 prevent any person from attending, listening to,
18 viewing, or otherwise participating in an expressive
19 activity. Conduct that “materially disrupts” shall
20 not include conduct that is protected under the First
21 Amendment to the United States Constitution or Section
22 22 of Article 2 of the Oklahoma Constitution. Such
23 protected conduct includes but is not limited to
24 lawful protests in the outdoor areas of campus
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 841
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 generally accessible to the members of the public,
2 except during times when those areas have been
3 reserved in advance for other events, or minor, brief
4 or fleeting nonviolent disruptions of events that are
5 isolated and short in duration;
6 4. “Outdoor areas of campus” means the generally accessible
7 outside areas of campus where members of the campus community are
8 commonly allowed, such as grassy areas, walkways or other similar
9 common areas and does not include outdoor areas where access is
10 restricted from a majority of the campus community;
11 5. “Public institution of higher education” means any
12 institution within The Oklahoma State System of Higher Education or
13 technology center schools overseen by the State Board of Career and
14 Technology Education; and
15 6. “Student organization” means an officially recognized group
16 at a public institution of higher education, or a group seeking
17 official recognition, comprised of admitted students that receive or
18 are seeking to receive benefits through the public institution of
19 higher education.
20 B. Expressive activities protected under the provisions of this
21 section include but are not limited to any lawful verbal, written,
22 audio-visual or electronic means by which individuals may
23 communicate ideas to one another, including all forms of peaceful
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 842
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 asse mbly, protests, speeches and guest speakers, distribution of
2 literature, carrying signs, and circulating petitions.
3 C. 1. The outdoor areas of campuses of public institutions of
4 higher education in this state shall be deemed public forums for the
5 campus community, and public institutions of higher education shall
6 not create “free speech zones” or other designated areas of campus
7 outside of which expressive activities are prohibited. Public
8 institutions of higher education may maintain and enforce reasonable
9 time, place, and manner restrictions narrowly tailored in service of
10 a significant institutional interest only when such restrictions
11 employ clear, published, content- and viewpoint-neutral criteria and
12 provide for ample alternative means of expression. Any such
13 restrictions shall allow for members of the campus community to
14 spontaneously and contemporaneously assemble and distribute
15 literature.
16 2. Nothing in this subsection shall be interpreted as limiting
17 the right of student expression elsewhere on campus.
18 D. 1. Any person who wishes to engage in noncommercial
19 expressive activity on campus shall be permitted to do so freely, as
20 long as the person’s conduct is not unlawful and does not materially
21 and substantially disrupt the functioning of the public institutions
22 of higher education, subject only to the requirements of subsection
23 C of this section.
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 843
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. Nothing in this subsection shall prohibit public
2 institutions of higher education from maintaining and enforcing
3 reasonable time, place, and manner restrictions that are narrowly
4 tailored to serve a significant institutional interest only when
5 such restrictions employ clear, published, content- and viewpoint-
6 neutral criteria. Any such restrictions shall allow for members of
7 the campus community to spontaneously and contemporaneously
8 assemble, speak, and distribute literature.
9 3. Nothing in this subsection shall be interpreted as
10 preventing public institutions of higher education from prohibiting,
11 limiting or restricting expression that the First Amendment does not
12 protect or prohibiting harassment as defined by this section.
13 4. Nothing in this section shall enable individuals to engage
14 in conduct that intentionally, materially, and substantially
15 disrupts another person’s expressive activity if that activity is
16 occurring in a campus space reserved for that activity under the
17 exclusive use or control of a particular group.
18 E. Public institutions of higher education shall make public in
19 their handbooks, on their websites and through their orientation
20 programs for students the policies, regulations, and expectations of
21 students regarding free expression on campus consistent with this
22 section.
23 F. Public institutions of higher education shall develop
24 materials, programs, and procedures to ensure that those persons who
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 844
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 have responsibility for discipline or education of students,
2 including but not limited to administrators, campus police officers,
3 residence life officials and professors, understand the policies,
4 regulations, and duties of public institutions of higher education
5 regarding free expression on campus consistent with this section.
6 G. 1. Each public institution of higher education shall
7 publicly post on its website, as well as electronically submit to
8 the Governor, the Legislature, and the Chancellor of The Oklahoma
9 State System of Higher Education annually by December 31 utilizing
10 the centralized filing system provided for in Section 378 of this
11 act, a report that details the course of action implemented to be in
12 compliance with the requirements of this section. A report shall
13 also be given in the instance of any changes or updates to the
14 chosen course of action. The information required in the report
15 shall be:
16 a. accessible from the institution’s website home page by
17 use of not more than three links,
18 b. searchable by key words and phrases, and
19 c. accessible to the public without requiring
20 registration or use of a user name, password or
21 another user identification.
22 2. The report shall include:
23 a. a description of any barriers to or incidents of
24 disruption of free expression occurring on campus,
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 845
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 including but not limited to attempts to block or
2 prohibit speakers and investigations into students or
3 student organizations for their speech. The
4 description shall include the nature of each barrier
5 or incident, as well as what disciplinary action, if
6 any, was taken against members of the campus community
7 determined to be responsible for those specific
8 barriers or incidents involving students without
9 revealing those students’ personally identifiable
10 information, and
11 b. any other information each public institution of
12 higher education deems valuable for the public to
13 evaluate whether free expression rights for all
14 members of the campus community have been equally
15 protected and enforced consistent with this act.
16 3. If a public institution of higher education is sued for an
17 alleged violation of First Amendment rights, a supplementary report
18 with a copy of the complaint, or any amended complaint, shall be
19 electronically submitted to the Governor and the Legislature within
20 thirty (30) days utilizing the centralized filing system provided
21 for in Section 378 of this act.
22 H. Any person or student organization aggrieved by a violation
23 of this section may bring an action against the public institution
24 of higher education and its employees acting in their official
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 846
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 capa cities responsible for the violation and seek appropriate
2 relief, including but not limited to injunctive relief, monetary
3 damages, reasonable attorneys’ fees, and court costs. Any person or
4 student organization aggrieved by a violation of this section may
5 assert such violation as a defense or counter claim in any
6 disciplinary action or in any civil or administrative proceedings
7 brought against such student or student organization. Nothing in
8 this subsection shall be interpreted to limit any other remedies
9 available to any person or student organization.
10 I. A person shall be required to bring suit for violation of
11 this section no later than one year after the day the cause of
12 action occurs. For purposes of calculating the one-year limitation
13 period, each day that the violation persists and each day that a
14 policy in violation of this section remains in effect shall
15 constitute a new day that the cause of action has occurred.
16 J. If any provision of this section or any application of such
17 provision to any person or circumstance is held to be
18 unconstitutional, the remainder of the section and the application
19 of the provision to any other person or circumstance shall not be
20 affected.

1 Section 2632. A. To be eligible for an Oklahoma Tuition
