14 domiciled within this state, meet approved program and accreditation
15 standards as determined by the State Regents, and are fully
16 accredited by the Higher Learning Commission of the North Central
17 Association on Colleges and Schools or a national accrediting body
18 recognized by the United States Department of Education. To be
19 eligible, institutions must shall have been granted not-for-profit
20 status by the Internal Revenue Service and the U.S. Department of
21 Education prior to July 1, 2012. Additionally, eligible
22 institutions shall adhere to the complaint process policies and
23 procedures administered by the State Regents. The State Regents
24 shall review and take action, as authorized, on complaints
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 851
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 conc erning eligible institutions. The eligible institutions shall
2 include Family of Faith College, Mid-America Christian University,
3 Oklahoma Baptist University, Oklahoma Christian University, Oklahoma
4 City University, Oklahoma Wesleyan University, Oral Roberts
5 University, Southern Nazarene University, Southwestern Christian
6 University, the University of Tulsa, and Randall University.
7 E. If funds are not sufficient in the Oklahoma Tuition
8 Equalization Grant Trust Fund to provide grants for all eligible
9 applicants, the State Regents shall award grants on the basis of
10 need and take into consideration other grants and scholarships
11 received by an eligible applicant when awarding grants. Students
12 who have previously received a tuition equalization grant and who
13 continue to meet the requirements for eligibility shall have
14 absolute priority for continued financial support superior to any
15 student who is applying for a grant for the first time.
16 F. Students enrolling as first-time freshmen for the 2003-2004
17 school year shall be the first class of students eligible to apply
18 for Oklahoma Tuition Equalization Grants. In subsequent years, all
19 previously eligible students and the first-time enrolling freshmen
20 students shall be entitled to apply for a grant pursuant to this
21 section. Beginning in the 2007-2008 school year, all eligible
22 students shall be entitled to apply for a grant pursuant to this
23 section.
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 852
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 G. A grant provided by this section shall not be allowed for
2 courses or other postsecondary units taken in excess of the
3 requirements for completion of a baccalaureate program or taken more
4 than five (5) years after the student’s first grant is received.
5 The State Regents may award an Oklahoma Tuition Equalization Grant
6 for courses of postsecondary units taken more than five (5) years
7 after the student’s first grant is received only in hardship
8 circumstances. No Oklahoma Tuition Equalization Grant recipient may
9 receive benefits beyond a cumulative time period of five (5) years.
10 H. On or before February 1 after the first semester in which
11 grants are awarded pursuant to this act and every year thereafter,
12 the State Regents shall, utilizing the centralized filing system
13 provided for in Section 378 of this act, electronically provide a
14 report to the Governor, President Pro Tempore of the Senate, and the
15 Speaker of the House of Representatives to include, but not be
16 limited to the number of students awarded grants, grade point
17 average, persistence rates, degree completion rates, demographic

10 (e) It shall have the power to recommend to the Legislature

12 Inspector shall have the power to take custody of any records
13 necessary to the performance of the audit but shall minimize actual
14 physical removal of or denial of access to such records. At the
15 conclusion of the audit, the State Auditor and Inspector shall meet
16 with the president of the institution and the Audit Committee of the
17 board which governs the component audited to review the audit report
18 to be issued. The report, when issued, shall include any responses
19 to the audit which the president or the Audit Committee wishes to
20 have included and shall be electronically presented to the full
21 board, the Legislative Service Bureau, and the Oklahoma State
22 Regents for Higher Education with or in advance of the filing
23 required by Section 452.10 of Title 74 of the Oklahoma Statutes
24 utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in Section 378
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 873
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 of t his act. The cost of such audit shall be borne by the audited
2 entity and may be defrayed in whole or in part by any federal funds
3 available for that purpose.
4 D. Each board of regents shall require the employment of a
5 sufficient number of internal auditors to meet the board’s fiduciary
6 responsibilities. Internal audits shall be conducted in accordance
7 with the provisions of Sections 228 and 229 of Title 74 of the
8 Oklahoma Statutes. The internal auditors shall submit a report
9 directly and simultaneously to the audit committee of the board and
10 the president of the institution; all members of the board of
11 regents governing the institution, however, shall receive all
12 internal audit reports and the board of regents shall, at least
13 annually, review and prescribe the plan of work to be performed by
14 the internal auditors.
15 E. Any person who alters or destroys records needed for the
16 performance of an audit or causes or directs a subordinate to do
17 such acts shall be guilty of a Class D1 felony offense punishable by
18 imprisonment as provided for in subsections B through F of Section
19 20N of Title 21 of the Oklahoma Statutes, or by a fine of not more
20 than Twenty Thousand Dollars ($20,000.00), or by both such fine and
21 imprisonment. Such person shall also be subject to immediate
22 removal from office or employment.

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 874

1 Section 3954. A. The Oklahoma State Regents for Higher

12 an enterprise of a like character and with like aims;
13 2. By diversifying the investments of the Trust Funds so as to

18 B. There are hereby created the Oklahoma Securities Commission
19 and the Department of Securities. The Commission shall be the
20 policy making and governing authority of the Department, shall
21 appoint the Administrator and shall be responsible for the
22 enforcement of the Oklahoma Uniform Securities Act of 2004.
23 C. 1. The Commission shall consist of five (5) members,
24 including the State Banking Commissioner who shall serve as an ex
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 887
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 offi cio voting member. Four (4) members shall be appointed by the

4 from a list of five nominees submitted by the Oklahoma Bar
5 Association; one member shall be an active officer of a bank or
6 trust company operating in the State of Oklahoma appointed from a
7 list of five nominees submitted by the Oklahoma Bankers Association;

9 a list of five nominees submitted by the Oklahoma Society of
10 Certified Public Accountants; and one member shall be a resident of
11 this state actively engaged in the securities industry with the
12 qualifications set forth in paragraph 3 of this subsection.
13 2. Except for appointment of the member engaged in the

16 is registered as a broker-dealer, agent, investment adviser, or

1 G. The Administrator shall be a person of good moral character,

6 the salary of the Deputy Administrator. The Deputy Administrator

8 the Administrator.
9 L. The Commission and the Securities Department shall be
10 assigned offices in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, by the Office of
11 Management and Enterprise Services, and all records of the
12 Commission and Department shall be kept in those offices, unless and
13 until transferred to the Records Management Division of the Oklahoma
14 Department of Libraries.
15 M. 1. Neither the Administrator nor any employee of the
16 Department, during their respective terms of employment, shall serve
17 as a director, officer, shareholder, member, partner, agent, or
18 employee of any person who, during the period of such
19 Administrator’s or employee’s employment with the Department:
20 a. was licensed or applied for registration as a broker-
21 dealer, agent, investment adviser, or investment
22 adviser representative under this act, or
23 b. applied for or secured the registration of securities
24 under the Oklahoma Uniform Securities Act of 2004.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 895
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. Nothing in paragraph 1 of this subsection shall prohibit the
2 holding, purchasing, or selling of any securities by the
3 Administrator or any employee of the Department in accordance with
4 regulations adopted by the Commission for the purpose of protecting
5 the public interest and avoiding conflicts of interest.
6 3. Nothing contained in paragraph 1 of this subsection shall
7 prohibit the holding, purchasing, or selling of any securities of
8 any issuer described in subparagraph b of paragraph 1 of this
9 subsection of this section by the Administrator if either:
10 a. the Administrator together with his or her spouse, or
11 minor children, owns less than one percent (1%) of any
12 class of outstanding securities of any such issuer so
13 long as such securities are not purchased in an
14 initial public offering, or
15 b. such securities are held or purchased through a
16 management account or trust administered by a bank or
17 trust company authorized to do business in this state
18 that has sole investment discretion regarding the
19 holding, purchasing or selling of such securities and
20 the Administrator or employee did not, directly or
21 indirectly, advise, counsel, or command the holding,
22 purchasing or selling of any securities or furnish any
23 information relating to any such securities to such
24 bank or trust company and further, such account or
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 896
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 trust does not at any time have more than ten percent
2 (10%) of its total assets invested in the securities

4 of the outstanding securities of any class of
5 securities of any one issuer.
6 N. The Oklahoma Uniform Securities Act of 2004 does not create
7 or diminish a privilege or exemption that exists at common law, by
8 statute or rule, or otherwise.
9 O. The Administrator may develop and implement investor
10 education initiatives to inform the public about investing in
11 securities, with particular emphasis on the prevention and detection

18 with the securities industry, to develop and implement investor

21 a registrant in an investor education program.

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 916

1 D. An agency or official of the executive branch shall not
2 enter into a contingency fee contract that provides for the private
3 attorney or firm to receive an aggregate contingency fee that
4 exceeds:
5 1. Twenty-five percent (25%) of that portion of any amount
6 recovered that is Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000.00) or less;
7 2. Twenty percent (20%) of that portion of any amount recovered
8 that is more than Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000.00) but less than
9 or equal to Fifteen Million Dollars ($15,000,000.00);
10 3. Fifteen percent (15%) of that portion of any amount
11 recovered that is more than Fifteen Million Dollars ($15,000,000.00)
12 but less than or equal to Twenty Million Dollars ($20,000,000.00);
13 4. Ten percent (10%) of that portion of any amount recovered
14 that is more than Twenty Million Dollars ($20,000,000.00) but less
15 than or equal to Twenty-five Million Dollars ($25,000,000.00); and
16 5. Five percent (5%) of that portion of any amount recovered
17 that is more than Twenty-five Million Dollars ($25,000,000.00).
18 E. Notwithstanding subsection D of this section, the total fee
19 payable to all retained private attorneys in any contingency fee

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 F. The Attorney General shall develop a standard clause for
2 inclusion in every contract for contingent fee attorney services
3 that shall be used in all cases, describing in detail what is
4 expected of both the contracted private attorney and the state
5 including, but not limited to, the requirements as provided in this
6 subsection. The state shall not enter into a contract for
7 contingency fee attorney services that does not incorporate such
8 requirements:
9 1. The government attorneys shall retain complete control over
10 the course and conduct of the case;
11 2. A government attorney with supervisory authority shall be
12 personally involved in oversight of the case;
13 3. The government attorneys shall retain veto power over any
14 decision made by outside counsel related to the case;
15 4. Any defendant in the case may contact the lead government
16 attorneys directly, without having to confer with outside counsel;
17 5. A government attorney with supervisory authority for the
18 case shall attend all settlement conferences; and
19 6. Decisions regarding settlement of the case shall be reserved
20 exclusively to the discretion of the government attorneys and the
21 state.
22 G. Copies of any executed contingency fee contract with the
23 private attorney shall be posted on the Attorney General’s website
24 for public inspection within five (5) business days after the date
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 918
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 the contract is executed and shall remain posted on the website for
2 the duration of the contingency fee contract including any
3 extensions or amendments to the contract. Any payment of
4 contingency fees shall be posted on the Attorney General’s website
5 within fifteen (15) days after the payment of the contingency fees
6 to the private attorney or law firm and shall remain posted on the
7 website for at least three hundred sixty-five (365) days after the
8 payment is made.
9 H. Any private attorney or law firm under contract to provide
10 services to the state on a contingency fee basis shall from the
11 inception of the contract until at least four (4) years after the
12 contract expires or is terminated, maintain detailed current records
13 including documentation of all expenses, disbursements, charges,
14 credits, underlying receipts and invoices and other financial
15 transactions related to the attorney services. The private attorney
16 or law firm shall make all such records available for inspection and
17 copying upon request of the Attorney General. In addition, the
18 private attorney or law firm shall maintain detailed contemporaneous
19 time records for the attorneys and paralegals working on the matter
20 in increments of no greater than one-tenth (1/10) of an hour and
21 shall promptly provide such records to the Attorney General upon
22 request.
23 I. Before entering into a contract for legal representation by
24 one or more private attorneys, an agency or official of the
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 919
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 exec utive branch shall furnish a copy of the proposed contract to
2 the Attorney General and notify the Attorney General of the
3 following:
4 1. The nature and scope of the representation including, but
5 not limited to, a description of any pending or anticipated
6 litigation or of the transaction requiring representation;
7 2. The reason or reasons for not obtaining the representation
8 from an attorney employed by the agency or official, if an attorney
9 is employed by the agency or official;
10 3. The reason or reasons for not obtaining the representation
11 from the Attorney General by contract;
12 4. The anticipated cost of the representation including the
13 following:
14 a. the basis for or method of calculation of the fee
15 including, when applicable, the hourly rate for each
16 attorney, paralegal, legal assistant, or other person
17 who will perform services under the contract, and
18 b. the basis for and method of calculation of any
19 expenses which will be reimbursed by the agency or
20 official under the contract;
21 5. An estimate of the anticipated duration of the contract;
22 6. The past or present relationship, if any, between such
23 attorney, law firm or any partner or other principal in such law
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 920
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 firm and the state agency or state agent proposing to enter into the
2 contract;
3 7. If the contract contemplates that all or part of the fee is
4 contingent on the outcome of the legal proceeding, the reasons the
5 contingent fee arrangement is believed to be in the state’s interest
6 and any efforts undertaken to obtain private counsel on a
7 noncontingent fee basis; and
8 8. The justification for the determination that the selection
9 of a contract for legal representation by one or more private
10 attorneys or firms was made based on the ability of the private

13 J. After the approval of the contract by the Attorney General
14 for legal representation by one or more private attorneys or law

18 K. 1. Before entering into a contract for legal representation
19 by one or more private attorneys or firms where the agency has
20 reason to believe that the case, transaction or matter will equal or
21 exceed Twenty Thousand Dollars ($20,000.00) or after employment when
22 it becomes apparent that the case, transaction or matter will equal
23 or exceeds Twenty Thousand Dollars ($20,000.00), an agency or
24 official of the executive branch shall obtain the approval of the
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 921
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Atto rney General when the total cost including fees and expenses, of
2 all contracts relating to the same case, transaction, or matter will
3 equal or exceed Twenty Thousand Dollars ($20,000.00).
4 2. Before entering into a contract for legal representation by
5 one or more private attorneys or firms to initiate a legal action on
6 behalf of the state where the agency has reason to believe that the
7 total cost of the case, transaction or matter including fees and
8 expenses will equal or exceed One Million Dollars ($1,000,000.00),
9 an agency or official of the executive branch shall initiate a
10 request for proposal from at least three qualified private attorneys
11 or firms, when possible, engaged in providing such services. Notice
12 of the request for proposal shall be published on the Attorney
13 General’s website. The request for proposal shall solicit a
14 billable hourly rate, regardless of whether a contingency fee is
15 ultimately agreed upon, and shall specify the importance of price,
16 quality, ability and experience. The selection of a contract for
17 legal representation by one or more private attorneys or firms shall
18 be made using the criteria established in the request for proposal
19 and shall be based on the response to the request which is the most
20 economical and provides the most competent service which furthers
21 the best interests of the state. Most economical and most competent
22 shall not be construed to mean the least expensive proposal.
23 3. Any amendment, modification or extension of a contract
24 which, had it been a part of the original contract would have
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 922
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 requ ired approval by the Attorney General, shall also require
2 approval by the Attorney General.
3 L. After entering into a contract for legal representation by
4 one or more private attorneys or firms where the agency has reason
5 to believe that the case, transaction or matter will equal or exceed
6 One Million Dollars ($1,000,000.00), an agency or official of the
7 executive branch shall, utilizing the centralized filing system
8 provided for in Section 378 of this act, electronically submit a
9 copy of the contract to the Legislative Oversight Committee
10 overseeing the operations of the Legislative Office of Fiscal
11 Transparency (LOFT) along with the following:
12 1. A description of the litigation or of the transaction
13 requiring representation;
14 2. The reason or reasons for not obtaining the representation
15 from an attorney employed by the agency or official;
16 3. The justification for selecting an attorney or firm
17 contracted to represent the state; and
18 4. An estimate of the anticipated duration of the contract.
19 M. A settlement agreement shall not contemplate the ultimate
20 use and destination of recovered funds unless done in accordance
21 with paragraphs 11 and 12 of Section 18b of this title.
22 N. Within ten (10) days of an agency or official of the
23 executive branch entering into a settlement agreement where a
24 private attorney or firm was hired on a contingency fee contract and
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 923
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 the settlement was equal to or greater than One Million Dollars
2 ($1,000,000.00), the agency or official of the executive branch

4 Committee with oversight of the operations of the Legislative Office
5 of Fiscal Transparency (LOFT), unless otherwise postponed by LOFT.
6 O. When an agency or official of the executive branch enters
7 into a contract for professional legal services pursuant to this
8 section, the agency shall also comply with the applicable provisions

10 P. The provisions of this section shall not apply to the
11 Oklahoma Indigent Defense System created pursuant to Section 1355 et
12 seq. of Title 22 of the Oklahoma Statutes.
13 Q. Upon request of an agency or official of the executive

17 this section if an exemption is deemed to be in the best interest of
18 the state. Such exemption shall be issued at their discretion, in
19 writing and by unanimous consent, and shall be submitted to LOFT.
20 R. By February 1 of each year, the Attorney General shall,

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

14 Section 51.2b. A. This section shall be known and may be cited
15 as the “Oklahoma School Security Grant Program Act”.
16 B. The Office of Homeland Security shall solicit proposals for
17 and make grants for the enhancement of campus security at
18 institutions of higher learning, technology center schools, public
19 schools, and private schools.
20 C. The goals and objectives of the Oklahoma School Security
21 Grant Program are to:
22 1. Increase the awareness of the public and educational
23 institutions of the risks, threats, and vulnerabilities of school
24 campuses as well as mitigation strategies;
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 928
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. Incentivize participation in school security training
2 programs designed to assess campus risks, threats, and
3 vulnerabilities;
4 3. Provide assistance to institutions of higher learning,
5 technology center schools, public schools, and private schools
6 initiating or implementing school security plans, programs, and
7 activities; and
8 4. Build upon the success of the pilot education grant program
9 established by the Office of Homeland Security.
10 D. The Office of Homeland Security shall determine grant
11 project criteria and establish a process for the consideration of
12 proposals. The proposals shall be considered on a statewide
13 competitive basis among peer institutions. To be eligible for an
14 Oklahoma School Security Grant Program award, an institution of
15 higher learning, technology center school, public school, or private
16 school shall:
17 1. Complete a risk and vulnerability assessment conducted by
18 the Oklahoma School Security Institute or a nationally qualified
19 risk and vulnerability assessor; and
20 2. Agree to expend grant funds on items recommended by the risk
21 and vulnerability assessment and/or or to provide de-escalation and
22 behavioral threat assessment and management training to employees.
23 Recommended items eligible for grant fund expenditures may include,
24 but shall not be limited to, physical security enhancements such as
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 929
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 came ras, gates, lighting, locks, doors, windows, security
2 geofencing, and ballistic storm shelters.
3 E. Each year the Office of Homeland Security shall prepare an
4 annual report on the Oklahoma School Security Grant Program and,
5 utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in Section 378
6 of this act, submit the report electronically to the Governor, the
7 President Pro Tempore of the Senate, and the Speaker of the House of
8 Representatives. The report shall include a list of the recipients
9 of Oklahoma School Security Grant Program awards and information on
10 how grant funds were used during the previous year.

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 934

13 most cost-effective and reasonable modes of travel for single trips

15 mileage reimbursement; and

17 in Section 378 of this act, electronically provide, upon the request
18 of the Governor, the President Pro Tempore of the Senate, or the

10 for exemption and, as applicable, detailed comparison of the
11 acquisition with comparable items, any identified cost savings
12 resulting from the acquisition and a description of benefits to the
13 state. The State Purchasing Director shall take no action under the
14 provisions of this subsection prior to such public posting.
15 B. Competitively bid acquisitions shall be awarded to the
16 lowest and best, or best value, bidder, or bidders.
17 C. Bids for an amount requiring submission of requisitions to
18 the Purchasing Division shall be evaluated by the Purchasing
19 Division and the state agency receiving the acquisition. At a
20 minimum, cost and technical expertise shall be considered in
21 determining the lowest and best, or best value, bid. Further, the
22 state agency shall present its evaluation and recommendation to the
23 State Purchasing Director. A documented evaluation report
24 containing the evaluations of the Purchasing Division or the state
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 964
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 agen cy shall be completed prior to the contract award and such
2 report shall be a matter of public record.
3 D. Except as otherwise specifically provided by law, the
4 acquisition of food items or food products by a state agency from a
5 public trust created pursuant to Sections 176 through 180.56 of
6 Title 60 of the Oklahoma Statutes shall comply with competitive
7 bidding requirements of this section.
8 E. Cooperative contracts shall not be utilized unless the
9 purchasing cooperative and its affiliated suppliers have complied
10 with competitive bid requirements of the Oklahoma Central Purchasing
11 Act and associated rules.
12 F. Notwithstanding any provision of the Oklahoma Central
13 Purchasing Act, in all cases where federal granted funds are
14 involved, the federal laws, rules and regulations thereto shall
15 govern to the extent necessary to inure to the benefit of such funds
16 to this state.
17 G. A court order requiring an acquisition by a state agency,
18 whether or not such state agency is subject to the Oklahoma Central
19 Purchasing Act, shall not invalidate competitive bidding procedures
20 required by this section if such court order does not specify a
21 specific supplier. Any such acquisition shall comply with
22 competitive bid procedures.

11 B. STATE AGENCIES
12 1. Except as otherwise provided by law, the State Auditor and
13 Inspector shall audit at least once every two (2) fiscal years the
14 books and accounts of all state agencies whose duty it is to
15 collect, disburse or manage funds of the state. The State Auditor
16 and Inspector shall audit a state agency each fiscal year if that
17 state agency is required to be audited on an annual basis pursuant
18 to the federal Single Audit Act of 1984, as amended, 31 U.S.C.,
19 Section 7501 et seq. If the state agency is audited only once every
20 two (2) fiscal years, the audit shall cover both fiscal years.
21 2. Except as otherwise provided by law, the scope of audits
22 performed by the State Auditor and Inspector shall include all funds
23 collected, disbursed, or managed by a state agency including, but
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 982
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 not limited to, all special, revolving, depository, canteen, or
2 other nonstate funds.
3 3. As used in this section, “state agency” means every agency,
4 board, or commission included in the primary government of the State
5 of Oklahoma. For purposes of this paragraph, the primary government
6 of the State of Oklahoma includes all agencies, boards, and
7 commissions included in the primary government in the State of
8 Oklahoma Comprehensive Annual Financial Report. The agencies,
9 boards, and commissions included in the primary government of the
10 State of Oklahoma shall be determined using criteria set by the
11 Governmental Accounting Standards Board.
12 4. As used in this subsection, “audit” means any of the
13 following:
14 a. “financial audit”, which means an audit of financial
15 statements in order to express an opinion on the
16 fairness with which they are presented in conformity
17 with generally accepted accounting principles or any
18 other comprehensive basis of accounting, as defined by
19 the American Institute of Certified Public
20 Accountants’ Professional Standards, latest revised
21 edition. Financial audits must shall be conducted in
22 accordance with auditing standards generally accepted
23 in the United States and the standards applicable to
24 financial audits contained in Government Auditing
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 983
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Standards, latest revised edition, issued by the
2 Comptroller General of the United States,
3 b. “operational audit”, which means an audit conducted in
4 accordance with applicable Government Auditing
5 Standards, the purpose of which is to evaluate
6 management’s performance in administering assigned
7 responsibilities in accordance with applicable laws,
8 administrative rules, and other policies and
9 guidelines and to determine the extent to which the
10 internal control, as designed and placed in operation,
11 promotes and encourages the achievement of
12 management’s control objectives in the categories of
13 compliance, reliability of financial records and
14 reports, and safeguarding of assets,
15 c. “performance audit”, which means an audit of a
16 program, activity, or function of a state agency
17 conducted in accordance with applicable Government
18 Auditing Standards. The term includes, but is not
19 limited to, an audit to assess program, activity, or
20 function effectiveness, economy and efficiency,
21 internal control, or compliance,
22 d. “special or investigative audit”, which means an audit
23 with respect to a particular situation which may be,
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 984
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 but is not required to be, conducted in accordance
2 with applicable Government Auditing Standards,
3 e. any other type of engagement conducted in accordance
4 with Government Auditing Standards, and
5 f. engagements not conducted in accordance with
6 Government Auditing Standards, when engagements
7 involve state agencies that collect less than Three
8 Million Dollars ($3,000,000.00) annually.
9 C. GUBERNATORIAL REQUEST
10 Whenever called upon to do so by the Governor, it shall be the
11 duty of the State Auditor and Inspector to examine the books and
12 accounts of any officer of the state or any of the officer’s
13 predecessors. The cost of the audit shall be borne by the entity to
14 be audited.

18 year.

24 the audit of the financial records, performance measures, and
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 985
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 comp liance with state or federal statutes and rules, and compliance

4 2. The State Auditor and Inspector shall examine and file a

17 using a risk-based approach. The audit may include, but shall not
18 be limited to, the audit of the financial records, performance
19 measures, and compliance with any state or federal statutes and
20 rules, and compliance with any regulations of state or federal
21 programs. The expense of the audits shall be paid by the Department

21 be made by the county so examined.
22 J. AUDITORS
23 The State Auditor and Inspector shall have power to employ
24 auditors. No auditor shall examine the books or records of the
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 987
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 coun ty of the auditor’s residence in counties of under two hundred
2 thousand (200,000) population according to the most recent Federal
3 Decennial Census. The State Auditor and Inspector may employ on an
4 as-needed basis only, legal counsel to carry out the statutory
5 duties of the Office of the State Auditor and Inspector.
6 K. EXAMINATION OF LEVIES
7 It shall be the duty of the State Auditor and Inspector to
8 examine all levies to raise public revenue to see that they are made
9 according to law and constitutional provisions. The State Auditor
10 and Inspector shall have the power to order all excessive or
11 erroneous lines (levies) to be corrected by the proper officers, and
12 shall report any irregularities to the Governor, the Speaker of the
13 House of Representatives and the President Pro Tempore of the
14 Senate.
15 L. PETITION AUDITS
16 1. The State Auditor and Inspector shall audit the books and
17 records of any subdivision of the State of Oklahoma upon petition
18 signed by the requisite number of voters registered in the
19 subdivision and meeting the requirements set out in this subsection.
20 2. The petition must shall contain the number of signatures
21 equivalent to ten percent (10%) of the registered voters of the
22 subdivision as determined by the county election board or, if the
23 county election board determines that the number of registered
24 voters in the subdivision cannot be determined due to boundary lines
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 988
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 not conforming to precinct lines, the required number of petitioners
2 shall be twenty-five percent (25%) of the total number of persons
3 voting in the last general election. If the subdivision is a public
4 trust, the required number of petitioners shall be the same as those
5 required for an audit of its beneficiary. The appropriate county
6 election board shall provide the number of signatures so required
7 upon request.
8 3. The petition shall be in the form of an affidavit wherein
9 the signatory shall declare upon oath or affirmation that the
10 information given is true and correct and that he or she is a
11 citizen of the entity to be audited. The petition shall clearly
12 state that falsely signing shall constitute perjury. It shall
13 include the signature of the individual, the name of the signatory
14 in printed form, the individual’s residential address, the date of
15 signing, the public entity to be audited and the anticipated range
16 of the cost of the audit provided by the State Auditor and
17 Inspector.
18 4. Any person desiring to petition for an audit shall list the
19 areas, items or concerns they want to be audited, and request from
20 the State Auditor and Inspector the anticipated range of cost of the
21 audit. Within thirty (30) days from the receipt of the request, the
22 State Auditor and Inspector shall mail a petition form to the person
23 requesting the information which shall state the anticipated range
24 of the cost and the items or concerns to be audited. The
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 989
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 circ ulators of the petition shall have thirty (30) days from the
2 date the petition is mailed by the State Auditor and Inspector to
3 obtain the requisite number of signatures and return it to the State
4 Auditor and Inspector.
5 5. Upon collection of the required number of signatures, the
6 person desiring the audit shall present the signed petitions to the
7 State Auditor and Inspector. Within thirty (30) days of receipt of
8 the petitions, the State Auditor and Inspector shall present the
9 petitions to the county election board located in the county in
10 which the subdivision is located.
11 6. The county election board shall determine whether the
12 signers of the petition are registered voters of the county in which
13 the subdivision to be audited is located and whether the petition
14 has the requisite number of signatures of such registered voters.
15 The county election board shall certify the petition as having the
16 required number of signatures or as failing to have the required
17 number of signatures and return it to the State Auditor and
18 Inspector.
19 7. The cost of the audit shall be borne by the public entity
20 audited. Upon notification by the State Auditor and Inspector of
21 receipt of the petition, certified by the county election board as
22 having the required number of signatures, the public entity shall
23 encumber funds in an amount specified by the State Auditor and
24 Inspector, which shall be within the range of anticipated cost
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 990
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 stat ed on the petition from any funds not otherwise specifically
2 appropriated or allocated. Payment for the audit from such
3 encumbered funds shall be made as work progresses, and final payment
4 shall be made on or before its publication.
5 8. The names of the signers of any petition shall be
6 confidential and neither the State Auditor and Inspector, the county
7 election board nor the county treasurer may release them to any
8 other person or entity except upon an order from a court of
9 competent jurisdiction.
10 M. PENALTIES FOR NONPAYMENT
11 Except as otherwise provided by law, the cost of any services
12 provided by the State Auditor and Inspector or as specified in an
13 audit contract shall be borne by the entity or fund audited and
14 shall be due and payable upon receipt of progress billing during the
15 course of an audit. Any such costs not paid within ninety (90) days
16 of the date of receipt of billing shall incur a penalty of Ten
17 Dollars ($10.00) per day for each day from the date of receipt of
18 billing.

17 Educational Authority Board.

19 1. “State agency” means any constitutionally or statutorily
20 created state board, commission, or department, including the
21 Legislature and the Courts;
22 2. State agencies are authorized to enter into contracts and
23 agreements for the payment of food and lodging expenses as may be
24 necessary for employees or other persons who are performing
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1009
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 subs tantial and necessary services to the state by attending

6 for the employees and the cost for food and lodging for each
7 employee shall not exceed the total daily rate as provided in the

8 SECTION 328. AMENDATORY 74 O.S. 2021, Section 500.18, as
9 last amended by Section 1, Chapter 325, O.S.L. 2022 (74 O.S. Supp.
10 2025, Section 500.18), is amended to read as follows:
11 Section 500.18. A. Except for members of the Legislature, the
12 Governor, and the Lieutenant Governor, provisions of Sections 500.1
13 through 500.18 of this title shall be mandatory for all officials
14 and employees of all departments, boards, commissions, and
15 institutions of the state, regardless of the provisions of any other
16 act of the Legislature, except as provided by this section. The
17 enactment of any measure in the future providing for travel
18 reimbursement of state officers and employees on the basis of
19 “actual and necessary” expenses or in any other manner inconsistent
20 with Sections 500.1 through 500.18 of this title shall be deemed to
21 provide for reimbursement in accordance with Sections 500.1 through
22 500.18 of this title unless a contrary intent is explicitly
23 expressed in this section. Sections 500.1 through 500.18 of this
24 title shall not apply, however, to travel reimbursements made by
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1019
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 poli tical subdivisions of this state, except as otherwise provided
2 by law.
3 B. The agencies listed below are authorized certain exceptions
4 and/or or exemptions to the provisions of Sections 500.1 through
5 500.18 of this title to the extent specified:

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1050
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

5 3. By diversifying the investments of the System so as to
6 minimize the risk of large losses, unless under the circumstances it
7 is clearly prudent not to do so; and
8 4. In accordance with the laws, documents and instruments
9 governing the System.
10 B. The Board of Trustees may procure insurance indemnifying the
11 members of the Board of Trustees from personal loss or
12 accountability from liability resulting from a member’s action or
13 inaction as a member of the Board of Trustees.

15 The investment committee shall be composed of not more than five (5)
16 members of the Board of Trustees appointed by the chairman of the
17 Board of Trustees. The committee shall make recommendations to the
18 full Board of Trustees on all matters related to the choice of
19 custodians and managers of the assets of the System, on the
20 establishment of investment and fund management guidelines, and in
21 planning future investment policy. The committee shall have no
22 authority to act on behalf of the Board of Trustees in any

24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1051
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 have effect as an action of the Board of Trustees nor take effect
2 without the approval of the Board of Trustees as provided by law.
3 D. The Board of Trustees shall retain qualified investment
4 managers to provide for the investment of the monies of the System.
5 The investment managers shall be chosen by a solicitation of
6 proposals on a competitive bid basis pursuant to standards set by
7 the Board of Trustees. Subject to the overall investment guidelines
8 set by the Board of Trustees, the investment managers shall have
9 full discretion in the management of those monies of the System
10 allocated to the investment managers. The Board of Trustees shall
11 manage those monies not specifically allocated to the investment
12 managers. The monies of the System allocated to the investment
13 managers shall be managed by the investment managers, which may
14 include selling investments and realizing losses if such action is
15 considered advantageous to longer term return maximization. Because
16 of the total return objective, no distinction shall be made for
17 management and performance evaluation purposes between realized and
18 unrealized capital gains and losses.
19 E. Funds and revenues for investment by the investment managers
20 or the Board of Trustees shall be placed with a custodian selected
21 by the Board of Trustees. The custodian shall be a bank or trust
22 company offering pension fund master trustee and master custodial
23 services. The custodian shall be chosen by a solicitation of
24 proposals on a competitive basis pursuant to standards set by the
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1052
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Boar d of Trustees. In compliance with the investment policy

5 I. The Board shall distribute the corpus and income of the
6 System to the members and their beneficiaries in accordance with the
7 System’s laws and rules and regulations. At no time prior to the
8 satisfaction of all liabilities with respect to members and their
9 beneficiaries shall any part of the corpus and income be used for,
10 or diverted to, purposes other than the exclusive benefit of the
11 members and their beneficiaries.

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 I. On or before October 31, 2022, the Office, with the advice
2 of the Council, shall develop a set of broadband grants or incentive
3 awards guidelines to be approved by the Board. The grants and
4 incentive awards shall be for unserved and underserved areas. The
5 Office shall submit a copy of the guidelines to the Office of the
6 Speaker of the Oklahoma House of Representatives, the Office of the
7 President Pro Tempore of the Oklahoma State Senate, and to the
8 offices of the chairs of the appropriate legislative committees.
9 The guidelines shall:
10 1. Consider a weighted approach for awards based upon the
11 following:
12 a. the area’s need for services, including, but not
13 limited to, whether the area is underserved or
14 unserved,
15 b. whether there are existing broadband assets in the
16 area, based on the statewide map,
17 c. whether existing federal, state, local, tribal, or
18 private resources have been allocated to broadband
19 services in the area,
20 d. a preference for federal, state, local, tribal, or
21 private partnerships, and
22 e. the capacity of the provider to maintain assets for an
23 extended period of time;
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1121

1 2. Recommend any necessary controls including, but not limited

5 incentive award and to protect against waste, fraud, or abuse; and
6 3. Include annual reporting requirements of award recipients to

10 SECTION 371. AMENDATORY Section 3, Chapter 231, O.S.L.

12 follows:
13 Section 12003. A. 1. The Treasurer shall prepare and maintain
14 and provide to each state governmental entity a list of financial
15 companies that boycott energy companies. In maintaining the list,
16 the Treasurer may:
17 a. review and rely, as appropriate in the Treasurer’s
18 judgment, on publicly available information regarding
19 financial companies including information provided by
20 the state, nonprofit organizations, research firms,
21 international organizations, and governmental
22 entities, and
23 b. request written verification from a financial company
24 that it does not boycott energy companies and rely, as
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1122
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 appropriate in the Treasurer’s judgment and without
2 conducting further investigation, research, or
3 inquiry, on a financial company’s written response to
4 the request.
5 2. A financial company that fails to provide to the Treasurer a
6 written verification under subparagraph b of paragraph 1 of this
7 subsection before the sixty-first day after receiving the request
8 from the Treasurer is presumed to be boycotting energy companies.
9 3. The Treasurer shall update the list annually or more often
10 as the Treasurer considers necessary, but not more often than
11 quarterly, based on information from, among other sources, those
12 listed in subparagraph a of paragraph 1 of this subsection.
13 4. Not later than the thirtieth day after the date the list of
14 financial companies that boycott energy companies is first provided
15 or updated, the Treasurer shall, utilizing the centralized filing
16 system provided for in Section 378 of this act, electronically file
17 the list with the presiding officer of each house of the Legislature
18 and the Attorney General and post the list on a publicly available
19 Internet website.
20 5. The Treasurer may retain third-party consultants to assist
21 in the implementation of the provisions of this act.
22 B. Not later than the thirtieth day after the date a state
23 governmental entity receives the list provided under paragraph 1 of
24 subsection A of this section, the state governmental entity shall
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1123
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 noti fy the Treasurer of the listed financial companies in which the
2 state governmental entity owns direct holdings or indirect holdings.
3 C. 1. For each listed financial company identified under
4 paragraph 1 of subsection A of this section, the state governmental
5 entity shall send a written notice:
6 a. informing the financial company of its status as a
7 listed financial company,
8 b. warning the financial company that it may become
9 subject to divestment by state governmental entities
10 after the expiration of the period described by
11 paragraph 2 of this subsection, and
12 c. offering the financial company the opportunity to
13 clarify its activities related to companies described
14 by paragraph 1 of subsection A of this section.
15 2. Not later than the ninetieth day after the date the
16 financial company receives notice under paragraph 1 of this
17 subsection, the financial company shall cease boycotting energy
18 companies to avoid qualifying for divestment by state governmental
19 entities.
20 3. If, during the time provided by paragraph 2 of this
21 subsection, the financial company ceases boycotting energy
22 companies, the Treasurer shall remove the financial company from the
23 list maintained under paragraph 1 of subsection A of this section,
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1124
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 and this subsection will no longer apply to the financial company
2 unless it resumes boycotting energy companies.
3 4. If, after the time provided by paragraph 2 of this
4 subsection expires, the financial company continues to boycott
5 energy companies, the state governmental entity shall sell, redeem,
6 divest, or withdraw all publicly traded securities of the financial
7 company, except securities described by subsection E of this
8 section, according to the schedule provided under subsection D of
9 this section.
10 D. 1. A state governmental entity required to sell, redeem,
11 divest, or withdraw all publicly traded securities of a listed
12 financial company shall comply with the following schedule:
13 a. at least fifty percent (50%) of those assets shall be
14 removed from the state governmental entity’s assets
15 under management not later than the one-hundred-
16 eightieth day after the date the financial company
17 receives notice pursuant to paragraph 1 of subsection
18 C of this section unless the state governmental entity
19 determines, based on a good-faith exercise of its
20 fiduciary discretion and subject to subparagraph b of
21 this subsection, that a later date is more prudent,
22 and
23 b. one hundred percent (100%) of those assets shall be
24 removed from the state governmental entity’s assets
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1125
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 under management not later than the three-hundred-
2 sixtieth day after the date the financial company
3 receives notice pursuant to paragraph 1 of subsection
4 C of this section.
5 2. If a financial company that ceased boycotting energy
6 companies after receiving notice pursuant to paragraph 1 of
7 subsection C of this section resumes its boycott, the state
8 governmental entity shall send a written notice to the financial
9 company informing it that the state governmental entity will sell,
10 redeem, divest, or withdraw all publicly traded securities of the
11 financial company according to the schedule in paragraph 1 of
12 subsection D of this section.
13 3. Except as provided by paragraph 1 of subsection D of this
14 section, a state governmental entity may delay the schedule for
15 divestment under that subsection only to the extent that the state
16 governmental entity determines, in the state governmental entity’s
17 good-faith judgment, and consistent with the entity’s fiduciary
18 duty, that divestment from listed financial companies will likely
19 result in a loss in value or a benchmark deviation described by
20 paragraph 1 of subsection F of this section.
21 4. If a state governmental entity delays the schedule for
22 divestment, the state governmental entity shall, utilizing the
23 centralized filing system provided for in Section 378 of this act,
24 electronically submit a report to the Treasurer, the presiding
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1126
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 offi cer of each house of the Legislature, and the Attorney General
2 stating the reasons and justification for the delay in divestment by
3 the state governmental entity from listed financial companies. The
4 report shall include documentation supporting its determination that
5 the divestment would result in a loss in value or a benchmark
6 deviation described by paragraph 1 of subsection F of this section
7 including objective numerical estimates. The state governmental
8 entity shall update the report every six (6) months.
9 E. A state governmental entity is not required to divest from
10 any indirect holdings in actively or passively managed investment
11 funds or private equity funds. The state governmental entity shall
12 submit letters to the managers of each investment fund containing
13 listed financial companies requesting that they remove those
14 financial companies from the fund or create a similar actively or
15 passively managed fund with indirect holdings devoid of listed
16 financial companies. If a manager creates a similar fund with
17 substantially the same management fees and same level of investment
18 risk and anticipated return, the state governmental entity may
19 replace all applicable investments with investments in the similar
20 fund in a time frame consistent with prudent fiduciary standards but
21 not later than the four-hundred-fiftieth day after the date the fund
22 is created.
23
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1127
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 F. 1. A state governmental entity may cease divesting from one
2 or more listed financial companies only if clear and convincing
3 evidence shows that:
4 a. the state governmental entity has suffered or will
5 suffer a loss in the value of assets under management
6 by the state governmental entity as a result of having
7 to divest from listed financial companies under this
8 subsection, or
9 b. an individual portfolio that uses a benchmark-aware
10 strategy would be subject to an aggregate expected
11 deviation from its benchmark as a result of having to
12 divest from listed financial companies under this
13 subsection.
14 2. A state governmental entity may cease divesting from a
15 listed financial company as provided by this section only to the
16 extent necessary to ensure that the state governmental entity does
17 not suffer a loss in value or deviate from its benchmark as
18 described by paragraph 1 of this subsection.
19 3. Before a state governmental entity may cease divesting from
20 a listed financial company under this section, the state
21 governmental entity shall, utilizing the centralized filing system

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1128
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 reas on and justification, supported by clear and convincing
2 evidence, for deciding to cease divestment or to remain invested in
3 a listed financial company. The state governmental entity shall
4 update the report required by this subsection semiannually, as
5 applicable.
6 4. This section does not apply to reinvestment in a financial
7 company that is no longer a listed financial company.
8 G. Except as provided in subsection F of this section, a state
9 governmental entity shall not acquire securities of a listed
10 financial company.

(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. The name and address of the agency;

14 SECTION 377. AMENDATORY 75 O.S. 2021, Section 308, as
15 last amended by Section 2, Chapter 420, O.S.L. 2025 (75 O.S. Supp.
16 2025, Section 308), is amended to read as follows:
17 Section 308. A. Upon receipt of any proposed permanent rules,
18 the Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President Pro
19 Tempore of the Senate shall assign such rules to the appropriate
20 committees of each house of the Legislature for review. Except as
21 otherwise provided by this section:
22 1. If such rules are received on or before April 1, the
23 Legislature shall have until the last day of the regular legislative
24 session of that year to review such rules. Provided, proposed
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1148
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 perm anent rules for consideration by the 2nd Session of the 59th
2 Legislature shall be submitted on or before March 1. For each
3 legislative session thereafter, proposed permanent rules shall be
4 submitted on or before February 1 of the given year; and
5 2. If such rules are received after the date established
6 pursuant to paragraph 1 of this subsection, the Legislature shall
7 have until the last day of the regular legislative session of the
8 next year to act on such rules.
9 B. By the adoption of joint resolutions during the review
10 period specified in subsection A of this section, the Legislature
11 may disapprove or approve any rule and disapprove all or part of a
12 rule or rules. Any rules not acted upon by the adoption of a joint
13 resolution shall be deemed disapproved.
14 C. Unless otherwise authorized by the Legislature, whenever a
15 rule is disapproved as provided in subsection B of this section, the
16 agency adopting such rules shall not have authority to resubmit an
17 identical rule, except during the first sixty (60) calendar days of
18 the next regular legislative session. Any effective emergency rule
19 which would have been superseded by a disapproved permanent rule
20 shall be deemed null and void on the date the Legislature
21 disapproves the permanent rule. Rules may be disapproved in part or
22 in whole by the Legislature. Upon enactment of any joint resolution
23 disapproving a rule, the agency shall file notice of such
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1149
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 legi slative disapproval with the Secretary for publication in “The
2 Oklahoma Register”.
3 D. Unless otherwise provided by specific vote of the
4 Legislature, joint resolutions introduced for purposes of
5 disapproving or approving a rule shall not be subject to regular
6 legislative cutoff dates, shall be limited to such provisions as may
7 be necessary for disapproval or approval of a rule, and any such
8 other direction or mandate regarding the rule deemed necessary by
9 the Legislature. The resolution shall contain no other provisions.
10 E. A proposed permanent rule shall only be deemed finally
11 adopted if:
12 1. Approved by a joint resolution pursuant to subsection B of
13 this section, provided that any such resolution becomes law in
14 accordance with Section 11 of Article VI of the Oklahoma
15 Constitution; or
16 2. Disapproved by a joint resolution pursuant to subsection B
17 of this section which has been vetoed by the Governor in accordance
18 with Section 11 of Article VI of the Oklahoma Constitution and the
19 veto has not been overridden.
20 F. Prior to final adoption of a rule, an agency may withdraw a
21 rule from legislative review. Notice of such withdrawal shall be
22 electronically given to the Governor, the Speaker of the House of
23 Representatives, the President Pro Tempore of the Senate, and to the
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1150
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 Secr etary, utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in
2 Section 378 of this act, for publication in “The Oklahoma Register”.
3 G. An agency may promulgate an emergency rule only pursuant to
4 Section 253 of this title.
5 H. Any rights, privileges, or interests gained by any person by
6 operation of an emergency rule, shall not be affected by reason of
7 any subsequent disapproval or rejection of such rule by either house
8 of the Legislature.
9 SECTION 378. NEW LAW A new section of law to be codified
10 in the Oklahoma Statutes as Section 600 of Title 75, unless there is
11 created a duplication in numbering, reads as follows:
12 A. The Office of the Secretary of State shall create and
13 maintain a centralized filing system for all reports required by
14 statute to be submitted to such system. Such filing system shall
15 provide:
16 1. An index of all statutorily required reports, sortable by
17 agency name, policy area, title of law, and other information;
18 2. The dates of the last submitted report by an agency or
19 entity and the due date of the next report;
20 3. Notification to the statutorily designated report recipients
21 when a report is submitted;
22 4. Confirmation of successful submission to the agency or
23 entity submitting a report using such system; and
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1151
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 5. A dashboard reflecting whether a report has been submitted
2 or is overdue.
3 B. The Secretary of State shall, when newly enacted legislation
4 requires an agency or entity of this state to submit a report,
5 electronically notify such agency or entity of such requirement
6 within five (5) business days of the date when such enacted
7 legislation is filed in the Office of the Secretary of State, as
8 provided for in Section 12a of Title 75 of the Oklahoma Statutes.
9 C. The Secretary of State shall, utilizing the centralized
10 filing system provided for in this section, electronically report to
11 the President Pro Tempore of the Senate, the Speaker of the House of
12 Representatives, and the Governor if an agency or entity has not
13 submitted a statutorily required report to the centralized filing
14 system by the date of submission provided in statute.
15 SECTION 379. AMENDATORY 76 O.S. 2021, Section 17, is
16 amended to read as follows:
17 Section 17. Whenever a claim of personal injury is made against
18 any practitioner of the healing arts or a licensed hospital, a
19 report shall be made to the appropriate licensing board or agency by
20 the liability insurer of such practitioner or hospital within sixty
21 (60) days after receipt of information that a claim is being made.
22 In the event that such claim is made against a party not insured,
23 the report shall be made by the party. The report shall be in
24 writing on a form containing the following information:
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1152
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 1. The name and address of the practitioner or hospital;
2 2. The name, age, and address of the claimant;
3 3. A brief statement of the nature of the injury, illness or
4 condition complained of and the act or omission complained of; and
5 4. Whether a suit is pending and, if so, the court, style and
6 docket number of the action.
7 And whenever such claim or suit is concluded, the disposition
8 shall be reported to the appropriate board or agency promptly.
9 This report shall be privileged except as hereinafter provided.
10 The licensing board or agency shall take any remedial,
11 disciplinary, or corrective action as it may deem warranted by the
12 facts contained in the report.
13 Any person or liability insurer failing to furnish a report on a
14 claim as required in this section shall be guilty of a misdemeanor.
15 Further, the board or agency shall annually, utilizing the
16 centralized filing system provided for in Section 378 of this act,
17 electronically furnish the President Pro Tempore of the Senate and
18 the Speaker of the House of Representatives a full report of all
19 such claims except that names and addresses of all parties shall be
20 omitted. Such report shall include disposition of the claim as well
21 as a report of all action taken by the board or agency and the
22 reason therefor.

3 and a neurosurgeon, and one of whom shall be licensed in this state
4 as a podiatric physician; and
5 3. The Speaker of the House of Representatives shall appoint
6 three members, one of whom shall be licensed in this state as an
7 osteopathic physician, one of whom shall be licensed in this state
8 either as a doctor of medicine or a doctor of osteopathy and shall
9 be engaged in the practice of occupational medicine, and one of whom
10 shall be licensed in this state as a chiropractic physician.
11 Any member serving on the effective date of this section shall
12 serve the remainder of his or her term. Thereafter, each position
13 will be filled by the appointing official for a term of three (3)
14 years. Members shall be subject to reappointment, with any new
15 appointee to serve out the remainder of the unexpired term of the
16 Committee member so replaced.
17 B. The Committee shall:
18 1. Assist and advise the Workers’ Compensation Commission
19 regarding utilization review as it relates to the medical practice
20 and treatment of work-related injuries. Such utilization review
21 shall include a review of reasonable and necessary medical
22 treatment; abusive practices; needless treatments, testing, or
23 procedures; or a pattern of billing in excess of or in violation of
24 the Schedule of Medical Fees. The Physician Advisory Committee
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1169
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 shal l review and make findings and recommendations to the Commission
2 with respect to charges of inappropriate or unnecessary treatment or
3 procedures, abusive practices, or excessive billing disclosed
4 through utilization review;
5 2. Assist the Commission in reviewing medical practices of
6 health care providers, including evaluations of permanent disability
7 provided by health care providers. The Committee shall review and
8 make findings and recommendations to the Commission with respect to
9 charges of abusive practices by health care providers providing
10 medical services or evaluations of permanent partial disability
11 through the workers’ compensation system;
12 3. After public hearing, review and make recommendations for
13 acceptable deviations from the American Medical Association’s
14 “Guides to the Evaluation of Permanent Impairment”;
15 4. After public hearing, adopt Physician Advisory Committee
16 Guidelines (PACG) and protocols for only medical treatment not
17 addressed by the latest edition of the Official Disability
18 Guidelines;
19 5. After public hearing, adopt Physician Advisory Committee
20 Guidelines for the prescription and dispensing of any controlled
21 substance included in Schedule II of the Uniform Controlled
22 Dangerous Substances Act if not addressed by the current edition of
23 the Official Disability Guidelines;
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1170
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 6. Review utilization on cases or of providers when requested
2 by any employer, injured employee or insurer. The Committee may
3 issue a public or private censure to any provider for utilization
4 which is excessive or inadequate, or recommend the Commission order
5 treatment within the treatment guidelines;
6 7. Provide general recommendations to the Commission on the
7 issues of injury causation and apportionment;
8 8. Conduct educational seminars for the Commission, employers,
9 employees, and other interested parties;
10 9. Assist the Commission in accessing medical information from
11 scientific literature; and
12 10. Report Electronically report its progress annually to the
13 Governor, the President Pro Tempore of the Senate, and the Speaker
14 of the House of Representatives utilizing the centralized filing
15 system provided for in Section 378 of this act.
16 C. The Commission shall recognize the latest edition of the
17 Official Disability Guidelines as the primary standard of reference,
18 at the time of treatment, in determining the frequency and extent of
19 services presumed to be medically necessary and appropriate for
20 compensable injuries under this act, or in resolving such matters in
21 the event a dispute arises.
22 D. Members of the Physician Advisory Committee shall receive no
23 compensation for serving on the Committee but shall be reimbursed by
24 the Commission for their necessary travel expenses incurred in the
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1171
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 perf ormance of their duties in accordance with the State Travel
2 Reimbursement Act.
3 E. Meetings of the Physician Advisory Committee shall be called
4 by the Commission but held at least quarterly. The presence of a
5 majority of the members shall constitute a quorum. No action shall
6 be taken by the Physician Advisory Committee without the affirmative
7 vote of at least a majority of the members.
8 F. The Commission shall provide office supplies and personnel
9 of the Commission to assist the Committee in the performance of its
10 duties.
11 G. Upon written request, the Insurance Commissioner, CompSource
12 Mutual Insurance Company, and every approved self-insured employer
13 in Oklahoma shall provide the Committee with data necessary to the

15 H. Any health care provider acting in good faith and within the

17 Committee shall be immune from civil liability for making any report
18 or other information available to the judges of the Commission or to
19 the Commission or for assisting in the origination, investigation,
20 or preparation of the report or other information so provided.

14 provided by the employer.

4 H. Fee Schedule.
5 1. The Commission shall conduct a review and update of the

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1175
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 medi cal care is assured and maintained for injured employees. The

17 customary and reasonable medical payments to workers’ compensation

23 trade area for comparable treatment of a person with similar
24 injuries.

SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1179
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 for each ingredient plus a dispensing fee of Five Dollars ($5.00)

7 11. Payment for medical care as required by the Administrative

4 I. Formulary. The Commission by rule shall adopt a closed
5 formulary. Rules adopted by the Commission shall allow an appeals
6 process for claims in which a treating doctor determines and
7 documents that a drug not included in the formulary is necessary to

9 rule shall require the use of generic pharmaceutical medications and

5 cost for annual maintenance attributed to case management,
6 docketing, and recordkeeping on behalf of the CEC.
7 E. The non-CEC case management system, docketing, appellate
8 records, and other records maintained in the normal course of
9 business of the Court of Civil Appeals shall remain with the
10 Oklahoma Court Information System (OCIS) of the Supreme Court. The
11 Director may contract and expend funds as needed to modify the OCIS
12 to support the CEC trial judge and the CEC Division of the Court of
13 Civil Appeals.
14 F. The CEC shall operate by the rules adopted by the Workers’
15 Compensation Court prior to February 1, 2014. The CEC Division of
16 the Court of Civil Appeals shall adopt rules of practice and
17 procedure for appeals to the CEC Division.
18 G. The CEC shall continue as a court of record, with respect to
19 any matter within the limits of its jurisdiction. Any party to a
20 hearing on a claim for compensation before the CEC trial judge or
21 the CEC Division shall be entitled to the services of a court
22 reporter. The cost of transcripts shall be borne by the requesting
23 party unless otherwise determined by order of the CEC trial judge.
24
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1191
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 H. The Director shall contract with the Commission for office
2 and courtroom space for the CEC trial judge. The CEC trial judge
3 may hold hearings and other proceedings in facilities provided by
4 the Director or the Commission.
5 I. 1. The CEC trial judge shall be vested with jurisdiction
6 over all claims filed pursuant to the Workers’ Compensation Code or
7 previous statute in effect on the date of an injury that occurred
8 before February 1, 2014. All claims so filed shall be heard by the
9 judge sitting without a jury. The CEC trial judge shall have full
10 power and authority to determine all questions in relation to
11 payment of claims for compensation under the provisions of the
12 Workers’ Compensation Code or previous statute in effect on the date
13 of an injury that occurred before February 1, 2014.
14 2. The Commission shall coordinate with the Director to
15 schedule a docket for hearings on claims for compensation and other
16 proceedings requested by either party, including all cases pending
17 on the effective date of this act. Hearings on claims for
18 compensation and other proceedings may be conducted in person or
19 remotely at the discretion of the CEC trial judge. The CEC trial
20 judge may conduct other proceedings on preliminary or supplemental
21 matters as needed. If any claim or proceeding is assigned to the
22 Commission by the CEC trial judge for a preliminary review, the
23 chair of the Commission may assign the matter to any Commission
24 administrative law judge (ALJ), or other hearing officer. Any party
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1192
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 may present evidence and be represented by counsel before the CEC
2 trial judge, the ALJ, or other hearing officer.
3 3. Upon receipt of the recommendation of the ALJ or other
4 hearing officer, any party may request a de novo rehearing with the
5 CEC trial judge or may waive the rehearing. If both parties waive
6 the rehearing, any recommendation by the ALJ or other hearing
7 officer shall not become a final determination until approved by
8 order of the CEC trial judge. Only the final order based upon the
9 decision of the CEC trial judge shall be appealable.
10 4. The decision of the CEC trial judge shall be final as to all
11 questions of fact and law; provided, the decision of the CEC trial
12 judge may be appealed to the CEC Division of the Court of Civil
13 Appeals within ten (10) days or the Supreme Court as provided by the
14 Workers’ Compensation Code or previous statute in effect on the date
15 of an injury that occurred before February 1, 2014. The Commission
16 shall coordinate with the Director to schedule a docket for hearings
17 on appeals to the CEC Division of the Court of Civil Appeals,
18 including all cases pending on the effective date of this act. The
19 parties may appeal a decision of the CEC Division to the Supreme
20 Court as provided by the Supreme Court rules in the same manner as
21 from the three-judge en banc panel. The decision of the CEC trial
22 judge shall be issued within thirty (30) days following the
23 submission of the case by the parties. The power and jurisdiction
24 of the CEC trial judge over each case shall be continuing and he or
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1193
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 she may, from time to time, make such modifications or changes with
2 respect to former findings or orders relating thereto if, in his or
3 her opinion, it may be justified.
4 J. For an injury occurring before February 1, 2014, all
5 benefits and procedures to obtain benefits shall be determined by
6 the workers’ compensation law of this state in effect on the date of
7 the injury.
8 K. All accrued rights and penalties incurred pursuant to a
9 final order of the CEC trial judge shall be preserved. No accrued
10 right, penalty incurred, or proceeding begun by virtue of a statute
11 repealed by this act shall be abrogated by the terms of this act.
12 L. 1. On or before July 1 of each year, the Administrator of
13 the Commission shall prepare and electronically submit a report for
14 the prior calendar year to the Governor, the Chief Justice of the
15 Supreme Court, the President Pro Tempore of the Senate, and the
16 Speaker of the House of Representatives, utilizing the centralized
17 filing system provided for in Section 378 of this act, which shall
18 include a statement of the number of awards made and the causes of
19 the accidents leading to the injuries for which the awards were
20 made, total work load data of the CEC trial judge, a detailed
21 statement of the expenses of the Commission for all administrative
22 duties performed on behalf of the CEC, together with any other
23 matter which the Commission deems proper to report to the Governor
24 including any recommendations he or she may desire to make.
SENATE FLOOR VERSION - SB1877 SFLR Page 1194
(Bold face denotes Committee Amendments)

1 2. On or before July 1 of each year, the Director shall
2 electronically submit to the officials listed in paragraph 1 of this
3 subsection, utilizing the centralized filing system provided for in
4 Section 378 of this act, a report for the prior calendar year which
5 shall include the total workload of the CEC Division of the Court of
6 Civil Appeals and a detailed statement of expenses incurred because
7 of three-judge en banc panel appeals.
8 M. Subject to the availability of funds, the Director may enter
9 into a contract to employ one part-time attorney to assist parties
10 without representation with duties and assignments to be determined
11 by the CEC trial judge and Director.
